All these evidences lead us to ask if WA outflows are the `missing link' between the UFOs and the molecular outflows. AGN are believed to be one of the main drivers behind the kpc scale molecular outflows 2013ApJ...776...27V,2014A&A...562A..21C,2017A&A...601A.143F,2018ApJ...868...10L . These large scale molecular outflows carry large amount of kinetic luminosity and mass outflow rate and are capable of removing materials from the host galaxy and also regulate the star formation rate. However, the exact mechanism by which the AGN interacts with the molecular gas in the host galaxy and drives the outflows is not known. X-ray outflows are a very important candidate for such an interaction, either by energy or momentum conserving processes see for e.g., 2014A&A...562A..21C,2015Natur.519..436T,2017ApJ...843...18V,2020A&ARv..28....2V . IRAS F11119+3257 is one example where the molecular outflow (traced by neutral NaI, OH absorption and CO emission lines) have been found to be driven by an inner UFO in an energy conserving way 2015Natur.519..436T,2017ApJ...843...18V . Similarly, the BAL quasar Mrk 231 where both UFO and molecular outflows have been detected as traced by CO(2-1) and CO(3-2) emission lines 2015A&A...583A..99F , it is found that the UFO is driving the molecular outflow (MO) in an energy conserving way. On the other hand for the source PDS 456 an energy conserving mechanism of the UFO creating the MO may be ruled out 2019A&A...628A.118B .

sources.summary: The paper presents PDS 456 as an extreme nearby luminous QSO whose UV and X-ray spectra indicate broad blueshifted emission, Ly absorption, and a decelerating cooling outflow.

quotes.quote: Here we present the UV spectrum of PDS 456 as observed by the Hubble Space Telescope. We find the UV spectrum is also extreme, displaying very broad emission-lines, with C iv 1549 blueshifted by 5000 km s ^ 1 and a broad Ly absorption trough blueshifted by 14000--24000 km s ^ 1 . No strong, broad high-ionization absorption features are seen. We interpret the combined UV and X-ray spectrum of PDS 456 as the signature of a decelerating, cooling outflow, which may be driven by radiation and/or magnetic field.

sources.summary: The paper describes PDS 456 as a radio-quiet 3C 273 analog with broad lines, comparable absolute magnitude, substantial day-scale optical variability, and factor-of-two X-ray changes in eight hours.

quotes.quote: PDS 456 ( z = 0.184 , M_V = -26.9 ) is a radio-quiet analog of 3C 273 Torres97, Simpson99 . It has broad lines like 3C 273.

Although we did not often obtain daily sampling, for PDS 456 we did find several 10 variations in the V band over 24 hour periods at the 99 confidence level over three observing seasons even without attempting to subtract out any host galaxy component. PDS 456 is also an extreme X-ray variable with factor of two changes in 8 hours Reeves02 .

sources.summary: The paper uses PDS 456 as an AGN example with variable ultra-fast outflow velocities, an additional simultaneous absorption layer, and Fe K emission interpreted as a wind-scattered P-Cygni profile.

quotes.quote: For example, the UFO in PDS 456 appears at slightly different velocities (from 0.23 -0.33 ) in almost every observation taken of the source Gofford14 , most likely due to a flux-dependent velocity shift Matzeu17 . However, an additional layer of ultra-fast absorption is present in PDS 456 Reeves18_pds456 simultaneously, which should be included separately.
For example, Nardini15 interpret the emission line in PDS 456 as a P-Cygni profile, where the Fe K emission is produced by scattering off the outflowing wind.

gmw_00000750.name: galaxy D in MG2016+112
aliases_external: MG2016+112 lens galaxy D / WISEA J201918.06+112713.1
gmw_00000750.overview: Galaxy D in MG2016+112 is a massive, old early-type galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0202342] classified as a giant elliptical [arXiv:1511.01097]. Located at a redshift of z=1.004, it acts as a gravitational lens [arXiv:astro-ph/0202342] and is embedded in a probable galaxy cluster [arXiv:astro-ph/0202342, arXiv:1511.01097].

sources.summary: The paper models the z=1.004 E/S0 lens galaxy D, finding a massive old system with substantial dark matter, an approximately isothermal total mass profile, and constrained halo slope.

quotes.quote: In a previous paper (Koopmans Treu 2002; hereafter KT02) we described the Keck and HST observations of the E/S0 lens galaxy D in MG2016+112, the most distant spectroscopically confirmed lens galaxy known ( z=1.004 ). A review of the current observations of this system can be found in Koopmans et al. \ (2002; hereafter K02), who also propose a detailed lens model, where the image separation is caused predominantly by galaxy D, and find a central velocity dispersion =320--340 \ for an isothermal total mass distribution. This value should correspond closely to the central stellar velocity dispersion (e. g. Kochanek 1994; Kochanek et al. \ 2000). Our direct measurement ( =328 32 ; KT02) shows that D is indeed a massive old E/S0 galaxy, possibly embedded in a proto-cluster, with a central stellar velocity dispersion in agreement with that inferred from the lens models.

In particular, for galaxy D in MG2016+112 ( z=1.004 ) we have demonstrated that: (i) Dark matter is unambiguously present and accounts for > 50 (99 CL) of the mass within the Einstein radius. A mass distribution following light with constant M/L can be ruled out at more than 8-- level. Considering an additional constraint on M_*/L_B from the evolution of the FP, the smallest allowable fraction of dark matter increases to 60 (99 CL). (ii) The total mass distribution (dark+luminous) inside the Einstein radius is well described by an isothermal sphere, i.e. _ tot ^ ' with '=2.0 0.1 .

sources.summary: The paper presents MG2016+112 as the sample's farthest giant elliptical lens, embedded in a probable galaxy cluster with nearby high-density structure and resolvable lensed images.

quotes.quote: MG2016+112 (hereafter 2016 ) is a giant elliptical galaxy 1984Sci...223...46L,1986AJ.....91..991S . It is the farthest lens we consider in this sample. It lies in a cluster that consists of 69 probable galaxies 2003MNRAS.344..337T . The cluster shows a high density of galaxies close to the lens in a south-east direction, at about 130-140^ . Two lensed images are very close in this fold lens, but could be resolved by 2009MNRAS.394..174M .

gmw_00000751.name: J2054-0005
gmw_00000751.overview: J2054-0005 is a high-redshift quasar host galaxy [arXiv:2203.03658, arXiv:2401.04211] with a supermassive black hole [arXiv:2401.04211] and a high star formation rate [arXiv:1101.1553]. The system contains substantial dust [arXiv:1101.1553] and exhibits a low line-to-infrared luminosity ratio [arXiv:2203.03658].

sources.summary: The paper presents J2054--0005 observations tuned to cover CO(7--6), uses its published SED fit, and reports it as the sample object with the lowest quoted line-to-infrared ratio.

quotes.quote: J2054--0005 was observed in October--November 2013 (program ID: X04D). We used the WideX correlator and tuned the receiver to secure the and CO(7--6) lines in a single setup. The sources 3C454.3 and MWC349 were observed for bandpass and flux calibration. For the amplitude and phase calibration, we observed 2059+034. The final cube includes 16881 visibilities, corresponding to 21.10 h (5-antenna equivalent).

Finally, the /IR luminosity ratios in our sample span a factor 6 , from 1.4 10^ 3 (J2219+0102) to 2.4 10^ 4 (J2054--0005), with lower /IR ratios typically observed for quasars with L_ IR >3 10^ 12 see, e.g., stacey10,gullberg15 .

sources.summary: The paper says J2054-0005 is better matched by a tuned 70 Myr model, yielding high star formation, high metallicity, substantial dust, and a stellar mass of 4.7e10 solar masses.

quotes.quote: In view of this we find that this scenario at an epoch of 100 Myr is more appropriate for the QSOs J1048+4637 fan03 at z = 6.23 and J2054-0005 jiang08 at z = 6.06. For the latter QSO a fine tuning of the epoch to 70 Myr results in a better match. At this epoch we obtain a SFR of 1150 yr ^ 1 and a metallicity of 4.4 . The amount of dust is M_ d 2.7 10^ 8 (for M_ cl = 100 ), while the stellar mass is M_ 4.7 10^ 10 . The lower derived L'_ CO(1- 0) leads to _ g,H_ 2 3--4 in case = 0.8--1 (K km s ^ 1 pc ^ 2 ) ^ 1 is applied, while for _ g,H_ 2 2 a value for of 1.6 would be required.

sources.summary: The paper describes J2054 0005 as a faint Stripe 82 quasar host observed in [C II], with resolved line and continuum sizes and fitted far-infrared luminosity.

quotes.quote: J2054 0005 . The quasar was selected from SDSS stripe 82 with m_ 1450 =20.60 , i.e. about one magnitude fainter than the objects discovered from the SDSS main survey jiang08 . We observed the [ C II ] line emission at 0.64'' 0.58'' resolution. The deconvolved FWHM source size is (0.35'' 0.04'') (0.32'' 0.05'') and PA=91^ 173^ . The 262 GHz continuum flux density measured in the line-free windows is 2.98 0.05 mJy , and the deconvolved continuum source size is (0.27'' 0.03'') (0.26'' 0.03'') with PA=168^ 68^ .

sources.summary: The paper describes J2054-0005 as a fainter z=6.39 QSO with updated MgII black-hole mass and an ALMA [CII] rotation-curve dynamical-mass limit.

quotes.quote: SDSS J205406.49-000514.8 (hereafter J2054-0005) at z=6.39 was selected from SDSS stripe 82 with m_ 1450 = 20.60, i.e. about one magnitude fainter than the objects discovered from the SDSS main survey jiang2008 . Leipski2014 reported observations in band z, y, J, H, K and with Herschel , however they were not able to fully study its SED due to lack of observations in the mm/sub-mm regime. wang2013 reported a BH mass of M_ BH = 8.6 10^8 \ M_ , later updated to M_ BH = 1.48 10^9 \ M_ using the MgII emission line neeleman2021 . neeleman2021 studied the rotation curve of J2054-0005 using an high-resolution ALMA observation of the [ CII ] emission of this object, and they determined a lower limit for the dynamical mass of M_ dyn > 2.9 10^ 10 \ M_ .

sources.summary: The appendix says this quasar needed unweighted continuum fitting, low signal-to-noise limited one line to a single Gaussian, and another line contribution was set to zero.

quotes.quote: SDSS J2054--0005 To properly fit the continuum over the entire range, it was necessary to disable the fit weights for the continuum model. The low signal-to-noise ratio of this spectrum did not justify to use more than one Gaussian component to model the line. For the same reason we set the contribution of the line to the complex to zero.

gmw_00000752.name: The Dragonfly Galaxy
gmw_00000752.overview: The Dragonfly Galaxy is a high-redshift, hyper-luminous infrared radio galaxy at z = 1.92 [arXiv:1510.02381, arXiv:2312.09649]. It is a precoalescent major merger of three gas-rich components undergoing intense starburst activity [arXiv:1510.02381, arXiv:2312.09649] and hosting a compact steep-spectrum active galactic nucleus with radio jets [arXiv:2312.09649, arXiv:2305.03979].

sources.summary: ALMA observations show the Dragonfly Galaxy is a hyper-luminous high-redshift radio galaxy undergoing a gas-rich triple merger that drives starburst and radio-AGN activity.

quotes.quote: We here present high (0.3 ^ ) resolution ALMA Cycle 2 observations of CO(6-5) emission and dust continuum in the Dragonfly Galaxy (MRC 0152-209) at z = 1.92. This is one of the most IR luminous HzRGs ( L_ IR (SB) 2 10^ 13 L_ ), well in the regime of HYLIRGs, with a star formation rate determined with Spitzer and Herschel (3 - 500 m) of 3000 M _ yr ^ 1 dro14 . Just how unique this source is compared to other HzRGs is visualised in Fig. fig:IR , where we show that the Dragonfly Galaxy lies almost one dex above the typical starburst IR luminosity of HzRGs at similar redshifts. After initially presenting a detection of ^ 12 CO(1-0) in this system hereafter EM11 emo11 , we recently used the Australia Telescope Compact Array (ATCA) to image this CO(1-0), part of which appears to be widespread tidal debris from a merger hereafter EM15 emo15 . Our current ALMA data provide a unique view on this merger, and reveal that significant amounts of molecular gas are being redistributed within the merging system.

Precoalescent merger Our ALMA data show that the high-redshift Dragonfly Galaxy is a precoalescent galaxy merger. This merger involves at least two gas- and dust-rich galaxies, and likely a third, smaller gas-rich companion. This is consistent with the claim by ivi12 that galaxy mergers are ubiquitous among starbursting HzRGs, but the detailed analysis from the combined ALMA and HST data allowed us to confirm the merger origin, and study the role of the cold gas, with much more confidence than heretofore possible in these systems.

sources.summary: The paper studies MRC 0152-209, the Dragonfly galaxy, as a z=1.92 high-redshift radio galaxy, HyLIRG, and major merger with starburst activity, powerful jets, rapid black-hole growth, and molecular outflow.

quotes.quote: In this work, we studied CO(6-5) line emission and continuum emission in a high-redshift radio galaxy, MRC 0152-209, at z=1.92 using ALMA up to a 0 024 resolution (corresponding to 200 pc). This system is a starburst major merger constituted of two galaxies: the northwest (NW) one hosting the RLAGN with jet kinetic power L jet 2 10^ 46 \ and the southeast (SE) one. Based on the SED fitting for the entire system (NW+SE galaxies), we found AGN bolometric luminosity L AGN,bol 0.9-3 10^ 46 \ for the RLAGN. We estimated black hole mass through M_ BH M_ scaling relations and found an Eddington ratio of Edd 0.7-4 conservatively. These results suggest that the RLAGN is radiatively efficient and the powerful jets could be launched from a super-Eddington accretion disc.

In this work, we study a HzRG -- MRC 0152-209 (( L 147 \ MHz =3.2( 1.0) 10^ 28 W Hz 1 )), named Dragonfly galaxy, which has starbursts ( SFR 3000 \ M_ \ yr^ 1 ) 2014AA...566A..53D . It is a HyLIRG ( L IR 2 10^ 13 \ L_ ) and a major merger constituted of three components: the North-West (NW) galaxy, the South-East (SE) galaxy, and a possible Companion galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes the NW galaxy as the RLAGN host in the Dragonfly merger, with compact disc-like CO emission, concentrated dust continuum, line splitting, and a blueshifted component interpreted as jet-driven outflow.

quotes.quote: It is a HyLIRG ( L IR 2 10^ 13 \ L_ ) and a major merger constituted of three components: the North-West (NW) galaxy, the South-East (SE) galaxy, and a possible Companion galaxy. Its double radio hotspots were identified by Very Large Array (VLA) at 4.7 and 8.2 GHz 2000AAS..145..121P .

The CO(6-5) line emission of the NW galaxy is still disc-like but is highly compact with a 2D Gaussian component of 0 091( 0 01) 0 083( 0 01) , corresponding to a radius of R NW 0.8 kpc, after deconvolved from the beam. This size is more compact than R NW 1 kpc based on Cycle 2 and 4 observations. In the dust continuum, the NW galaxy has its flux density concentrated within a radius of 0 07 \ ( 0.6 \ kpc ) , agreeing well with the molecular gas distribution, but shows diffuse emissions extended to the east.

NW galaxy shows a clear line splitting into two velocity components and a broadening of the main component. As we will discuss in sec: outflow origin , this blueshifted component (NW - 1) may represent the outflow driven by the expanding bubble attributed to the radio jet in a 3D shell-like geometry e.g., 2019A&A...632A..61G .

sources.summary: The paper reports CO(1-0) detection in the z=1.92 radio galaxy, deriving a large molecular gas mass and noting its IR-bright, compact steep-spectrum radio source.

quotes.quote: We report the detection of molecular CO(1-0) gas in the high- z radio galaxy MRC 0152-209 ( z = 1.92 ) with the Australia Telescope Compact Array Broadband Backend (ATCA/CABB). This is the third known detection of CO(1-0) in a high- z radio galaxy to date. CO(1-0) is the most robust tracer of the overall molecular gas content (including the wide-spread, low-density and subthermally excited component), hence observations of CO(1-0) are crucial for studying galaxy evolution in the Early Universe.

We derive L'_ CO = 6.6 2.0 10^ 10 K km s^ 1 pc^2 for MRC 0152-209, which is comparable to that derived from CO(1-0) observations of several high- z submillimeter and starforming BzK galaxies. The CO(1-0) traces a total molecular hydrogen mass of M _ H2 = 5 10^ 10 ( _ x /0.8) M _ . MRC 0152-209 is an infra-red bright radio galaxy, in which a large reservoir of cold molecular gas has not (yet) been depleted by star formation or radio source feedback. Its compact radio source is reliably detected at 40 GHz and has a steep spectral index of = -1.3 between 1.4 and 40 GHz ( 4-115 GHz in the galaxy's rest-frame).

sources.summary: The paper studies the Dragonfly galaxy, a z=1.92 radio and hyper-luminous infrared galaxy, resolving its hotspots and core and interpreting it as a compact steep-spectrum radio AGN with young jets.

quotes.quote: In this work, we have studied a HzRG -- MRC 0152-209, named Dragonfly galaxy, which has a star formation rate (SFR) of 3000 \ M_ \ yr^ 1 and M_ 5 10^ 11 \ M_ 2015A&A...584A..99E,2015MNRAS.451.1025E . This galaxy constitutes of three components: the North-West (NW) galaxy, the South-East (SE) galaxy, and a Companion (C) with massive molecular gas (see panels (a), (NW), and (SE) in Fig. fig:imaging ). The low-frequency observations at 4.7 and 8.2 GHz using the Very Large Array (VLA) were studied by 2000AAS..145..121P , and they identified two radio hotspots, the NW and SE ones, while the radio core remained non-detection (see green contours in panel (a) in Fig. fig:imaging ).

Based on these assumptions, the luminosity distance of the Dragonfly galaxy is 15200 Mpc, and 1 corresponds to a projected physical scale of 8.62 kpc. The calculation has made use of 2006PASP..118.1711W .
Since the projected linear separation of the two hotspots is only 1 5 ( 13 kpc at z=1.92 ), the Dragonfly galaxy can be classified as a CSS source 2021A&ARv..29....3O .

sources.summary: The paper describes MRC 0152-209 as a z=1.92 infrared-luminous high-redshift radio galaxy embedded in molecular gas, with a blended WISE host measured through deblended photometry.

quotes.quote: GLEAM J015455-204023 (MRC 0152-209) This z=1.92 radio galaxy (`the Dragonfly') is described by as ``the most infrared-luminous high-redshift radio galaxy known in the southern hemisphere'', and is embedded within a large reservoir of molecular gas Emonts2016 . The host galaxy of this source is not listed in the WISE catalogue, probably because it is blended with a nearby star. An object close to the radio position is visible in the WISE image, and one of us (THJ) has measured the following deblended magnitudes for the WISE galaxy: W1 = 16.259 0.036 mag; W2 = 15.177 0.014 mag; W3 = 11.973 0.096 mag; W4 = 8.828 0.081 mag.

gmw_00000753.name: J0318+684
gmw_00000753.overview: J0318+684 is a giant radio galaxy spanning approximately 1.5 megaparsecs [arXiv:2003.09183, arXiv:2103.00999] with a Fanaroff-Riley type II morphology [arXiv:1305.3622, arXiv:2006.09388]. It features a Giga-Hertz Peaked Spectrum core [arXiv:2003.09183, arXiv:2006.09388] and diffuse, off-axis emission extending hundreds of kiloparsecs [arXiv:2003.09183, arXiv:2103.00999].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0318.7+6828 as an FRII giant radio galaxy and narrow-line AGN with partially covered X-ray emission, modest absorption, weak Fe emission, and long-term variability.

quotes.quote: The source J0318.7+6828 is identified as an FRII GRG (see for a full description of the radio and optical properties). The optical spectrum (INT telescope in La Palma) is typical of a narrow line AGN, while the optical image shows a faint galaxy-like object associated to the radio core but the resolution is not enough to clarify the host morphology. We could not find any information on the IR or UV maps from literature, even if the IR emission, mostly in the K _ s band, is found within 30'' from the source position in the Two Micron All Sky Survey (2MASS) at IPAC.

A simple, absorbed power law can fit the source J0318.7+6828 with sufficient accuracy, even in the soft 0.5--2 keV energy band, but the best-fit was gained by modeling its emission with a partially covered power law. A Gaussian was then added to model the faint Fe fluorescence line, fixing its rest energy at 6.4 keV, and the best-fit model takes the form zpcfabs*zpowerlaw + zgauss (Fig. fig:J0318_fig ).

sources.summary: J0318+684 is reported as a 1.5 Mpc giant radio galaxy with an inverted/GPS-like core and detailed GMRT morphology showing diffuse off-axis emission from the core.

quotes.quote: Comments on individual sources GMRT images J0318+684: this source has been reported as GRG by , and is one of the most extended in our sample (1.5 Mpc). Those authors found an inverted-spectrum radio core, proposing this to be suggestive of a Giga-Hertz Peaked Spectrum (GPS) source resulting from a restarted nuclear activity (as later confirmed in 2019ApJ...875...88B ). Our GMRT map has an angular resolution of 15" 8", at a position angle of 70 , that is the most detailed image of this source to date. The morphology at 325 MHz shows a diffuse emission starting from the core and extending towards NW, perpendicularly to the axis of the main jets (detected in 1998A&A...336..455S as well). This region reaches a distance of 180" from the core, corresponding to 310 kpc at the redshift of this source ( z =0.09).

sources.summary: LOFAR and ancillary images show J0318+684 as a very extended GRG with a GPS core, active lobes, bright compact core, and diffuse off-axis emission extending hundreds of kiloparsecs.

quotes.quote: J0318+684 This is one of the most extended GRG of our sample (1.5 Mpc, 2016MNRAS.461.3165B ). In our previous works, we discussed the discovery of a young radio source (GigaHertz Peaked Spectrum source - GPS) in the core region, suggesting a recent reactivation of the jet 2019ApJ...875...88B , and discussed the X-shaped like morphology 2020MNRAS.494..902B . Beyond our proprietary data, in the discussion of this object we considered other data available in the literature. Namely, Very Large Array Sky Survey data at 3 GHz (VLASS, first epoch quick-look image, 2020PASP..132c5001L ), and optical images from The Panoramic Survey Telescope and Rapid Response System (Pan-STARRS, chambers2016panstarrs1 ). In Fig. fig:0318_LOFAR (top panel) we show the LOFAR full resolution image of the source, with the low resolution image contours overlaid. The source morphology is consistent with our GMRT image at 325 MHz from . The Northern and Southern lobes are the brightest components, with the latter having a peak surface brightness of 143 mJy/beam with respect to 17.3 mJy/beam for the Northern one (at 6 '' resolution). The core is well visible as a compact and bright spot at the center of the source, with a peak of 13.1 mJy/beam. Diffuse, off-axis emission is present, with a remarkable extension of 3 arcmin towards NW, and 2.5 arcmin SE from the core (corresponding to 300 kpc and 250 kpc, respectively).

sources.summary: The paper describes WNB0313+683 as a 1.52 Mpc GRG with a GPS core, FR-II morphology, recurrent activity, a Seyfert host, hard X-ray detection, and old radio lobes.

quotes.quote: R2-1: WNB0313+683 The GRG R2-1 or WNB0313+683 has a projected linear size of 1.52 Mpc with a GHz-peaked spectrum (GPS) core, FR-II type morphology, and recurrent activity Schoenmakers1998, Bruni2019 . It is hosted by a Seyfert type-1 galaxy (LEDA 3095635) at z 0.09 that is detected in hard X-rays winter08 . estimated its spectral age to be 144 Myr, but recently, Machalski11 estimated the age of the lobes to be 250 Myr.

gmw_00000754.name: J0801.7+4764
gmw_00000754.overview: J0801.7+4764 is a giant radio galaxy classified as a Fanaroff-Riley II source [arXiv:2103.00999]. It exhibits asymmetric lobes, with the northern lobe oriented toward the observer and a knotty southern lobe that is receding [arXiv:2103.00999].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0801.7+4764 as an FRII source with asymmetric lobes, knotty southern-lobe emission, diffuse lobe components, and a deprojected size of about 2 Mpc.

quotes.quote: J0801.7+4764 The LOFAR image presented here (Fig. fig:0801 ) shows a typical FRII morphology, with the Northern lobe brighter than the Southern one. The Southern lobe presents a brighter region composed by three knots at 2.5 arcmin from the core (marked as A in the figure). A check with Pan-STARRS images does not show correspondence with any host galaxy, suggesting that these are either real features along the jet, or radio emission from distant galaxies which faint emission is not detected in the optical band (see e.g. baldi13 ). The same is true for the bright spot westwards of the Northern hotspot (marked as B in the figure), that lies on a region of diffuse emission between the core and the jet. Both A and B component have a spectral index _ 0.14 ^ 1.44 =-0.91 0.06 , calculated between LOFAR and NVSS data, as expected for diffuse regions in the lobes. provided an estimate of the jet inclination angle with respect to the line of sight, on the basis of the lobes flux density ratio, and assuming it is due to Doppler boosting: they obtained a value of 37 ^ , larger than the typical maximum angle of 10 ^ found for blazars, but still resulting in an asymmetry in jets brightness. Following their argument, the Northern lobe is the one oriented towards the observer, while the Southern lobe is the receding one. Information about jet axis inclination allows us to provide an improved estimate of the physical size of the source. The source size as measured from LOFAR observations, from lobe to lobe, is 440 arcsec, that translates into a projected linear size of 1.2 Mpc at the redshift of the source. Considering an inclination angle of 37 ^ for the jet axis, the deprojected linear size of the source is 2 Mpc.

gmw_00000755.name: B2 1144+35B
gmw_00000755.overview: B2 1144+35B is a radio galaxy situated within a galaxy cluster [arXiv:2103.00999]. Multi-scale observations of its jet morphology reveal discontinuities that suggest intermittent jet activity, while low-frequency imaging recovers diffuse, extended emission around its lobes [arXiv:2103.00999].

sources.summary: LOFAR recovers extended emission around B2 1144+35B, including a larger W2 region, steep-spectrum plasma, nearby cluster environment, and evidence for intermittent or perturbed jet activity.

quotes.quote: B2 1144+35B performed a detailed study of this source through multi-scale observations, from pc to kpc. In particular, they found discontinuities in the jet morphology through VLA observations, suggesting an intermittent activity. The LOFAR image at 20 '' resolution (Fig. fig:1144 , top panel) highlights diffuse, extended emission around the lobes, and, in particular, southwards of the Western one (indicated as W2 in figure fig:1144 ). The latter was partially reconstructed by the VLA image at 1.4 GHz in , and tentatively identified as the Western lobe. The LOFAR image presented here recovers a larger emission region, both in NS and EW directions, at a frequency a factor of ten lower. We could estimate the spectral index of the W2 region using LOFAR and NVSS data, although in the latter only the brightest part is detected (see 2020MNRAS.494..902B ): we obtained a value of _ 0.14 ^ 1.44 =-0.92 0.06 . This is consistent with the value estimated by between 1.4 and 5 GHz, of about --1. In the NW sector a radio galaxy is present (corresponding to object 2MASX J11470784+3503390), moreover at the same redshift as our target ( z=0.06 ), and partially overlapping with its line of sight. Indeed, the host galaxy of our target matches with one of the clusters from the catalogue (identified with ID 2259 in that work, see Sec. cluster ).

gmw_00000756.name: J1153.9+5848
gmw_00000756.overview: J1153.9+5848 is a giant radio galaxy that exhibits two symmetric tails of faint emission detected towards the north and south sides of its core [arXiv:2103.00999]. The geometry of these features suggests an X-shaped structure, which likely originates from relic lobes or jet precession rather than backflow [arXiv:2103.00999].

sources.summary: LOFAR detects faint symmetric tails in J1153.9+5848 for the first time, with geometry suggesting an X-shaped structure from relic lobes or jet precession rather than backflow.

quotes.quote: J1153.9+5848 From the LOFAR 20 '' resolution contours of this source (Fig. fig:4maps , top left panel) two symmetric tails of faint emission are detected for the first time, towards both the North and South sides with respect to the core, and with an extension larger than the one of the ongoing radio phase. The contours highlight the presence of bright spots at the end of the tails (named H1 and H2 in Fig. fig:4maps , top left panel). The axis connecting the peaks at the edges of the structure intercepts the core region, and has a PA of -4 ^ . As a comparison, the axis connecting the main lobes of the source shows a PA of +36 ^ . Both axes are shown as dashed lines in Fig. fig:4maps . The fact that the distance between these two features is larger than the one between the lobes, allow us to reasonably exclude a backflow origin (see a similar case in 2002MNRAS.330..609D ).

gmw_00000757.name: J1238.4+5349
gmw_00000757.overview: J1238.4+5349 is a giant radio galaxy with two lobes oriented in an east-west direction [arXiv:2103.00999]. Its central region features a host-associated component and a possible jet knot, with curved emission between the lobes and the core that resembles backflow [arXiv:2103.00999].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1238.4+5349 as a LoTSS GRG whose LOFAR image shows E-W lobes, a host-associated inner component, a possible jet knot, and curved backflow-like emission.

quotes.quote: J1238.4+5349 This source was included in the GRG catalogue compiled from the first data release of LoTSS 2020A&A...635A...5D . The full resolution image (Fig. fig:4maps , bottom-left image) shows two lobes in E-W direction and two compact components in the middle. On the one hand, the easternmost among the latter ones corresponds to the X-ray position from our previous works, and shows a counterpart in the Pan-STARRS i-band image, and is thus coincident with the host galaxy of our GRG (see Fig. fig:4maps , bottom-right panel). On the other hand, the Western component of the inner couple does not correspond to any known source in the SIMBAD astronomical database 2000A&AS..143....9W , and could thus be interpreted as a knot along the jet. Finally, in the 6 arcsec resolution image from LOFAR a curved, symmetric, emission is visible between the lobes and the core (from the Eastern lobe towards North, and from the Western lobe towards South), resembling the typical morphology of a backflow.

gmw_00000758.name: J1503.7+6850
gmw_00000758.overview: J1503.7+6850 is a giant radio galaxy that exhibits a typical Fanaroff-Riley II morphology [arXiv:2103.00999]. The galaxy displays asymmetric emission with a northern lobe that is brighter than its southern counterpart, and it shows no signs of restarted radio activity [arXiv:2103.00999].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1503.7+6850 as an FRII GRG with asymmetric emission, a brighter northern lobe, an unrelated optical-source component, and no LOFAR evidence for restarted activity.

quotes.quote: J1503.7+6850 (4C+69.18) An asymmetric emission is visible for this source (Fig. fig:4maps , top-right panel), with the Northern one being brighter than the Southern one. discussed how this source almost lies on the plane of the sky, with an inclination angle of 81 ^ with respect to line of sight. This could suggest that the difference in jets brightness could be due to a denser medium probed by the Northern jet (see source Mrk 1498 below for a similar case). An additional emission spot (indicated as A in the figure) is visible southward of the core: it corresponds to an unidentified optical source in Pan-STARRS, and should thus not be considered as part of the GRG emission. At the lower resolution of NVSS it is not resolved from the core. presented a VLA image of this source at 1.4 GHz, and 10 '' resolution: the same morphological properties are visible, including component A, that shows a peak flux density of about 6 mJy/beam, resulting in a spectral index _ 0.14 ^ 1.44 0.45 . No signs of restarted radio activity are evident in the LOFAR image or previous ones from the literature, showing a typical FRII morphology.

gmw_00000759.name: 4C+63.22
gmw_00000759.overview: 4C+63.22 is a giant radio galaxy with its active galactic nucleus X-ray position corresponding to the radio core [arXiv:2003.09183, arXiv:2103.00999]. High-resolution imaging confirms it has an X-shaped morphology, though the off-axis emission could represent either remnant lobes from a previous radio phase or plasma backflow [arXiv:2103.00999].

sources.summary: 4C +63.22 is discussed as a giant radio galaxy with TGSS evidence for an external lobe pair and off-axis inner diffuse emission, possibly an X-shaped remnant.

quotes.quote: 4C +63.22: the TGSS image for this source (Fig. maps1 , bottom-right panel) shows an external couple of lobes, and an inner diffuse emission with a different axis with respect to the kpc-scale structure, similarly to X-shaped sources. This could be tentatively interpreted as a inner couple of ancient lobes, pertaining to a previous radio phase, but also as a distortion of the relaxed plasma due to its buoyancy in the intergalactic medium. A clear conclusion cannot be drawn at this angular resolution and sensitivity. The detected morphology is more evident in the higher resolution Low-Frequency Array (LOFAR) image, subject of a future work (Bruni et al. in prep.). If the X-shaped morphology is confirmed, this source could be a case similar to PKS 0707--35 and J0318+684, where the remnants of the previous radio activity are visible between the more recent and extended lobes.

sources.summary: The paper confirms 4C+63.22 as X-shaped in higher-resolution LOFAR imaging, but leaves the off-axis emission ambiguous between remnant lobes from an earlier phase and backflow.

quotes.quote: 4C+63.22 This source was presented as a tentative X-shaped radio galaxy in , based on the TIFR GMRT Sky Survey (TGSS, 2017A&A...598A..78I ) image at 150 MHz, which shows hints of emission distributed in almost perpendicular direction with respect to the main jet axis. The AGN X-ray position nicely corresponds with the radio core. The nature of the diffuse off-axis emission between the lobes and the core was not clearly established at the resolution of the available image (25 arcsec). The LOFAR image presented here (Fig. fig:4C+63 ) offers an improvement in angular resolution by a factor of 4, and a factor of 10 in sensitivity, confirming the X-shaped morphology. Nonetheless, we cannot easily classify the off-axis emission as either remnant lobes from a previous radio phase, or plasma backflow created by the present jets. Indeed, the Southern region looks like plasma expanding in the direction from the core region towards to external medium, while the Northern one presents the typical backflow morphology, with a broadening from the lobe towards the core. Finally, the optical images available from surveys are not of sufficient resolution to measure the major axis orientation, and test the scenario proposed by previously invoked for source J0318+684. Further observations, aiming at studying the plasma aging across these structures, will allow us to shed light on the evolution of this source.

gmw_00000760.name: Mrk 1498
gmw_00000760.overview: Mrk 1498 is an elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1908.06080] classified as a Seyfert 1.9 [arXiv:1908.06080] and a giant Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy [arXiv:1908.06080, arXiv:2003.09183]. It features a double-lobed radio structure [arXiv:1612.06006, arXiv:1908.06080] with a recently restarted active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2003.09183, arXiv:2103.00999] and extended, rotating ionized gas [arXiv:1612.06006, arXiv:1908.06080].

sources.summary: Mkn 1498 is discussed as a large-scale double radio-source host whose emission region is highly elongated but not biconical, with ringlike features dominated by rotation and a possible nuclear outflow.

quotes.quote: These include NGC 5252 with its well-documented set of ionization cones ( PrietoFreudling , morse ), and the large-scale double radio source hosts NGC 5972 VCV2001 and Mkn 1498 Huub .
The emission region in Mkn 1498 is highly elongated but not biconical.

Fig. fig-lineprofiles overlays the [ O III ] profiles for the Gemini IFU data on HST narrowband images in [ O III ] , with the line profiles representing averages of the data over 0.5 0.5 \ regions. figure 1 figure 1 figure 1 figure 1 figure 1 figure 1 In Mkn 1498 the ringlike emission features which are prominent at H are dominated kinematically by rotation, possibly with one outflowing feature adjacent to to the nucleus (Fig. fig-mkn1498velfields ). The implied rotation axis is close to the minor axis of the largest emission-line structures, making it almost parallel to the large-scale radio structure. In the line profiles, red wings occur along the outer ring to the south, and about 2 \ east of the nucleus along the other ring (but not the rest of it). The inner arc structure to the northwest of the nucleus has narrow line profiles, but departs from the kinematic pattern of the velocity peaks elsewhere in the ring.

sources.summary: The paper presents Mrk 1498 as a Seyfert 1.9 FR II radio galaxy with giant radio lobes, extended ionized gas, heavy nuclear absorption, restarted activity, and possible merger-driven tidal structures.

quotes.quote: In the present work we focus on the nucleus of Mrk 1498 (also named WN 1626+5153, 1626+518, Swift J1628.1+5145, RA=16h28m04.0s, DEC=+51d46m31s, z=0.0547, degrijp1992 ). This is a FR II radio source showing a classical double-lobed structure and an unresolved nucleus, which was discovered in the Westerbork Northern Sky Survey (WENSS) at 325 MHz, while in the optical it shows a broad H component (FWHM 6500 km/s ), and therefore is classified as a Seyfert 1.9 rottgering1996,winter2010 , and is hosted in an elliptical galaxy. Mrk 1498 is included in the sample of , who performed a radio/gamma-ray study of the INTEGRAL- Swift AGN population.

Interestingly, the radio lobes in Mrk 1498 have a projected separation of 1.1 Mpc rottgering1996,schoenmakers2001 , while the orientations of the emission-line structures observed by the Hubble Space Telescope ( HST ) and the large scale radio lobes observed by the Very Large Array ( VLA ) differ by about 70 degrees keel2015 . The Hubble Space Telescope ( HST ) data of Mrk 1498 shows extended emission in the [ OIII ] emission line image, revealing multiple clumps of ionised gas at radii 0.6-1.8 kpc kinematically dominated by rotation keel2017 , plus irregular and asymmetric dust lanes extending outward to 7.5 kpc. To explain its complexity, we propose that Mrk 1498 is the result of a merging event or secular processes, such as a minor interaction, that has triggered the nuclear activity and produced tidal streams.

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 1498 as a merger-linked, reactivated radio galaxy with an obscured AGN, GPS core, bent northern jet, branched southern lobe, and asymmetric structure.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1498 This source was the subject of a multi-wavelength study by , who discussed a possible reactivation of the nuclear activity following a merger event. Indeed, the source presents an obscured AGN, with a GPS in its core region 2019ApJ...875...88B . discussed the morphological properties visible in the TGSS map at 150 MHz (25 '' resolution), where basically the core and lobes where detected, while the jet connecting them was not present. The LOFAR image (Fig. fig:Mrk1498 , top panel) shows for the first time details about the complex structure along the jet, with a bent Northern jet, and a branched Southern lobe, with several knots (see a zoom on this region in figure fig:Mrk1498 , bottom-right panel). A hint of the two Southern jet branches was already visible in the TGSS image, although the lower resolution did not allow to fully distinguish them. The overall source morphology is asymmetric, with the Northern structure having almost twice the extension of the Southern one (11 arcmin versus 7.5 arcmin, from core to hotspot). A similar asymmetry was discussed by for 3C 236, explaining it as a difference in the IGM probed by the two jets. This hypothesis may hold also for Mrk 1498, moreover it could explain the enhanced surface brightness and forked morphology of the Southern jet and lobe, as a result of a strong interaction with a dense medium.

sources.summary: Mrk 1498 is described as a giant radio galaxy with strong lobes, a defined core, GPS-spectrum restarted activity, merger-related reactivation signs, and pc-scale new jet components.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1498 shows a couple of strong lobes, and a well defined core, but the jets connecting them are barely visible in the TGSS images (Fig. maps2 , top-left panel). The core shows a typical GPS radio spectrum, indicating a recently restarted radio activity 2019ApJ...875...88B that suggests the presence of two different phases. A multi-wavelength study of this source, showing signs of a possible reactivation as the consequence of a merger event, was presented in . In that work, a pc-scale VLBA image of the radio core region, showing recently emitted jet components, is presented, thus confirming the ongoing activity linked to the central GPS radio source.

sources.summary: The paper discusses Mrk 1498 as a giant radio galaxy core, using literature or archive radio measurements to evaluate spectral shape, variability, or restarting activity.

quotes.quote: Finally, for Mrk 1498, we analyzed also the JVLA archive data from 2015: since the full bandwidth of these observations was 4 GHz (spanning the observing frequency from 4 GHz to 8 GHz), we split that into four measurements of 1 GHz bandwidth each, in order to increase the frequency coverage.

gmw_00000761.name: 4C+34.47
gmw_00000761.overview: The giant radio galaxy 4C+34.47 features a well-defined core previously classified as a gigahertz-peaked spectrum source [arXiv:2103.00999]. Both its northern and southern lobes contain bright, active hotspots, and its southern jet exhibits a knotty structure that may indicate intermittent core activity [arXiv:2103.00999].

sources.summary: LOFAR reveals 4C+34.47 with a defined GPS core, bright active hotspots, knotty southern jet, and possible intermittent, recollimated, or helical jet behavior.

quotes.quote: 4C+34.47 This source presents a well defined core in the LOFAR image (Fig. fig:4C+34 , left panel), previously classified as a GPS 2019ApJ...875...88B . Both Southern and Northern lobes have bright and well defined hotspots, sign of an active fueling from the jets. The Northern jet is not visible, probably due to a Doppler dimming effect, while the Southern jet shows a knotty structure between the core and the lobe. These properties were not revealed at the lower resolution of the TGSS image discussed in . first revealed the knotty structure of the Southern jet from VLA observations at 1.4 GHz and 5 GHz, and also provided an estimate of the jet angle to line of sight. On the basis of the jet/counter-jet flux density ratio, and the jet bulk velocity, they infer a jet axis angle within 57^ from the line of sight. A knotty, discontinuous, jet could be the result of a intermittent activity of the radio core. Another option could be an alternating recollimation/expansion of the jet, resulting in an enhanced flux for the collimated components. Alternatively, a helical jet could produce a similar morphology, with brighter knots corresponding to regions where the jet orientation is close to the line of sight. In appendix Appendix:chandra , we present an archival Chandra image in the 0.5 - 7 keV energy range, were the Southern hotspot and a knot are detected.

gmw_00000762.name: J0802+4711
gmw_00000762.overview: J0802+4711 is a newly discovered radio galaxy that displays a double-double morphology with an internal dimmer set of lobes and an external brighter set [arXiv:2103.00999]. Its core is detected in the FIRST and VLASS surveys, and the source has a total projected linear size under 0.7 Mpc [arXiv:2103.00999].

sources.summary: The paper reports J0802+4711 as a newly found radio source with double-double morphology, FIRST/VLASS core detection, estimated radio power, and uncertain possible GRG nature.

quotes.quote: During inspection of the sky region in the LoTSS image of GRG J0801.7+4764, we found a new radio source which we name J0802+4711. As evident in the LOFAR image (see figure fig:newRG ) the source displays two sets of lobes: an internal dimmer one, and an external brighter one (thus resembling the morphology of a double-double radio galaxy). No core is visible at this frequency in between the two set of lobes. The 144 MHz flux density of each individual lobe is reported in table tab:newRG . The source extent is roughly 2 arcmin, implying a projected linear size smaller than the GRG threshold of 0.7 Mpc for a redshift up to z=0.45 . The source is detected in the FIRST and VLASS surveys, where however only the core and external couple of lobes are visible (i.e. the brightest and compact emission in the GHz domain, see figure fig:newRG ). Finally, the NVSS survey resolve the source into only two components, due to the lower angular resolution (45 arcsec), corresponding to the external+internal lobes regions. Nevertheless, it allow us to estimate a total flux density at 1.4 GHz of 62 mJy. The flux density values extracted for the single components in the LOFAR, FIRST, and VLASS surveys are reported in Table tab:newRG . In the FIRST image, the core is detected at only 5 sigma significance (0.76 0.08 mJy/beam, with an RMS of 0.15 mJy/beam), and thus it is not reported in the FIRST sources catalogue. Nevertheless, its position at the center of a large and complex radio source make the detection more reliable.

gmw_00000763.name: J1641+3755
gmw_00000763.overview: J1641+3755 is a radio-quiet, high-redshift quasar at z=6.04 [arXiv:2211.13820, arXiv:2206.05303] that is classified as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:2211.13820]. It is powered by a low-mass supermassive black hole accreting at a super-Eddington rate [arXiv:2211.13820, arXiv:2206.05303], and it has shown flux variations in both the X-ray and ultraviolet bands [arXiv:2206.05303].

sources.summary: The paper compares CFHQS J1641+3755 to J0921+0007, describing it as a high-redshift NLS1 with similar Mg II width, low black-hole mass, high accretion, and strong X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: The quasar CFHQS J1641+3755 at z=6.04 was initially discovered by willott07 . willott10 obtained NIR spectroscopy for this source with the NIRI instrument on the Gemini-North Telescope. It shows an Mg ii profile ( FWHM_ Mg ii = 1740 190 km s^ 1 ) that is very similar to the one observed in the MOIRCS spectrum of J0921+0007 presented in our work. According to the rakshit21 classification criterion, this makes it a high-z NLS1.

The derived black hole mass and Eddington ratio are M_ bh =2.4 10^ 8 M_ and = 2.3 , indicating that CFHQS J1641+3755 may be powered by a low-mass, strongly accreting black hole. vito19 report the X-ray observation of this quasar with Chandra . While it has a relatively modest bolometric luminosity, it is the second-most X-ray-luminous source in their sample, making it deviate from the _ OX L_ UV by 1.8 with respect to the best-fitting relation of steffen06 . vito19 also derive a steep photon index for this source ( =2.56 ).

sources.summary: The paper reports J1641+3755 as a z~6 QSO that faded sharply in X-rays while brightening in UV, and discusses explanations including obscuration and lensing.

quotes.quote: We present X-ray ( , 100 ks) follow-up observations of a z 6 QSO, J1641+3755, which was found to be remarkably X-ray bright in a 2018 dataset. J1641+3755 is not detected in the 2021 observation, implying that its X-ray flux decreased by a factor 7 on a notably short timescale (i.e., 115 rest-frame days), making it the z>4 QSO with the largest variability amplitude. We also obtained rest-frame UV spectroscopic and photometric data with the Large Binocular Telescope ( LBT ). Surprisingly, comparing our LBT photometry with archival data, we found that J1641+3755 became consistently brighter in the rest-frame UV band from 2003 to 2016, while no strong variation occurred from 2016 to 2021. Its rest-frame UV spectrum is consistent with the average spectrum of high-redshift QSOs. Multiple narrow absorption features are present, and several of them can be associated with an intervening system at z=5.67 . Several physical causes can explain the variability properties of J1641+3755, including intrinsic variations of the accretion rate, a small-scale obscuration event, gravitational lensing due to an intervening object, and an unrelated X-ray transient in a foreground galaxy in 2018.

As part of an X-ray survey of z>6 QSOs, in , we presented observations (54.3 ks in total) of the radio-quiet, This QSO has R<10 ( Vito19b ), where R=f_ ,5 GHz /f_ ,4400 is the radio-loudness parameter; i.e., the ratio of the flux densities at rest-frame 5 GHz and 4400 Å e.g., Kellerman89 . luminous ( M_ 1450 =-25.7 ; Banados16 ) optically selected QSO CFHQS J164121+375520 (hereafter J1641+3755) at z=6.047 Willott07,Willott10b . This object appears to be powered by a relatively small SMBH (log M_ BH M_ =8.4 ; Willott10b, Vito19b ) accreting at a super-Eddington rate. The main physical parameters of J1641+3755 are reported in Tab. Tab_phy_prop (see also Vito19b ). J1641+3755 was found to be one of the most luminous z>6 QSOs in X-ray band ( F_ 0.5-7 keV =1.06_ 0.15 ^ +0.16 10^ 14 , corresponding to an intrinsic luminosity L_ 2-10 keV =3.3 10^ 45 ; Vito19b ).

gmw_00000764.name: IC 5332
gmw_00000764.overview: IC 5332 is a face-on [arXiv:2012.00757], flocculent dwarf spiral galaxy [arXiv:2212.06434] located approximately 9.01 Mpc away [arXiv:2012.00757]. It is characterized by low star formation activity [arXiv:2212.02667], weak spiral structure [arXiv:2212.02667], a molecule-poor interstellar medium [arXiv:2212.06434], and low surface density throughout its disk [arXiv:2212.11177].

sources.summary: The paper includes IC 5332 in PHANGS-JWST PAH mapping, noting its lower metallicity, low disk surface density, sensitivity-limited topology, and lower PAH filling fraction than the other targets.

quotes.quote: We observed NGC 628, NGC 1365, NGC 7496, and IC 5332 with the MIRI imager between July 6 to August 13, 2022 using the F770W, F1000W, F1130W, and F2100W filters. Table tab:galaxies lists relevant properties of our targets. F770W and F1130W are both centered specifically on strong PAH features at 7.7 and 11.3 . Because of the relatively narrow widths of these filters ( 2.2 and 0.7 \ for F770W and F1130W, respectively) which are comparable or smaller than the widths of the 7.7 and 11.3 \ PAH features themselves, the fluxes in our targets are generally dominated by PAHs for typical radiation field intensities, with only a minimal contribution from the underlying hot dust continuum see models from for example draine2007a . Stellar continuum is only an important contribution to the F770W and F1130W bands towards specific mid-IR bright stars or low-ISM, high stellar surface density regions like the nucleus of NGC 628 HOYER_PHANGSJWST , which do not make up a significant fraction of the sight-lines in our maps. Given the dominance of PAH emission at the F770W and F1130W filters in our targets, we do not attempt any removal of the hot dust continuum using F1000W (which may also include a strong or dominant contribution from PAH emission) or F2100W. The observations and data reduction for the PHANGS MIRI images are described in LEE_PHANGSJWST . Validation of the background levels of the images, which is of particular importance for studying faint emission, follows the methods described in Appendix A and B of LEROY1_PHANGSJWST . Noise levels in the native resolution F770W and F1130W maps are 0.1-0.2 . lccccccc Galaxy Properties Target & Distance & M_* & SFR _ tot & 12 + (O/H) & i & R _e & F770W I_ CO,2-1 , F1130W I_ CO,2-1 & (Mpc) & (M _ ) & (M _ yr ^ 1 ) & (dex) & ( ^ ) & (kpc) & ( MJy \ sr ^ 1 \ ( K \ km s^ 1 )^ 1 ) IC 5332 & 9.0 & 9.7 & 0.4 & 8.30 & 27 & 3.6 & 0.79, 1.07 NGC 628 & 9.8 & 10.3 & 1.7 & 8.53 & 9 & 3.9 & 0.93, 1.29 NGC 1365 & 19.6 & 11.0 & 17 & 8.67 & 55 & 2.8 & 0.72, 1.07 NGC 7496 & 18.7 & 10.0 & 2.2 & 8.51 & 36 & 3.8 & 1.29, 1.78 Properties adopted following LEE_PHANGSJWST , which draws distances from anand21 ; stellar masses ( M_* ), star formation rates (SFR _ tot ), and effective radius (R _e ) values from leroy2021 ; inclinations ( i ) from lang20 ; and metallicities ( 12+ (O/H)) from B. Groves, K. Kreckel et al. (MNRAS submitted). Gas phase metallicities are presented on the S cal system scal16 estimated at the effective radius R_e . The F770W I_ CO,2-1 and F1130W I_ CO,2-1 values are taken from LEROY1_PHANGSJWST . Our Approach and Caveats As discussed above, tracing gas surface density with PAH emission involves several assumptions: 1) a PAH-to-gas ratio (which can be separated into dust-to-gas and PAH fraction components), and 2) a radiation field intensity and spectrum (in the regime where PAHs experience single-photon heating so the scaling with radiation field intensity is linear). In this Letter, we make two main assumptions that allow us use PAH emission to estimate the gas column density. These assumptions are likely too simplistic and should be greatly improved in future work, but they let us perform a pilot study for this approach to tracing gas. First, we assume that the PAH emission to gas surface density ratio does not vary with cold gas phase. We can then pick regions where we know the gas surface density to empirically estimate this ratio. For this purpose, we select regions that are likely to be H _2 dominated where we also have an estimate of the H _2 surface density based on ALMA ^ 12 CO J=(2-1) maps. To do this, we convert the CO emission to gas surface density with an assumed CO-to-H _2 conversion factor and find the ratio with PAH emission. The second assumption we make is that the diffuse ISRF responsible for heating the dust is approximately constant outside the vicinity of star-forming regions. This allows us to apply the PAH emission to gas surface density ratio measured for the molecular gas to the rest of the ISM. In practice, we adopt the median ratio of PAH emission to CO (2-1) emission measured by LEROY1_PHANGSJWST for moderate mid-IR intensities 0.5 to 30 and 1.7'' 70 160 pc resolution in each target. Over this range of mid-IR intensities, the PAH tracing F770W and F1130W emission correlates strongly with CO and an approximately linear relationship relates the PAH emission to CO emission The relationship between CO emission and PAH emission is not exactly linear. We adopt the median ratio for simplicity. If we had chosen to evaluation the power law fits of CO to PAH emission in LEROY1_PHANGSJWST at 1 \ instead, the ratio of PAH emission to CO emission would be 20 50 higher. However, at this low surface brightness, Hi could already make important contributions to the total gas surface density. . These PAH emission to CO ratios resemble those expected for typical diffuse ISRF values in inner galaxy regions ( U few U_ MMP U_ MMP is the ISRF intensity normalized to the mathis83 Solar neighborhood ISRF. ), PAH fractions ( q_ PAH 4-5 ), and dust-to-gas ratios (D/G 0.01 ; see their Equations 4 and 6). But we emphasize that the value we adopt is fundamentally empirical and anchored to the ALMA CO (2-1) data, not derived from models. At the high resolution of our data we expect this median PAH to CO emission ratio to capture mostly diffuse ISM emission. Massive star forming regions with high radiation fields are certainly present and associated with bright PAH and continuum emission e.g., CHASTENET1_PHANGSJWST,EGOROV_PHANGSJWST,HASSANI_PHANGSJWST , but as Figures fig:contour_ic5332 through fig:contour_7496 show, such bright regions do not make up most of the area in our maps, and typically only dominate the dust heating on scales of 100 pc e.g., lawton2010 . Adopting these two assumptions and this empirical ratio of PAH emission to CO emission, we calibrate a relationship between PAH emission and gas surface density. Then we extrapolate this scaling to the diffuse ISM, where we expect the gas to be predominantly atomic. We note that we do not mask star forming regions in our resulting maps, but our focus on low surface density gas (or equivalently, low surface brightness PAH emission) means such regions are generally not included in the diffuse gas we consider. For the purpose of this pilot study, these simple assumptions and this fundamentally empirically approach allow a straightforward scaling of PAH emission to gas surface density, but a number of potential effects will need to be addressed in future work to rigorously calibrate PAH emission as a tracer. There is evidence from measurements of depletion that dust-to-gas ratios vary with ISM density jenkins09,romanduval2021 by a factor of up to 2, though this depends on whether the observed depleted oxygen is incorporated into dust whittet2010,choban2022 . Changes in the dust-to-gas ratio between ISM phases at fixed metallicity have also been observed using far-IR, , and CO observations romanduval2014,chiang2018,vilchez2019 . From high spatial resolution studies of Local Group galaxies chastenet2019 and the early PHANGS-JWST targets CHASTENET1_PHANGSJWST,EGOROV_PHANGSJWST , the PAH fraction appears to be relatively constant in the atomic and molecular phases, but does drop sharply in H II regions, and has a metallicity dependence. Milky Way observations have suggested that PAH abundance can vary between the warm neutral medium (WNM) and cold neutral medium (CNM) hensley2022 . The diffuse ISRF in galaxies, judged from the U_ min component in far-IR spectral energy distribution modeling e.g. draine2007b,aniano12 , appears to vary smoothly and primarily radially in highly resolved Local Group galaxies draine2014,chastenet2019 . In the Milky Way, the diffuse ISRF varies primarily radially and is dominated by B stars mathis83 . To summarize, we expect there are a number of effects that can alter the PAH emission to gas surface density conversion, not all of which will be mitigated by our local calibration of the scaling in each galaxy. PAH Emission to Gas Surface Density Scaling We adopt PAH emission to CO ratios for each galaxy from LEROY1_PHANGSJWST . We note that these values are specific to the four galaxies we target and could change if D/G, q_ PAH , or the diffuse ISRF intensities vary. The average value across all galaxies for the ratio of PAH emission to CO (2 1) integrated intensity are: F770W MJy \ sr ^ 1 = 0.91 \ I_ CO,2-1 K \ km s^ 1 F1130W MJy \ sr ^ 1 = 1.26 \ I_ CO,2-1 K \ km s^ 1 These ratios vary slightly between galaxies. The ratio for F770W is 0.79, 0.93, 0.72, and 1.29 in IC 5332, NGC 628, 1365, and 7496, respectively. For F1130W, it ranges between 1.07, 1.29, 1.07, and 1.78 for the same galaxies see Table 3, and Table tab:galaxies above LEROY1_PHANGSJWST . Assuming the Milky Way CO-to-H _2 conversion factor, including He and metals, _ CO 1-0 ^ MW = 4.35 \ pc ^ 2 (K km/s) ^ 1 ) bolatto2013 , and a CO J = (2-1) to (1-0) line ratio of R_ 21 =0.65 denbrok2021,leroy2022 , we can convert these ratios into PAH emission per gas surface density, assuming all gas is molecular. These values, for the average ratios shown in Equation eq:ratio77 and eq:ratio113 , are: _ gas M_ \ pc ^2 = 7.4 F770W MJy \ sr ^ 1 _ CO 1-0 _ CO 1-0 ^ MW 0.65 R_ 21 _ gas M_ \ pc ^2 = 5.3 F1130W MJy \ sr ^ 1 _ CO 1-0 _ CO 1-0 ^ MW 0.65 R_ 21 Given that the noise levels are comparable in the F770W and F1130W maps at native resolution (between 0.1 0.2 MJy sr ^ 1 ), it is clear that our most sensitive tracer is F1130W, so we proceed in the following to use that as our primary observable. F1130W's point spread function has a FWHM of 0.36'' , yielding 15.7, 17.1, 34.2, and 32.6 pc resolution at the distances of IC 5332, NGC 628, NGC 1365, and NGC 7496, respectively. We note that at this resolution, which resolves giant molecular cloud complexes and is significantly smaller than the expected scale height of the gas disk, correcting for the inclination of the galaxy introduces an additional assumption about the extent of the emitting structures along the line of sight. The appropriate inclination correction may differ between clouds or a diffuse medium. Therefore, we proceed in the following without an inclination correction, but note that if one were applied, the largest effect would be for NGC 1365 which has an inclination of i=55^ . For this pilot study, we use a single scaling factor to convert PAH emission to gas surface density. Several of the components in the conversion of PAH emission to gas surface density are likely to change radially in galaxies. The CO-to-H _2 conversion factor likely increases with radius, tracking decreasing metallicity wolfire2010,leroy2011,glover2012,bolatto2013,madden2020 and also can decrease in the centers of galaxies sandstrom2013 , likely due to excitation and optical depth effects teng2022 . Dust-to-gas ratios and PAH fractions may decrease with metallicity, and therefore radius we note that the observed PAH fraction in our targets does vary with metallicity, but at a relatively minor level given the Z_ metallicities; CHASTENET1_PHANGSJWST . The diffuse ISRF also is likely to vary radially, tracing a combination of the stellar mass surface density and star formation rate surface density of the galaxies mathis83,wolfire2003 . In future work, we aim to calibrate a radially dependent PAH-to-gas surface density tracer by finding diffuse ISRF dominated, CO-bright regions to measure the scaling factor as a function of radius. We note that some of the factors will likely vary with opposing radial trends, potentially leading to a less variable PAH-to-gas conversion than the individual components. In Figures fig:contour_ic5332 to fig:contour_7496 we show the F1130W emission re-scaled to gas surface density using the scalings quoted above for each galaxy. In regions without bright CO emission, the diffuse ISM traced by our scaled PAH maps may include a combination of: (i) CO-bright \ missed due to the current CO map sensitivity, (ii) a CO-dark \ component, or (iii) a mixture of atomic gas in the WNM and CNM phases. Shielding models for \ formation KrumholzMcKee2009ApJ...693..216K,SternbergLePetit2014ApJ...790...10S,BialySternberg2016ApJ...822...83B predict the transition between \ or \ dominated ISM at _ HI 10 (Z/Z_ )^ 1 . In the models, the transition point varies with the assumed geometry and role of self-shielding. In simulations which take into account the filamentary, turbulent nature of the gas, the column density of the / \ transition can vary significantly e.g. glover2007,glover2016 . Transitions at 10 \ have been measured in the Milky Way StanimirovicMurray2014ApJ...793..132S,LeeStanimirovic2015ApJ...809...56L . This transition is found at higher surface density in the Magellanic Clouds JamesonBolatto2016ApJ...825...12J , following the expected trend with metallicity. Similarly, SchrubaBialy2018ApJ...862..110S find a (Z/Z_ )^ 1 dependence sampling a range of metallicities in 70 nearby galaxies, primarily on >100 pc scales. The galaxies we target are all at approximately Solar metallicity, aside from IC 5332 which has a gradient extending down to Z 0.5 Z_ .

IC 5332 has low surface density throughout its disk, and a large fraction of the emission in the map is below 7 \ pc ^ 2 . This is in good agreement with the faint CO emission observed in this galaxy leroy2021 . Many of the faint structures in all of the targets appear filamentary. Filaments in the PAH emission are explored in much greater detail in THILKER_PHANGSJWST and MEIDT_PHANGSJWST in this Issue. In some cases, filamentary structures are observed to have widths comparable to a few resolution elements, and lengths that are substantially longer. For instance, in a few cases in NGC 628, we measure lengths of up to 400 pc for filamentary structures with _ gas < 7 \ pc ^ 2 , yielding a 10:1 aspect ratio. Many of these filamentary structures appear as spurs or low surface density continuations of spiral arm structures. While such morphology may be expected as a consequence of the spiral structure of the galaxies, previous \ observations have not had the resolution to characterize these outside the Local Group. Throughout the diffuse ISM, structure is evident down to the noise level of the maps ( 3 2 ). At present, comparing our predicted surface densities from scaled F1130W to measured CO and \ is limited by the available gas observations. The ALMA CO observations from the PHANGS survey have noise limits near this surface density leroy2021,LEROY1_PHANGSJWST . More limiting is the availability and resolution of \ data. NGC 628 was observed as part of the THINGS survey walter2008 and the ``robust'' weighted map has 6'' resolution. NGC 7496 has recent MeerKAT observations at 12'' resolution. Future observations with higher resolution, higher S/N \ and CO maps for the PHANGS-JWST targets will be necessary to measure gas surface densities for comparison to the F1130W emission at these faint levels. One of the distinctive features of the faint regions of the F770W and F1130W maps is the clear presence of holes in the ISM distribution (see Figures fig:contour_ic5332 to fig:contour_7496 ). These holes may be due to dynamical effects or feedback, topics explored in more detail in WATKINS_PHANGSJWST , BARNES_PHANGSJWST , and MEIDT_PHANGSJWST . The pervasive emission in the PAH-tracing bands is one of the reasons these holes appear so clearly in the JWST maps. To quantify the topology of the faint emission, we use the genus statistic. The genus statistic is the difference between the number of connected regions above and below a brightness threshold. A positive genus corresponds to a topology reflecting compact regions or sources that are bright compared to the threshold, surrounded by connected networks of lower brightness regions. Positive genus topology is referred to as source-dominated or a ``meatball''-like. A negative genus is characterized by connected networks of emission above the threshold level and isolated holes below the threshold, which are referred to as a hole-dominated or a ``Swiss-cheese'' topology Coles1988MNRAS.234..509C . By computing the genus through a range of intensity values, the resulting genus curve characterizes variations in the topology. When the genus curve crosses zero, the topology changes from ``Swiss-cheese'' to ``meatball''. The genus statistic is a well-known measure of spatial topology that was developed to study the topology of the cosmic microwave background Coles1988MNRAS.234..509C and has since been used to quantify the topology of the turbulent ISM, both in simulations and observations e.g., KowalLazarian2007ApJ...666L..69K,ChepurnovGordon2008ApJ...688.1021C,BurkhartStanimirovic2010ApJ...708.1204B,KochWard2017MNRAS.471.1506K,Burkhart2021PASP..133j2001B . Here we use the implementation of the Genus statistic in the TurbuStat v1.3; turbustat.readthedocs.io package Koch2019 . We compute the genus curve for each scaled F1130W image at native resolution using a set of intensities chosen by percentiles in the images. We discard all regions smaller than 20 pixels, 4 the F1130W PSF area, in the images to minimize the effect of noise on the genus curves, where a connected region includes pixels adjacent along both the edges and corners. We note that the zero-crossing point is not strongly affected by the choice of minimum area (for 10 - 40 pixels). There is no restriction on the morphology of a region (e.g., round vs. elongated). We additionally impose three masking constraints on the data when computing the genus: We mask the PSF artifacts from the active galactic nuclei in NGC 1365 and NGC 7496. We use PSFs generated with WebbPSF and impose a masking cut-off at 1/1000^ th the maximum of the the normalized PSF. Two negative strips remain in the NGC 1365 (vertical through center, see Figure fig:contour_1365 ) and NGC 7496 (horizontal through center, see Figure fig:contour_7496 ) mosaics after our calibration. We impose by-hand masks to remove the visible negative regions. Finally, we apply a radial mask at R_ gal > 0.4R_ 25 to provide a consistent basis for comparison between the 4 targets. This corresponds to radial cut-offs of 3.2, 5.7, 13.7, 3.6 kpc in IC 5332, NGC 628, NGC 1365 and NGC 7496, respectively. This cut-off was chosen based on the range of R_ gal that is well-sampled across all four galaxies, and where diffuse PAH emission is visually obvious in Figures fig:contour_ic5332 to fig:contour_7496 . Masks applied to the data will reduce the value of the genus statistic, roughly proportional to the total area, but will not change the relative shape within a single closed contour. Because of the wide dynamic range, we empirically choose 600 thresholds linearly sampled from the 0.01 to 98^ th percentiles, and 50 logarithmically sampled thresholds from the 98^ th to 99.99^ th percentiles. This ensures we sample the genus curve well near zero-crossings, tracing transitions from ``Swiss cheese'' to ``meatball'' topologies in the faint emission tail, while still characterizing the high-end intensity tail. Figure fig:genus_1130 shows the genus curves in the F1130W band We note the F770W band has a similar topology and the genus analysis gives similar results, though with lower surface density sensitivity (see sec:obs ). computed with a cut R_ gal < 0.4R_ 25 . There is a clear transition in the topology traced by the 11.3 m emission that varies by galaxy. Table tab:genus_crossings provides the zero crossing points from the genus curves. The genus crossing corresponds to a gas surface density of 8 - 10 \ (scaled from Eq. eq:ratio113 ) for NGC 628, NGC 1365, and NGC 7496. This closely matches the expected H _2 HI transition point of 10 \ based on shielding models at solar metallicity. The genus zero crossing point in IC 5332 is substantially lower, at 1.6 \ (0.3 MJy sr ^ 1 ), 2 significance above the background level of the map, and thus affected by noise fluctuations altering the genus curve. A field of Gaussian noise will produce a similar transition from negative to positive genus, with a zero-crossing at the mean field value Coles1988MNRAS.234..509C . The significant detected structure alters the shape of the genus, as we see in Figure fig:genus_1130 , but for the sensitivity-limited IC 5332, the genus zero-crossing is qualitatively consistent with a noise-like field. The diffuse PAH emission in IC 5332 being sensitivity-limited is a substantially lower filling fraction in the PAH emission compared to the other three galaxies (Figure fig:contour_ic5332 ). Given that IC 5332 is the only dwarf spiral in this sample, with a lower stellar mass, star formation rate, and metallicity relative to the other galaxies, this lower filling fraction at a common sensitivity limit is expected for example, in the Magellenic Clouds Jameson2018,Chevance2020b .

sources.summary: The paper includes IC 5332 among the first PHANGS-JWST galaxies, noting incomplete NIRCam coverage, MIRI-only initial imaging with borrowed off-galaxy background, visible background imperfections, low star formation, and weak spiral structure.

quotes.quote: Due to a guide star acquisition failure, the NIRCam observations of IC 5332 were not completed and have been rescheduled for later in JWST Cycle 1. Since our observation strategy uses the time when the NIRCam observations are targeting the galaxy to observe the MIRI off-source position ``in parallel,'' our IC 5332 dataset also currently lacks an off-source observation for MIRI.

For IC 5332, only the on-galaxy MIRI observations were obtained during our initial visit. Therefore, we used the off-galaxy observation from NGC 7496 for this purpose in IC 5332. This appears to work reasonably well, but does result in a few visible imperfections in the background subtraction for IC 5332.

The four galaxies bracket the full range of star formation properties in the sample (with NGC 1365 having the highest star formation activity and gas surface density and IC 5332 among the lowest) and feature varied dynamical structures (e.g., bars in NGC 1365 and NGC 7496; strong spiral arms in NGC 628 and weak spiral structure in IC 5332).

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 5332 as a flocculent dwarf spiral with a molecule-poor ISM, JWST filamentary structure, and azimuthal filament spacings measured for disk-fragmentation tests.

quotes.quote: JWST/MIRI imaging of the nearby galaxies IC 5332, NGC 628, NGC 1365 and NGC 7496 from PHANGS reveals a richness of gas structures that in each case form a quasi-regular network of interconnected filaments, shells and voids. IC 5332 is a flocculent dwarf spiral, NGC 628 is a moderate-mass grand design spiral, NGC 1365 is a massive, strongly-barred spiral galaxy, and NGC 7496 is a moderate-mass barred spiral LEE_PHANGSJWST .

In the case of the molecule-poor galaxy IC 5332, we estimate that the ISM is roughly 10 molecular and thus we supplement CO constraints with additional information, as described more below. For IC 5332, we use the semi-empirical rotation curve model implied by this galaxy's stellar mass see leroy21b , following .

The filaments in NGC 628 and IC 5332 also tend to increase in both separation and length moving outwards in galactocentric radius. We thus measure either the azimuthal separation between filament features at a given radius -- in NGC 628 and IC 5332 -- or the radial separations between filaments at a given azimuth -- in NGC 1365 and NGC 7496.

sources.summary: The paper gives the first reliable TRGB distance to face-on spiral IC 5332, measuring 9.01 Mpc from a well-placed PHANGS-HST parallel field.

quotes.quote: IC 5332 is a face-on spiral galaxy in the Sculptor constellation, but lying beyond the Sculptor group of galaxies. There was previously no reliable distance to this galaxy, with estimates relying on a tenuous group linkage with NGC 7713, or based on its recessional velocity. Our PHANGS-HST parallel is well-placed, with only a small portion of the field experiencing contamination from a young star cluster (which we remove). Based on this data, we measure m_ TRGB = 25.70 0.05 mag, which gives us D = 9.01 0.38 Mpc.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes IC 5332 as a PHANGS-JWST target with lower CO signal-to-noise, using an estimated rotation curve and noting noisier flat maps at the common resolution.

quotes.quote: We study the first four PHANGS-JWST targets: NGC 628, NGC 1365, NGC 7496, and IC 5332 and compare mid-IR emission at 7.7 m, 10 m, 11.3 m, and 21 m obtained as part of the PHANGS-JWST survey PHANGSJWST22 to CO (2-1) observed by ALMA as part of the PHANGS--ALMA survey PHANGSALMA21 and extinction-corrected H obtained as part of the PHANGS--MUSE survey PHANGSMUSE22 . For IC 5332, which has lower signal to noise than the other galaxies in CO, we use an estimated rotation curve for the reference at all locations.

After adjusting the cube so that all emission is centered in roughly the same channel, we integrate over a fixed velocity window picked to encompass all readily detected emission in the disk ( v =25 , 35 , 80 , and 55 km s ^ 1 for IC 5332, NGC 0628, 1365, and 7496). Aside from the higher but more even noise, which can be seen particularly clearly in the images of NGC 1365 (Figure fig:ngc1365 ) and IC 5332 (Figure fig:ic5332 ), these maps capture essentially the same features as the standard PHANGS broad maps (compare to the atlas images for the same galaxies in PHANGSALMA21 ). The comparison includes Spitzer 8 m and 24 m data, which are available for IC 5332, NGC 0628, and NGC 1365 but not NGC 7496, and WISE 12 m data, which are available for all targets.

sources.summary: The paper reports that IC 5332 is in both PHANGS samples but lacks detected ALMA CO emission; its MUSE kinematics show no reliable pattern because the velocity field is irregular.

quotes.quote: We find that all of the MUSE galaxies can be fitted, but due to no detected CO emission in the cube, the ALMA data for IC 5332 and NGC 3239 cannot be fitted. This leaves us with a total of 82 ALMA galaxies, and 18 of these overlap with MUSE observations. IC 5332 is present in both samples, but as no emission is detected in the ALMA cube, we only present measurements from the MUSE observations.

For some with a quality flag of 3 or 4, we detect no evidence for a pattern (IC 5332 and NGC 5068), but in these cases the velocity field is very irregular (and so the position angles change with radius).

For the nine galaxies that do not have well-measured pattern speeds, in two we detect no evidence of a pattern (IC 5332 and NGC 5068), and for the others we find the velocity field to be extremely messy, meaning the galaxy position angle changes strongly with radius.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes IC 5332 with PHANGS-JWST MIRI data, finding 188 compact 21 micron sources, while noting missing NIRCam imaging and too few recovered ALMA GMCs.

quotes.quote: We analyze MIRI data from four galaxies in the PHANGS-JWST survey described by LEE_PHANGSJWST : IC 5332, NGC 0628, NGC 1365 and NGC 7496 1 with properties summarized in Table tab:props . The data presented in this paper were obtained from the Mikulski Archive for Space Telescopes (MAST) at the Space Telescope Science Institute The specific observations analyzed can be accessed via 10.17909/9bdf-jn24 http://dx.doi.org/10.17909/9bdf-jn24 . We focus our analysis on the sources detected in the MIRI 21 data (F2100W filter, FWHM: 0 67 ) though we consider all data from the PHANGS-JWST filter set, which includes NIRCam (F200W, F300M, F335M, F360M) for NGC 0628, NGC 1365 and NGC 7496, and MIRI (F770W, F1000W, F1130W, and F2100W) for all four targets IC 5332 NIRCam observations have not been obtained yet. .
Our final catalog consists of 1271 compact sources with 188 in IC 5332, 502 in NGC 0628, 367 in NGC 1365 and 214 in NGC 7496.
We do not report results for IC 5332 GMCs since the ALMA map only recovers 11 GMCs in this low metallicity system.

sources.summary: The paper treats IC 5332 as the least-massive PHANGS-MUSE galaxy, excluded from some analyses for missing detections or H I data and showing the least significant homogeneity trend.

quotes.quote: IC 5332 is excluded from this analysis, as it contains no detected pixel above the necessary S/N cut.
All three tracers are currently available for only six of our eight galaxies, as IC 5332 and NGC 1672 currently have no H i observations available and are thus excluded.
IC 5332, the least massive galaxy in the sample, shows the least significant trend, but it also has the most limited statistics, with only 223 \ regions detected.

sources.summary: The paper maps PAH fraction across IC 5332 with JWST/MIRI, reporting its average PAH ratio and the lowest mean ratio among the four targets.
