Additionally, discovered a quasar close to SDSS J0927+2943, that has a radial velocity difference of 1400 km s ^ 1 and projected distance of 125 h _ 70 ^ 1 kpc 125 kpc for H _0 70 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 . Also supported the idea that this system is a superposition of two active galactic nuclei.

sources.summary: J0927+2943 is a peculiar QSO with multiple emission-line systems; CO observations find a galactic-scale molecular reservoir at the blue-system redshift, ruling out recoil/binary interpretations.

quotes.quote: The peculiar QSO J0927+2943 shows multiple sets of emission lines in its optical spectrum. This signature has been interpreted as the relative motion between a black hole, either recoiling or bound in a binary system, and its host galaxy, or as a superposition of two galaxies along the line of sight. In order to test these scenarios, we have collected CO(2--1) observations using the IRAM Plateau de Bure Interferometer, and optical images and spectroscopy at the Calar Alto observatory.

We find a clear line spatially consistent with the position of the QSO. The line peaks at 135.82 0.06 GHz. The associated CO(2--1) redshift is 0.69744 0.00008 , consistent with the redshift of the broad lines and the `blue' system of narrow lines in the optical QSO spectrum. The line flux is 0.32 0.05 Jy , yielding a line luminosity L_ = (8.1 1.3) 10^5 or L'=(2.1 0.3) 10^9 K pc ^2 . Assuming that CO emission is thermalized, and a ULIRG-like CO--to--H _2 conversion factor ( _ CO =0.8 [ K pc ^2 ] ^ 1 ), we derive a molecular gas mass of 1.7 10^9 .

sources.summary: The review describes SDSS J0927+2943 as an unusual quasar with two redshifted emission-line systems, interpreted as possible gravitational-wave recoil ejecting an SMBH from its host galaxy.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0927+2943 is an unusual quasar (Figure fig:example_1 f) whose spectrum shows two sets of optical emission lines at different redshifts. The first, or ``red” set consists of narrow [ O III ] lines and other ionized elements with a redshift of z =0.712. Such lines are usually associated with gas far from the center of the galaxy, excited by radiation of the central quasar. The second, ``blue" set consists of broad emission lines that are typically associated with hot gas very close to the galaxy center (and presumably to the central SMBH); these lines have a redshift of 0.697. The velocity difference between the two emission line systems is 2650 km/s.

The origin of these line systems is believed to be due to gravitational wave recoil: the ejection of a supermassive black hole from the center of the host galaxy. In this interpretation, one set of emission lines arises from gas associated with the SMBH, and the other set is associated with gas left behind in the galaxy. Precisely, when a black hole is ejected from the nucleus of a galaxy, this black hole carries away gas emitting broad lines, leaving behind most of the gas emitting narrow lines.

sources.summary: The paper studies SDSS J092712.64 + 294344.0 as an unusual quasar/AGN-like source, constraining acceleration, extended [O iii] emission, and alternatives involving recoil, binary black holes, or merging systems.

quotes.quote: Received; in original form firstpage - lastpage 2009 We report long-slit spectroscopic observations of SDSS J092712 + 294344 carried-out at the recently commissioned 2 m telescope in IUCAA Girawali Observatory, India. This AGN-like source is known to feature three sets of emission lines at = 0.6972, 0.7020 and 0.7128. Different scenarios such as a recoiling black hole after asymmetric emission of gravitational waves, binary black holes and possible merging systems are proposed for this object. We test these scenarios by comparing our spectra with that from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS), obtained 4 years prior to our observations.

Comparing the redshifts of [ O iii ] 4960,5008 we put a 3 limit on the relative acceleration to be less than 32 yr ^ 1 between different emitting regions. Using the 2D spectra obtained at different position angles we show that the [ O iii ] 5008 line from the = 0.7128 component is extended beyond the spectral point spread function. We infer the linear extent of this line emitting region is 8 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper treats J0927 as an unusual SDSS quasar with offset emission-line systems, viable binary or recoiling-black-hole interpretations, and unresolved competing explanations.

quotes.quote: So far a few SBHB candidates have been selected out of 21,000 SDSS quasars: J092712.65+294344.0 hereafter J0927; bogdanovic09, dotti09 , J153636.22+044127.0 J1536; bl09 , and J105041.35+345631.3 J1050; shields09 , where the first and the last object have also been flagged as recoiling SBH candidates komossa08b,shields09 . In the following section we describe in more detail results and uncertainties associated with this approach, using J0927 as an illustrative example, and summarize the status of the other two candidates. SBHB candidates: J0927, J1536, and J1050 The optical spectrum of J0927 features two sets of emission lines offset by 2650 km s^ 1 with respect to each other.

According to the recoiling SBH interpretation for J0927 note that this was the first explanation proposed for this object by komossa08b , the b-BELs originate in the broad-line region retained by the SBH, the b-NELs were attributed to gas that is marginally bound to the SBH, swept up or outflowing, and the -NELs are associated with the ISM of the host galaxy (the geometry of the recoiling SBH model is outlined in Figure 2). In this model the recoiling SBH of mass 6 10^8 appears as an AGN during the period in which it accretes from the disk that is carried along with the hole. The accreting SBH provides a source of ionizing radiation for the b-BELs and b-NELs, as well as for the -NELs, albeit from a larger distance. Another class of proposed explanations for J0927 includes interaction of two galaxies in the potential well of a massive cluster heckman09 and a superposition of otherwise unrelated AGN in a cluster environment sbs09 .

sources.summary: The paper argues SDSSJ092712.65+294344.0 is more likely a circumbinary-disc massive black-hole binary than a recoiling black hole, explaining its shifted broad and narrow lines.

quotes.quote: In this Letter we explore the hypothesis that the quasar SDSSJ092712.65+294344.0 is hosting a massive black hole binary embedded in a circumbinary disc. The lightest, secondary black hole is active, and gas orbiting around it is responsible for the blue--shifted broad emission lines with velocity off-set of 2650 km s ^ 1 , relative to the galaxy rest frame. As the tidal interaction of the binary with the outer disc is expected to excavate a gap, the blue-shifted narrow emission lines are consistent with being emitted from the low-density inhomogeneous gas of the hollow region.

We use the results of cosmological merger trees to estimate the likely-hood of observing SDSSJ092712.65+294344.0 as recoiling black hole or as a binary. We find that the binary hypothesis is preferred being one hundred times more probable than the ejection hypothesis. If SDSSJ092712.65+294344.0 hosts a binary, it would be the one closest massive black hole binary system ever discovered.

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J092712.65+294344.0 as a puzzling quasar with velocity-separated emission systems, interpreted as a recoiling black hole, binary black hole, or merger case.

quotes.quote: Within this line of research, which at the moment had covered only few objects, here we present the discovery of a companion quasar of the peculiar quasar SDSS J092712.65+294344.0 (hereafter, 0927), a promising massive black hole binary / recoiling black hole candidate (see section sec_s0927 ). The present discovery of a second quasar with v 1400 km/s along the line of sight and projected separation 125 h_ 70 ^ 1 kpc adds new, unexpected elements for our comprehension of 0927. In section sec_obs we present our observations and the data reduction. Results are given in section sec_res . In section sec_disc we discuss the nature of this quasar pair, focussing in particular on its environment.

0927 (RA(J2000): 09^ h 27^ m 12^ s .6 , Dec(J2000): +29^ d 43^ m 44^ s ; u = 18.69 , g = 18.42 , = 18.40 , i = 18.40 , z = 18.34 ) is a puzzling quasar, discovered by komossa08 out of the enormous SDSS spectroscopic database. It shows two sets of narrow emission lines at different redshifts (hereafter, the `blue' system at z_ b =0.698 and the `rest-frame' system at z_ rf =0.713 ; v 2650 km/s), and a set of broad emission lines at z_ b .

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSSJ092712.65+294344.0 as a quasar candidate for a recoiling supermassive black hole, emphasizing its unusual broad and narrow emission-line systems and large velocity shift.

quotes.quote: Recently, the first compelling candidate for a recoiling SMBH was discovered (Komossa et al. 2008). The quasar SDSSJ092712.65+294344.0 at z=0.7 exhibits two separate sets of unusual emission lines: a set of broad emission lines, presumably associated with gas bound to a SMBH, and a second set of atypically narrow emission lines. The broad lines are shifted by 2650 relative to the narrow lines. This is one of the key predicted signatures of a recoiling SMBH, which would carry with it the gas from the broad line region while leaving the bulk of the narrow line gas behind (Merritt et al. 2006; Bonning et al. 2007).

sources.summary: The paper classifies J0927+2943 as asymmetric line profile, describing shifted or unusual emission-line structure used to evaluate whether it is a massive black-hole binary candidate or another peculiar quasar.

quotes.quote: Classified: asymmetric line profile. J0927+2943 One of the known BHB candidates bogdanovic09,dotti09 . This quasar was first reported by komossa08 as a recoiling BH candidate. Three sets of lines are observed, two narrow systems at z=0.713 and z=0.699 and a broad line system consistent with the second set of narrow lines.

sources.summary: The paper presents SDSS J092712.65+294344.0 as a controversial quasar candidate, interpreted as either a recoiling black hole, a binary black hole, or a superposition.

quotes.quote: The quasar SDSS J092712.65+294344.0 was interpreted as a SBHB by bogdanovic09 and dotti09a , although it was proposed by komossa08a as a candidate recoiling BH (see also our discussion below).

As we mentioned above, SDSS J092712.65+294344.0 was identified by komossa08a via its shifted H line, who suggested that this is a rapidly-recoiling black hole. However, a number of different interpretations were offered by followup papers, including a binary black hole bogdanovic09, dotti09a , a chance superposition of objects at different redshifts on the sky shields09b , and a superposition of objects in the same, massive galaxy cluster heckman09 .

gmw_00000775.name: UGC 7559
gmw_00000775.overview: UGC 7559 is an irregular dwarf galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/9910178, arXiv:astro-ph/0210414] located approximately 4.9 megaparsecs away in the Canes Venatici I Group [arXiv:2006.01735]. The galaxy appears disrupted with a knotty structure [arXiv:astro-ph/9910178], is dominated by dark matter in its outer regions [arXiv:1111.6609], and contains four neutral hydrogen holes [arXiv:2006.01735].

sources.summary: The paper uses UGC 7559 as a dwarf-galaxy rotation-curve example, finding that its stellar mass, dark-matter dominance, and outer rotation velocity roughly match abundance-matching expectations.

quotes.quote: Fig. FigRotCur shows as well the rotation curves of two dwarf galaxies. The left panel shows UGC 7559, a galaxy with stellar mass M_ gal 1.7 10^7 M_ Swaters1999 ; the panel on the right shows the M_ gal 2.6 10^6 M_ Sagittarius Dwarf Irregular SDIG, Cote2000 . The published rotation curves are shown by the solid symbols with error bars; the smaller symbols connected by a dotted line shows the circular velocity profile once the contribution of the baryons (gas+stars) has been discounted. These two galaxies, like most faint galaxies in our sample, are clearly dark matter dominated in the outer regions.

According to this model, the total mass of the halo inhabited by UGC 7559 ought to be 2.8 10^ 10 M_ . As Fig. FigRotCur makes clear, UGC 7559 is in rough agreement with the model expectation. Its rotation curve reaches a maximum of V_ out 32 km/s, at r_ out =2.1 kpc, only slightly below the V_ out ^P 40 km/s expected at that radius according to the model.

sources.summary: The paper discusses DDO 126 as a case where MOND matches the rotation-curve shape but overpredicts amplitude unless a shorter distance estimate is adopted.

quotes.quote: For example, in the case of DDO 126 the estimated distances range from 3.9 to 5.1 Mpc (Karachentsev et al. 2003), all of these relying upon the same method (TRGB) So one should be cautions in taking the reported errors too seriously, particularly for these nearby objects. For DDO 126 the shape is reasonably well matched but the predicted amplitude is too high. Given the range of estimates for the distance we see that this could be due to an overestimate of the distance to the galaxy. Figure 5 shows the predicted rotation curves for the preferred distance of Oh et al., 4.9 Mpc, (upper solid curve) as well as the lowest value cited by Karachentsev et al., 3.9 Mpc (the lower solid curve). In the case of the shorter estimate the agreement of the MOND prediction with the observed curve is more precise.

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 7559 as an irregular, knotty, apparently disrupted galaxy with only an R-band frame and a nearly exponential profile with a flat center.

quotes.quote: UGC 7559 : irregular with a knotty face. Also seems disrupted, although not as severely as UGC 6817. We only have a R frame. SBP close to an exponential, with a flat central part.

sources.summary: The paper describes DDO 126 as a Canes Venatici I Group dwarf with four H I holes, including one kiloparsec-scale Type 1 hole.

quotes.quote: DDO 126 DDO 126 (UGC 7559, PGC 40791) lies about 4.9 Mpc away from the Milky Way in the Canes Venatici I Group and has a V-band magnitude of 14.9 . The galaxy has one Type 3 hole and three Type 1 holes including one kiloparsec-sized hole. The estimated \ disk radius and the scale height of the galaxy are about 2.9 kpc and 290 pc respectively.

sources.summary: The paper discusses UGC 7559 as a CVn I galaxy observed with WFPC2, describing its stellar population or morphology and deriving a TRGB-based distance.

quotes.quote: The resulting distances are 3.9 Mpc (UGC 6541), 4.9 Mpc (NGC 3738), 3.0 Mpc (NGC 3741), 4.5 Mpc (KK 109), >6.3 Mpc (NGC 4150), 4.2 Mpc (UGC 7298), 4.5 Mpc (NGC 4244), 4.6 Mpc (NGC 4395), 4.9 Mpc (UGC 7559), 4.2 Mpc (NGC 4449), 4.4 Mpc (UGC 7605), 4.6 Mpc (IC 3687), 4.7 Mpc (KK 166), 4.7 Mpc (NGC 4736), 4.2 Mpc (UGC 8308), 4.3 Mpc (UGC 8320), 4.6 Mpc (NGC 5204), and 3.2 Mpc (UGC 8833).

This irregular dwarf galaxy has been resolved into stars by Hopp Schulte-Ladbeck (1995), Georgiev et al. (1997), and Makarova et al. (1998), who derived distance estimates of 4.8 Mpc, 3.9 Mpc, and 5.1 Mpc, respectively. Our distance for UGC 7559 based on the TRGB (4.87 0.55 Mpc) is in the middle of the range previously obtained by other authors.

gmw_00000776.name: Sagittarius Dwarf Irregular SDIG
aliases_external: Sagittarius dwarf irregular galaxy / Sag DIG
gmw_00000776.overview: The Sagittarius Dwarf Irregular SDIG is a highly gas-rich, metal-poor dwarf irregular galaxy located at the outer edge of the Local Group [arXiv:1610.08505, arXiv:astro-ph/0610190]. It features a ring-like, double-peaked neutral hydrogen structure with no ordered rotation, and it hosts an ancient stellar population alongside ongoing star formation [arXiv:astro-ph/0505399, arXiv:1501.06709].

sources.summary: The paper uses near-IR and optical data to characterize SagDIG as a metal-poor Local Group dwarf irregular with many carbon-star candidates and constraints on its star-formation history.

quotes.quote: We detected a large population of C-star candidates in SagDIG, 18 of which were previously identified in Demers Battinelli (2002) and Cook (1987), and six new bona-fide carbon stars. We present their optical and near-IR colors and use their luminosity function to put constraints on the star formation history (SFH) in this dwarf irregular galaxy.

Following this approach we present the first J and K_s photometry of SagDIG and DDO210, two southern Local Group metal-poor dIrr galaxies. Near-IR data are complemented with ground based I band photometry for DDO 210, and V , I ACS/HST photometry by for SagDIG. DDO 210, also known as Aquarius dIrr, is one of the faintest dwarf galaxies lee+1999 , while SagDIG is one of the brightest (see Table t:par ). The number of C-stars is roughly a function of the galaxy mass or absolute magnitude groe2004,battdeme2005 , so the number of expected C-stars in the two galaxies is very different. As a part of a homogeneous survey, found only three C-stars in DDO 210, and found 16 in SagDIG. The aim of this paper is to investigate the AGB stars of SagDIG and DDO 210 and in particular to characterize the near-IR properties of their C-star population. SagDIG Observations were made during August 2003 with the SOFI moor+1998 camera mounted on the NTT telescope at ESO/La Silla Observatory. The camera is equipped with a 1024 1024 Hawaii CCD. In Large Field mode, with a spatial resolution of 0 288 \ pix ^ 1 , the field of view is 4 9 4 9 . Our data set consists of of 24 J frames and 48 K_s frames centered on SagDIG (see Table t:log for a log of the observations).

SagDIG is a much more luminous galaxy which has experienced a prolonged star formation moma+2005 , and consequently it harbors a rich intermediate-age population. Due to high foreground contamination, the bluer oxygen-rich AGB stars and bluer C-rich stars are more easily detectable through the narrow-band filter technique cook1987, demebatt2002 . However, the reddest carbon stars and dust enshrouded AGB stars are easier to pick up through near-IR imaging. We have combined the results from the literature with our near-IR images obtained with SOFI at NTT and optical ACS/HST data. All objects redder than the foreground sequence ( J-K_s>1.1 ) have been carefully inspected and blends or background galaxies were excluded from the final number of C-stars. In the near-IR CMD we identified 27 C-stars candidates in SagDIG.

sources.summary: The paper presents SDIG as a low-mass dwarf whose rotation curve and peak speed imply a halo mass far below the abundance-matching expectation, unless it is an unusual low-concentration outlier.

quotes.quote: The left panel shows UGC 7559, a galaxy with stellar mass M_ gal 1.7 10^7 M_ Swaters1999 ; the panel on the right shows the M_ gal 2.6 10^6 M_ Sagittarius Dwarf Irregular SDIG, Cote2000 . The published rotation curves are shown by the solid symbols with error bars; the smaller symbols connected by a dotted line shows the circular velocity profile once the contribution of the baryons (gas+stars) has been discounted. These two galaxies, like most faint galaxies in our sample, are clearly dark matter dominated in the outer regions.

The same is not the case for SDIG, whose paltry peak rotation speed is just V_ out 19 km/s, well below the V_ out ^P 30 km/s expected at the outermost radius, r_ out =1.3 kpc. This is clear indication that the SDIG halo mass is well below the abundance-matching expectation: a naive fit of the rotation curve yields M_ 200 10^ 9 M_ , a factor of 10 below the mass expected from abundance-matching considerations. Unless the rotation curve measurements are grossly in error, which we deem unlikely, it is difficult to evade the conclusion that SDIG truly inhabits a halo of mass much lower than expected from the model.

Indeed, a M_ 200 =10^ 10 M_ halo with =5 ( 3 below the average) would match the observed ( r_ out , V_ out ) for this galaxy. If this were true, it would mean that SDIG is a rare outlier, a possibility that may be checked by considering the remainder galaxies in our sample.

sources.summary: The paper portrays SagDIG as a highly gas-rich Local Group dwarf irregular with high specific star formation, rapid potential growth, and very low gas-phase metallicity comparable to Leo A.

quotes.quote: SagDIG has the highest gas fraction ( M (H i ) /M_* = ) of any galaxy in the Local Group. The high gas fraction also imparts a high specific SFR to SagDIG . The UV-measured SFR is 7.2 10^ 4 M_ yr ^ 1 kar13 , corresponding to a specific SFR of 0.4 Gyr ^ 1 . That makes SagDIG one of the fastest growing galaxies in the Local Group.

It could have grown to its present stellar mass at its present SFR in just 2.5 Gyr. SagDIG's high specific SFR makes its metallicity very interesting. It has a very low gas-phase metallicity O/H = -1.4 , ski89b,sav02 . That ties SagDIG with Leo A for the distinction of being the most oxygen-poor galaxy in the Local Group, with an oxygen abundance nearly as low as the quintessentially metal-poor galaxy I Zw 18 sea72,ski93 .

sources.summary: The paper obtains multi-epoch HST proper motions for Sag DIG, using two epochs and background galaxies, and finds its wide Milky Way passage leaves it a Local Group dwarf, not a satellite.

quotes.quote: We have also used multi-epoch \ data to obtain more accurate PM estimates for Leo A and Sag DIG. In summary, we used three epochs of data with a total time baseline of 15 years for Leo A, whereas only two epochs were used with a time baseline of 9 years for Sag DIG. To measure the PMs of Leo A and Sag DIG, we closely follow the established methods used by our previous work on M31, Leo I, the Draco/Sculptor dwarf spheroidal galaxies, and NGC 147 and NGC 185 Sohn_2012, Sohn_2013, Sohn_2017, Sohn_2020 .

We identify stars associated with Leo A and Sag DIG via CMDs constructed from multi-band photometry. To construct CMDs, we used the F475W images obtained during the first-epoch observations for Leo A, and F475W images obtained in 2003 observations for Sag DIG. While F814W images were also obtained in 2003 for the Sag DIG field, they were too shallow to be considered as a separate epoch of observation for the PM measurements. We thus identified 180 and 123 background galaxies that are suitable for defining a stationary astrometric reference frame, in the respective Leo A and Sag DIG fields.
For Sag DIG, with only two epochs of observations, the PM calculations were carried out in a straightforward way following those in Sohn_2020 .

sources.summary: The paper describes Sag DIG as a dwarf irregular with extended H I, no clear H I rotation, outer-Local-Group distance, and very metal-poor stars.

quotes.quote: Sag DIG The Sagittarius dwarf irregular (Sag DIG) galaxy was first discovered by and later confirmed by . detected what appeared to be three H II regions; only one appears to be a true H II region, whereas the other two sources are likely stars in \ emission. found that the H I component was more spatially extended than the stellar component. An H I clump of relatively high density is nearly spatially coincident with the H II region. There is no clear rotational motion for the H I gas.

Using resolved stellar photometry, and independently obtained TRGB distances of 1.11 Mpc, which places this dwarf at the outer boundaries of the Local Group and makes the membership to the Local Group uncertain. Photometry has also revealed that the underlying stellar population is very metal-poor with [ Fe/H ] 2 , which was also noted by .

sources.summary: The paper uses HST/ACS color-magnitude diagrams to show SagDIG has ongoing star formation, very low metallicity, an old red horizontal branch, and an ancient stellar population.

quotes.quote: We have obtained deep color-magnitude diagrams (CMDs) of the Sagittarius dwarf irregular galaxy with the Advanced Camera for Surveys (ACS) on board the Hubble Space Telescope . The new diagrams show for the first time the star formation history of SagDIG. The young main-sequence is traced down to 27.5 , well separated from the older He burning blue loop stars. The wide color range spanned by the blue and red super-giants is consistent with Padua evolutionary models with Z 0.0004 . A well-defined red giant branch indicates that the galaxy is dominated by a stellar population older than 1 Gyr, for which we have estimated a metallicity in the range [ Fe/H ] = -2.2 to 1.9 depending on the adopted reddening.

Perhaps most importantly, we have identified a small population of stars on a genuinely old red horizontal branch at =25.70 0.05 , which sets the first epoch of star formation in SagDIG back to 9-10 Gyr ago. This shows that SagDIG has been able to maintain a very low mean metallicity over a 10 Gyr life time, and that all Local Group dwarf galaxies share an ancient ( 10 Gyr) initial episode of star formation .

sources.summary: The paper presents SagDIG as an extremely metal-poor Local Group dwarf irregular and reports first spectroscopy confirming young massive-star candidates in the galaxy.

quotes.quote: The Sagittarius Dwarf Irregular Galaxy (SagDIG, 12+log(O/H)=7.37) represents an important landmark in the quest for analogues accessible with 10-m class telescopes. This paper presents low-resolution spectroscopy executed with the Gran Telescopio Canarias that confirms that SagDIG hosts massive stars. The observations unveiled three OBA--type stars and one red supergiant candidate.

The Sagittarius Dwarf Irregular Galaxy ( aka Sgr dIG, ESO 594-4, UKS 1927-17.7; SagDIG from now on) makes a promising environment to find very metal-poor, resolved massive stars. Its intermediate-age population stands out as one of the most Fe-poor among the Local Group galaxies that sustain star formation e.g. McC12 , with values inferred from the location of the red giant branch (RGB) that range from [ Fe/H ] = 2.0 to 2.45 Becal14,KAM99 . Regarding the youngest populations, the oxygen abundance determined from the only confirmed H ii region SagDIG-HII 3 SHK91 is also remarkably low 12+log(O/H) =7.37 ^ + 0.13 _ 0.11   SIal02 .

sources.summary: Sag DIG is compared to CHVCs as a Local Group dwarf irregular with similar H I clumps, diffuse halo gas, CNM/WNM phase structure, velocity fields, and a roughly 10^7 solar-mass gas and stellar content.

quotes.quote: The comparison with the Local Group dwarf irregulars, Leo A and Sag DIG, studied at high resolution by Young and Lo ( , ) is particularly apt. Both of these objects display the same general morphology and kinematics as the CHVCs: high column density clumps characterized by narrow linewidths (about 8 km s ^ 1 FWHM) and angular sizes of a few arcmin embedded in a diffuse halo of lower column density gas with broader linewidths (about 20 km s ^ 1 FWHM) extending over 10's of arcmin. Young and Lo reach the same conclusion presented here regarding the phase content of the dwarf irregulars, namely that cool condensations of CNM (with T_ k 100 K) are found within WNM envelopes (with T_ k 8000 K).

The velocity fields of both Leo A and Sag DIG are also very similar to those of the CHVCs. Modest velocity gradients of comparable magnitude are observed (about 10 km s ^ 1 over 10 arcmin) along one position angle in addition to a substantial unstructured component. Unlike the CHVCs, these galaxies contain comparable gas and stellar masses, each corresponding to about 10 ^8 M _ in the case of Leo A and about 10 ^7 M _ in Sag DIG.

sources.summary: Sag Dig is described as having ring-like, double-peaked H I with an offcenter depression and no ordered rotation, consistent with a possible dwarf-galaxy merger.

quotes.quote: Sag Dig From Begum et al. (2006; their Fig. 9), with the GMRT, and Becarri et al. (2014; their Fig. 12 and 13), with the VLA. The low resolution ( 67 ) H 1 map shows double-peaked quasi-spherical ring-like emission, with an offcenter H 1 depression ( D 1 ). The brightest H 1 peak roughly coincides with the bright star formation knots to the East, seen on the optical image (PA _ opt 170 ^ ). At intermediate resolution (30 x 30 ), the ring-like structure becomes more apparent, with multiple H 1 clumps and a large offcenter H 1 hole. The velocity field displays no sign of ordered rotation. Becarri et al (2014) hypothesized that this source could be the result of a merger with another dwarf.

sources.summary: SagDIG is treated as a nearby, very metal-poor star-forming dwarf irregular whose observed CMD blue loops require enhanced envelope overshooting, with EO=2 matching best.

quotes.quote: In this paper we continue this investigation by considering the case of SagDIG, a nearby star forming dwarf irregular whose metallicity is estimated to be even lower than that of the aforementioned galaxies, Z=0.0005. This galaxy is an ideal workbench to test the performance of models of intermediate- and high-mass stars because it harbours a recent burst of star formation, and it is sufficiently nearby that intermediate-mass stars with masses as low as M=2 can be detected. The foreground contamination has been eliminated using proper motions of individual stars Momany_etal14 . From a preliminary superposition of the standard PARSEC evolutionary tracks with EO=0.7 and the observed CMD of SagDIG, we already see that the models are not able to reproduce the observed loops.

We find that the model with EO=2 matches the observations best, while the blue loops predicted by the case EO=0.7 are not hot enough and those predicted in the case of EO=4 are likely hotter than the data indicate.

sources.summary: The paper compares Sag DIG with the earlier Solo analysis, finding that improved masking and background treatment favor a more extended surface-brightness profile.

quotes.quote: The Sagittarius Dwarf Irregular Galaxy Sag DIG was studied as the first Solo dwarf in Paper I. We previously noted differences between the HI structure and its stellar structure, similar to DDO 210 (Aquarius). There are faint extended RGB stars regions along the semi major axis of this dwarf. Qualitatively, these extensions are not found to have a HI counterpart in LITTLE THINGS observations Hunter2012 . Sag DIG lies at a low galactic latitude so the foreground contamination from the MW in the field is significant.

sources.summary: The paper models the Sagittarius dwarf as an originally rotating late-type disc, arguing that a misaligned Sgr disc explains stream bifurcation and predicting a surviving 20 km/s core rotation signal.

quotes.quote: We use N-body simulations to explore the possibility that the Sagittarius (Sgr) dwarf galaxy was originally a late-type, rotating disc galaxy, rather than a non-rotating, pressure-supported dwarf spheroidal galaxy, as previously thought. We find that bifurcations in the leading tail of the Sgr stream, similar to those detected by the SDSS survey, naturally arise in models where the Sgr disc is misaligned with respect to the orbital plane. Moreover, we show that the internal rotation of the progenitor may strongly alter the location of the leading tail projected on the sky, and thus affect the constraints on the shape of the Milky Way dark matter halo that may be derived from modelling the Sgr stream. Our models provide a clear, easily-tested prediction: although tidal mass stripping removes a large fraction of the original angular momentum in the progenitor dwarf galaxy, the remnant core should still rotate with a velocity amplitude 20 km s ^ 1 that could be readily detected in future, wide-field kinematic surveys of the Sgr dwarf.

sources.summary: The paper derives a JAGB distance for SagDIG from near-infrared JKs observations of red AGB stars and compares it with four TRGB distances.

quotes.quote: 2010); and they average to < _o (TRGB)> = 22.04 0.04 (stat) mag. Sagittarius Dwarf Irregular Galaxy (SagDIG) JAGB stars: Near-infrared JK_s observations of the Sagittarius Dwarf Irregular Galaxy (Sag DIG) were made by Gullieuszik et al. (2007b) using the SOFI instrument on the NTT at La Silla; the photometry for only 30 color- and magnitude-selected red AGB stars were ultimately published. The CMD based on these data can be seen in Figure fig:CMDmontage , from which we determine a JAGB apparent distance modulus of 24.95 0.06 (stat) mag, derived from only 19 JAGB stars.

sources.summary: The paper treats SagDIG as a faint irregular galaxy with a limited intermediate-age population, identified C-rich AGB stars, and an average age of 4 Gyr at low metallicity.

quotes.quote: A similar work on the Magellanic type galaxy NGC 6822 has recently been submitted ( ), as well as on the faint irregular galaxy SagDIG ( ), while a study of the nearby spiral galaxy M33 is currently in preparation ( ).

Although SagDIG contains a limited population of intermediate-age stars, C-rich AGB stars were carefully identified and their distribution analyzed as for the previous galaxies. Near-infrared observations were, in this case, obtained from the NTT telescope in La Silla. The population of SagDIG appears on average 4 Gyr old and with [ Fe/H ] < -1.3 dex.

sources.summary: The paper uses the Sagittarius dwarf as an active example of dwarf-galaxy disruption, noting its associated globular clusters, central M54 cluster, and eventual assimilation into the Milky Way halo.

quotes.quote: The on-going disruption of the Sagittarius (Sgr) dwarf by the Milky Way is an example of this process in action. There are a number of globular clusters currently associated with this dwarf ( ), and the cluster M54 lies at the dwarf galaxy's centre. When the Sgr accretion process is complete, these clusters will simply become part of the Galaxy's halo globular cluster population and their origin in Sgr will not be easily established.

sources.summary: The paper models the Sgr dwarf as the stream progenitor, adopting GaiaDR2 phase-space coordinates and a 6e8 mass while using its nearly planar orbit to test halo figure rotation.

quotes.quote: The Sgr dwarf progenitor has a mass of 6 10^8 law_majewski_10 , which is lower than some recent estimates 10^ 10 10^ 11 , laporte_etal_18 .

For most of our models we use the present day phase-space coordinates for the Sgr dwarf obtained with GaiaDR2 \ gaiacollab_helmi_18,vasiliev_belokurov_20 ( x=17.2 kpc, y= 2.5 kpc, z=-6.4 kpc, v_x=237.9 , v_y= -24.3 , v_z=209.0 ).

Since the Sgr dwarf and its resultant tidal stream are on an almost planar orbit that lies approximately in the Galactocentric x-z plane, we can simplify the discussion of the expected effects of figure rotation on the appearance of the Sgr stream by considering an orbit that lies exactly in the x-z plane in the stationary potential.

sources.summary: The paper places SagDIG closest to an ancestral stage and at the Local Group edge, while derived dwarf-galaxy groups are well differentiated from it.

quotes.quote: The galaxy SagDIG is the less diversified from an ancestral stage common to all this sample. Interestingly, SagDIG happens to be at the edge of the Local Group.
These three groups are all well differentiated from SagDIG, they all evolved in their own way.

gmw_00000777.name: J0909 + 1928
gmw_00000777.overview: J0909 + 1928 is a radio galaxy classified as a gigahertz-peaked spectrum source with a peak frequency near 5 GHz [arXiv:2111.06141]. It displays a core-twin-jet structure [arXiv:1807.02505] with a sharp parsec-scale jet bend [arXiv:1807.02505] and exhibits up to 25 percent variability [arXiv:2111.06141].

sources.summary: The paper classifies J0909 + 1928 as an FR 0 source with core-twin-jet structure, a sharp parsec-scale jet bend, and core brightness above the equipartition limit.

quotes.quote: J0909 + 1928 displays a sharp jet bending at 5 mas (2.7 pc) south of the core. The jets and counterjets in J0906 + 4124, J1025 + 1022, J1213 + 5044 and J1230 + 4700 are well aligned along the same direction. Two sources J0909+1928 and J1205+2031 show a core-twin-jet structure, similar to CSOs. Their core brightness temperatures are higher than the equipartition limit.

sources.summary: The paper reports that J0909+1928 has a constant quasi-simultaneous spectrum despite 25 percent variability, a GPS classification near 5 GHz, and VLBI core-jet morphology.

quotes.quote: The shape of the quasi-simultaneous spectrum of J0909+1928 is constant, although this source demonstrates variability of up to 25 . It can be classified as a GPS source with a peak frequency close to 5 GHz. Its VLBI image has core-jet morphology at 4.8 GHz 2018ApJ...863..155C .

gmw_00000778.name: J0933 + 1009
gmw_00000778.overview: J0933 + 1009 is a group-central LINER galaxy [arXiv:1801.03456] classified as a core-jet FR 0 radio galaxy [arXiv:1807.02505]. It hosts an active radio core [arXiv:1801.03456] with jet-like extended structures [arXiv:1403.6675] that showed a proper motion of 0.04 +/- 0.07 mas from 2012 to 2015 [arXiv:1807.02505].

sources.summary: The paper detects extended radio emission in NGC 2911, finding bright core emission plus 509 pc and 81 pc jet-like structures at different frequencies.

quotes.quote: The results yield the first resolved radio structure of the pc-scale jet in NGC 4594 (or Sombrero) and the detection of jet-like extended emission in NGC 1097 and NGC 2911.

For NGC 2911 (Figure  ngc2911 ) a bright core of S/N 400 and S/N 40 is detected at 4.9 and 14.9 GHz, respectively. In the 4.9 GHz image (Figure  ngc2911 , top), a component of S/N 9 is observed northwest of the core emission, which is slightly resolved toward the east. The extended structure has a total linear size of 509 pc oriented along a P.A.= 54^ . An extended structure is also observed at 14.9 GHz (Figure  ngc2911 , bottom), though in this case the radio emission extends more to the west (P.A.= 90^ ), where a component of S/N 9 is detected. The total linear size of the 14.9 GHz extended structure is 81 pc.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0933 + 1009 as a core-jet FR 0 with a steep spectral index, possible GPS-like flux discrepancy, low core brightness temperature, and no significant jet motion.

quotes.quote: (3) core and one-sided jet; four sources (J0933 + 1009, J1246 + 1153, J1559 + 2556, and J1606 + 1814) in Fig. 2 show a resolved coherent structure with a compact component appearing at one end (identified as the core) and contributing the most emission, representing a typical core-jet structure. J0933+1009 and J1559+2556, with core-jet morphology, show steep spectral indices, = -1.28 and = -1.12 , respectively. J0933 + 1009 and J0943 + 3614 have a ratio larger than 1.

J0933+1009 and J1559+2556 have brightness temperature of 1 10^ 10 K, and 2 10^ 10 K, respectively, below the equipartition limit. The jet component in J0933 + 1009, at a distance of 1.4 mas from the core, was not found to change in position significantly from 2012 January to 2015 September; the resulting proper motion is 0.04 0.07 from the two-epoch data. Three sources (J0933+1009, J1559+2556, J1606+1814) have multi-epoch 8.4 GHz VLBI data, and we derived their variability factors of <10 for J0933+1009, <20 for J1559+2556 and <15 for J1606+1814 on time scales of several years.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 2911 as a group-central LINER with an active radio core and a 297 mas southeast CLASS offset significant at 4 sigma.

quotes.quote: SDSS J093346.08+100908.9 (NGC 2911; Arp 232) Morphology: SA0(s)? pec, S0 pec, E (NED) Single CLASS detection of 24.1 mJy (NVSS J093346+100909), offset by 297 mas to the south-east of the SDSS position. This galaxy is at the centre of the NGC 2911 group, and a well-known LINER with an active radio core Filippenko1985,Filho2002,Schilizzi1983,Mezcua2014 . This offset deviates from the fitted Rayleigh distribution with 4-sigma confidence.

gmw_00000779.name: J0943 + 3614
gmw_00000779.overview: J0943 + 3614 is a radio galaxy characterized by an unresolved, compact core-type morphology [arXiv:1807.02505, arXiv:2111.06141]. It exhibits an inverted radio spectrum [arXiv:2302.12657] and displays slow flux density variability at 15 GHz [arXiv:1807.02505].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0943 + 3614 as an unresolved-core FR 0 with a flat spectrum, possible GPS-like flux behavior, slow 15 GHz brightening, and usefulness as a VLBI calibrator.

quotes.quote: The 14 sources display three types of morphologies: (1) compact core; three sources (J0910 + 1841, J0943 + 3614 and J1604 + 1744) in Fig. 1 show a single unresolved component, which is identified as the core; (2) core and two-sided jets; the remaining six sources in Fig. 1 show a bright central component straddled between two weaker symmetric features. Indeed the bright, compact component in J0943 + 3614 and J1606 + 1814 have flat spectrum with spectral indices , 0.13 and 0.13. J0933 + 1009 and J0943 + 3614 have a ratio larger than 1.

One of the 14 sources (J0943 + 3614) is monitored at 15 GHz with the OVRO 40 m telescope 2011ApJS..194...29R at intervals of two weeks. From 2008 to 2014, the flux density remains quite stable. After 2014, the source becomes brighter, with the flux density increasing from 0.17 Jy in 2013 August to 0.25 Jy in 2017 April. The maximum variability amplitude is 40 . The compact source structure and slow variability of J0943 + 3614 make it an ideal flux density calibrator in VLBI experiments.

sources.summary: The paper describes J0943+3614 as a stable-spectrum compact radio source with under 10 percent variability, an 8.3 GHz peak, GPS-like spectral indices, and unresolved VLBI core morphology.

quotes.quote: Quasi-simultaneous spectra of J0943+3614 have had constant shape during 14 months of RATAN observations. The variability is not bigger than 10 . According to available data, the peak frequency is 8.3 GHz and the spectral indices correspond to the classical criteria. Thus, J0943+3614 can be classified as an HFP source. We note that the VLBI image of the source has unresolved core-type morphology at 8.3 GHz 2018ApJ...863..155C .

sources.summary: The paper describes J0943+36 as an FR0 radio source, using VLBI/JVLA structure, flux ratios, spectra, and jet sidedness to infer compact parsec-scale radio morphology.

quotes.quote: J0943+36 This source is unresolved in our EVN observations and in VLBA observations at 8 GHz cheng18 . The source flux density is 140 mJy and in the image no dynamic range problem is present. The unresolved source is the only visible structure, also in large field images. The radio spectrum is inverted and it shows evidence of variability. This property and the relatively high radio power suggest a relativistic source oriented at a small angle from the line of sight.

gmw_00000780.name: J1037 + 4335
gmw_00000780.overview: J1037 + 4335 is a passive, low-redshift early-type galaxy [arXiv:2311.15009] that hosts a quasar [arXiv:2105.05396] and is classified as an FR0 radio source [arXiv:2302.12657]. High-resolution observations reveal a compact radio core [arXiv:2105.05396] with a faint, one-sided jet [arXiv:1807.02505, arXiv:2105.05396] and asymmetric, arc- or ring-like structures [arXiv:2311.15009].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1037+4335 as a z=0.025 quasar with a compact low-frequency core, newly resolved one-sided northwest jet, possible artifact SW component, and steep spectrum.

quotes.quote: J1037+4335 The source J1037+4335 is associated with a quasar at redshift z = 0.025 1999PASP..111..438F . The 150-MHz LOFAR image and 1.4-GHz NVSS image only detect a compact core with a flux density of 260.9 mJy and 132.2 mJy 1998AJ....115.1693C,2020A&A...642A.107C . J1037 + 4335 was resolved into two isolated components, separated by 19.7 pc in the previous study 2018ApJ...863..155C . Our high resolution observations first show a faint one-sided jet extending to the north-west (see Fig. fig:VLBI ).

Considering the large distance from the core and almost being perpendicular to the jet, we suggest that the SW component might be an artifact. The total flux density spectrum is steep ( ^ 0.15-1.4 = 0.30, ^ 1.4-5 = 0.56) at low frequencies 2020A&A...642A.107C . The total flux density spectrum is also steep ( ^ 4.87-8.37 _ VLBA = 0.58, ^ 4.99-8.42 _ EVN = 0.72) between 5 and 8 GHz with the quasi-simultaneous two frequency observations, suggesting an emission peak at < 1.4 GHz.

sources.summary: The paper resolves J1037 + 4335 into two separated components, identifies the high-brightness northeast component as a tentative core, and classifies the source as core plus one-sided jet.

quotes.quote: J1037 + 4335 is resolved into two isolated components, separated by 19.7 pc. The emission between the two components is fully resolved. The northeast component has a brightness temperature of 6 10^ 9 K (see Section 3.2), so we tentatively identify it as the core. Therefore the morphology of J1037 + 4335 is also identified as a core and one-sided jet; new VLBI observations at a different frequency confirm it as a flat spectrum core are needed.

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J103719.33+433515.3 as a low-redshift, passive E/S0 galaxy with asymmetric arc- or ring-like structures inconsistent with spiral structure.

quotes.quote: SDSS J103719.33+433515.3 , along with SDSS J105731.17+405646.1 (below), is the lowest redshift galaxy ( z = 0.025 ) in the PSS-CSO sample. It appears as a slightly lopsided but otherwise normal E/SO galaxy on the RGB image. The contrast-enhanced -band image shows a system of arc- or ring-shaped structures that are clearly asymmetric and cannot be interpreted as a spiral structure. SDSS J103719.33+433515.3 is a passive galaxy according to the WHAN diagram, the MBSED, the WISE colour-colour diagram, and the D4000n index.

sources.summary: The paper describes J1037+43 as an FR0 radio source, using VLBI/JVLA structure, flux ratios, spectra, and jet sidedness to infer compact parsec-scale radio morphology.

quotes.quote: J1037+43 This is a one-sided core dominated structure cheng21 . The correlated flux is significantly lower than the NVSS source flux density (the ratio is 0.2), suggesting the presence of a missing structure in VLBI images and/or source variability. The jet-to-counter-jet ratio is > 20.8; assuming that it is due to Doppler relativistic beaming, it requires a velocity larger than 0.54 and an angle smaller than 57 ^ .

gmw_00000781.name: J1205 + 2031
gmw_00000781.overview: J1205 + 2031 is a radio galaxy classified as an FR0 radio source [arXiv:2302.12657] that exhibits a core-twin-jet structure [arXiv:1807.02505]. High-resolution imaging reveals a one-sided jet alongside a diffuse, lobe-like structure to the northeast [arXiv:2302.12657, arXiv:1807.02505].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1205+20 as an FR0 radio source, using VLBI/JVLA structure, flux ratios, spectra, and jet sidedness to infer compact parsec-scale radio morphology.

quotes.quote: J1205+20 In images by cheng18 and cheng21 , this source shows a one-sided jet and a diffuse lobe-like structure on the other side. The correlated flux is 41.2 mJy at 8.4 MHz and the NVSS flux density is 89.9 mJy. The jet-to-counter-jet ratio near the core is 33 (v > 0.6c and < 50 ^ ). The spectral index of the diffuse NE structure is 0.75 or steeper. It could be the remnant of a previous activity and/or a peculiar structure related to the counter-jet interaction with the ISM.

sources.summary: The paper reports a possible counterjet or artifact northeast of J1205 + 2031, classifies it with a core-twin-jet structure, and finds high core brightness temperature.

quotes.quote: A diffuse feature is detected to the northeast of the core in J1205 + 2031; additional observations are required to clarify its physical nature as the counterjet or an artifact. Two sources J0909+1928 and J1205+2031 show a core-twin-jet structure, similar to CSOs. Their core brightness temperatures are higher than the equipartition limit. The high T_ b of the core and the morphology of symmetric outer jets could imply that the jet is initially beamed and has a sharp bending taking place within 0.2 pc.

gmw_00000782.name: J1559 + 2556
gmw_00000782.overview: J1559 + 2556 is a Fanaroff-Riley class 0 radio galaxy [arXiv:1807.02505, arXiv:2302.12657] that exhibits a steep spectral index [arXiv:1807.02505] and a one-sided core-jet morphology [arXiv:1807.02505, arXiv:2302.12657]. The galaxy displays modest radio variability over several years [arXiv:1807.02505] and features a jet with measurable proper motion [arXiv:1807.02505] extending to a size of 3 kiloparsecs [arXiv:2302.12657].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1559 + 2556 as a one-sided core-jet FR 0 with a steep spectral index, partially resolved VLBI emission, low brightness temperature, measurable jet motion, and modest variability.

quotes.quote: (3) core and one-sided jet; four sources (J0933 + 1009, J1246 + 1153, J1559 + 2556, and J1606 + 1814) in Fig. 2 show a resolved coherent structure with a compact component appearing at one end (identified as the core) and contributing the most emission, representing a typical core-jet structure. J0933+1009 and J1559+2556, with core-jet morphology, show steep spectral indices, = -1.28 and = -1.12 , respectively. J1559 + 2556 and J1606 + 1814 show a higher VLA flux density than VLBI, indicating that a fraction of the emission is resolved with the VLBI.

J0933+1009 and J1559+2556 have brightness temperature of 1 10^ 10 K, and 2 10^ 10 K, respectively, below the equipartition limit. The jet component J1 in J1559 + 2556 displays a significant position change of 0.44 mas over a time separation of 2.6 yr, corresponding to a proper motion of 0.49 0.06 . Three sources (J0933+1009, J1559+2556, J1606+1814) have multi-epoch 8.4 GHz VLBI data, and we derived their variability factors of <10 for J0933+1009, <20 for J1559+2556 and <15 for J1606+1814 on time scales of several years.

sources.summary: The paper describes J1559+25 as an FR0 radio source, using VLBI/JVLA structure, flux ratios, spectra, and jet sidedness to infer compact parsec-scale radio morphology.

quotes.quote: J1559+25 This source is one-sided in JVLA images baldi19 with a jet size of 3 kpc; it is unresolved in our EVN data, but one-sided in the 8GHz VLBA image by cheng18 . The parsec- and kiloparsec-scale structure are at the same PA. The jet is transversely resolved in the VLBA image. The jet-to-counter-jet ratio in the VLBA image is higher than 6. The minimum velocity is 0.34c and the maximum orientation angle is 70 ^ .

gmw_00000783.name: J1606 + 1814
gmw_00000783.overview: J1606 + 1814 is a Fanaroff-Riley class 0 radio galaxy featuring a flat-spectrum core and a one-sided jet structure [arXiv:1807.02505]. This jet extends up to 6 parsecs, exhibits relativistic beaming, and displays modest variability over several years [arXiv:1807.02505].

sources.summary: The paper presents J1606 + 1814 as a flat-spectrum one-sided core-jet FR 0 with the longest jet in the sample, high brightness temperature, Doppler boosting, measured jet speeds, and modest variability.

quotes.quote: (3) core and one-sided jet; four sources (J0933 + 1009, J1246 + 1153, J1559 + 2556, and J1606 + 1814) in Fig. 2 show a resolved coherent structure with a compact component appearing at one end (identified as the core) and contributing the most emission, representing a typical core-jet structure. Indeed the bright, compact component in J0943 + 3614 and J1606 + 1814 have flat spectrum with spectral indices , 0.13 and 0.13. The jet extends to a maximal distance of 6 pc in J1606 + 1814.

The flat-spectrum core of J1606+1814 has the highest brightness temperature of 2.1 10^ 11 K. A Doppler boosting factor of 4.2 is inferred from the ratio of T_ b,obs /T_ eq , where T_ eq = 5 10^ 10 K is the equipartition brightness temperature 1994ApJ...426...51R , indicating relativistic beaming of the jet. A total of four jet components are detected in J1606 + 1814. The jet speeds of J1, J2 and J3 are 0.17 0.09 , 0.16 0.11 and 0.23 0.08 , respectively. Three sources (J0933+1009, J1559+2556, J1606+1814) have multi-epoch 8.4 GHz VLBI data, and we derived their variability factors of <10 for J0933+1009, <20 for J1559+2556 and <15 for J1606+1814 on time scales of several years.

gmw_00000784.name: NGC 1042
gmw_00000784.overview: NGC 1042 is a late-type, bulgeless spiral galaxy [arXiv:1603.07928] that hosts a low-luminosity active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1108.1786, arXiv:1603.07928] and exhibits a grand-design spiral pattern with a short bar [arXiv:astro-ph/0406494]. It is likely a member of the NGC 1052 group [arXiv:1902.02807], showing asymmetric H I emission that suggests past tidal interactions [arXiv:1902.02807].

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 1042 as a late-type bulgeless disk galaxy with a low-luminosity AGN, circumnuclear shocked gas ring, inner-spiral-driven inflow, and uncertain distance.

quotes.quote: NGC 1042 is a late type bulgeless disk galaxy which hosts a low luminosity Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN) coincident with a massive nuclear star cluster. In this paper, we present the integral-field-spectroscopy (IFS) studies of this galaxy, based on the data obtained with the Mitchell spectrograph on the 2.7 meter Harlan J. Smith telescope. In the central 100 300 \ pc region of NGC 1042, we find a circumnuclear ring structure of gas with enhanced ionization, which we suggest is mainly induced by shocks. Combining with the harmonic decomposition analysis of the velocity field of the ionized gas, we propose that the shocked gas is the result of gas inflow driven by the inner spiral arms.

NGC 1042 NGC 1042 is a nearby late-type morphological type SAB(rs)cd, deVaucouleurs1991 bulgeless galaxy. The basic properties of this galaxy are listed in Table tbl-1 . The spiral arms in NGC 1042 are composed of two symmetric inner arms and multiple long and continuous outer arms, which are classified as AC \ 9 in the classification of spiral arms in Elmegreen1987 . In the RC3 catalogue deVaucouleurs1991 , NGC 1042 is classified as a weakly barred (SAB) spiral galaxy.

sources.summary: The note argues that NGC 1042 is not a foreground galaxy but a member of the NGC 1052 group, with disturbed H I, uncertain inclination, and a likely larger distance.

quotes.quote: The only plausible candidate is the spiral galaxy NGC 1042; in projection it is near NGC1052-DF2 and NGC1052-DF4, and Trujillo et al. \ suggest its distance is ``within a range of 8--13 Mpc". In this Research Note we show that NGC 1042 is probably not in the foreground of the NGC 1052 group but a member of it. In projection it is near NGC 1052 itself, its radial velocity of cz=1371 \ is close to the central group velocity ( cz 1450 ), and in group catalogs such as that of kourkchi:17 it is listed as a bona fide member.

The blue- and redshifted velocities of NGC 1042 show ordered rotation but the total emission exhibits an asymmetry along the NE-SW axis, in the direction of NGC 1052. As noted in vgork NGC 1042 is the only galaxy in the group with a large gas reservoir, and a likely interpretation of Fig. \ 1 is that NGC 1052 stripped gas from NGC 1042 in a past interaction. The distance of NGC 1042 quoted in Trujillo et al. \ (``8--13 Mpc") is based on the Tully-Fisher relation.

For inclinations i<30 the Tully-Fisher distance to NGC 1042 is D_ TF 18 Mpc. We infer that NGC 1042 is almost certainly a member of the NGC 1052 group, consistent with previous determinations that were based on its radial velocity and location in the sky kourkchi:17 .

sources.summary: The paper re-evaluates NGC1042 distances, attributes Tully-Fisher scatter to inclination uncertainty from its bar and spiral arms, and places it in the nearer 13.5 Mpc projected group.

quotes.quote: The distance to NGC1042 The distance to NGC1042 has been measured using different methods. On one hand, there have been attempts to measure the distance using the reconstruction of the Large Scale Structure along the line of sight of the object. The most accurate measurement, which made a full model of the peculiar velocity to the Galaxy within 80 Mpc and also corrects the peculiar velocity of NGC 1042 provides a distance of 13.2 Mpc parameter d _k^ in Table 5 of 2007A&A...465...71T . On the other hand, the distance to the galaxy has been explored by mean of the Tully-Fisher relation. This last approach is the one producing a larger scatter on the values to the system, ranging from 4 2008ApJ...676..184T to 8 1992ApJS...80..479T Mpc.

The reason for such discrepancies is that the axis ratio and position angle of the galaxy change along the radial distance due to the presence of a bar and the spiral arms of the object (see Fig. fig:ngc1042 ). We argue that the closest structure is formed by NGC1042 and NGC1035 and contains the dwarf galaxies: [ KKS2000 04 ] and NGC1052-DF4.

sources.summary: The paper considers NGC 1042 as the nearer possible DF2 host, estimates its dynamical mass, and finds its tidal radius would be less disruptive than NGC 1052.

quotes.quote: On the contrary, Trujillo2019 and Monelli2019 place the galaxy at 13.5 Mpc, associating it with the spiral galaxy NGC 1042 The revised surface brightness fluctuation estimation in Zonoozi2021 places an upper limit to the distance to of 17 Mpc. .

Monelli2019 finds two groups in the same line of sight, one at 13.5 Mpc, containing NGC 1042, and one at 20 Mpc, containing NGC 1052. They associated to the former (NGC 1042), at a projected separation of 20 5 . Even though the projected physical distance of to both galaxies would be equivalent to 80 kpc, the total mass of NGC 1052 M _ 1052,dyn (<40 kpc) = 4.14 0.71 10^ 11 M _ , Forbes2019 is more than 10 times larger than that of NGC 1042 (M _ 1042,dyn ( <8 kpc) = 3.0^ +3.0 _ 1.5 10^ 10 M _ , Trujillo et al. in prep.). In order to estimate the dynamical mass of NGC 1042 to the same radial distance than NGC 1052 (i.e., 40 kpc), we can assume that the rotational velocity of NGC 1042 is constant. Using this, then the dynamical mass is proportional to the radius. Therefore, the dynamical mass of NGC 1042 (very likely an upper limit due to our assumption) is M _ 1042,dyn (<40 kpc ) = 1.5^ +0.3 _ 0.2 10^ 11 M _ .
The tidal radius produced by NGC 1042 is r_ t ^ 1042 = 148_ 48 ^ +39 arcsec.

sources.summary: The paper detects faint central CO emission in NGC 1042 and finds its face-on nucleus has an unusually high CO(2-1)/CO(1-0) ratio compared with inactive spirals.

quotes.quote: We have studied the molecular gas in the vicinity of low-luminosity active galactic nuclei (AGNs) in three bulge-less spiral galaxies: NGC 1042, NGC 4178, and NGC 4395. The (1-0) and (2-1) transitions of gaseous carbon monoxide (CO) are clearly detected within the central kpc of all three galaxies. In general, the CO emission is faint, as may be expected from their less-than-spectacular star formation activity.

As can be seen in the right panel, the value of r_ 21 appears unusually high in two of the AGN (NGC 1042 and NGC 4395), while it is rather typical in NGC 4178. In this context, it is important to note that both NGC 1042 and NGC 4395 are oriented nearly face on (as is the entire comparison sample), while NGC 4178 is an edge-on spiral (see Fig. fig:mosaic ). The mean r_ 21 in NGC 1042 and NGC 4395 differs from the mean r_ 21 of the comparison sample by 4.75 standard errors, and the probability that both are part of the same parent population is 0.0001.

sources.summary: NGC 1042 is described as a nearby spiral with uncertain association to NGC 1052; similar velocities and distance uncertainties leave its physical connection ambiguous.

quotes.quote: Another giant galaxy in the same field is NGC 1042 -- a spiral galaxy separated by 15 from NGC 1052 to its south-east. There have been various distance estimations placing NGC 1042 between 4 and 20 Mpc 1992ApJS...80..479T,2007A&A...465...71T,2008ApJ...676..184T,2016ApJ...823...85L . A reconstruction of the velocity field of the galaxies in the local universe puts NGC 1042 at a distance of 13 Mpc 2007A&A...465...71T , which would mean that NGC 1052 and NGC 1042 are companions only in projection. However, they have similar velocities with v=1376 km s ^ 1 for NGC 1042 2009yCat.2294....0A and v=1510 km s ^ 1 for NGC 1052 2005MNRAS.356.1440D .

No low surface brightness features have been uncovered around the spiral galaxy NGC 1042, which leaves the association (physical or projected) between NGC 1052 and NGC 1042 ambiguous, though they have similar radial velocities. Their association will only be established once accurate distances to both objects have been measured.

sources.summary: The paper considers NGC1042 as another close-projected galaxy that could be physically linked to [KKS2000] 04, reviewing its redshift, Tully-Fisher distances near 8 Mpc, and a 13.2 Mpc estimate.

quotes.quote: However, these authors do not explore the possibility that the system could be physically linked to the also very close in projection (20 8) NGC1042 (cz=1371 km s ^ 1 ). The distance to NGC1042 has been estimated in the literature following different approaches. Making use of the Tully-Fisher relation, a distance estimation independent of the redshift, several authors have found that the galaxy NGC1042 is located at 8 Mpc (8.4 Mpc 1992ApJS...80..479T ; 7.8 2007A&A...465...71T ; 8 Mpc 2016ApJ...823...85L ). Interestingly, 2007A&A...465...71T obtained a distance of 13.2 Mpc reconstructing the proper motions of the galaxies in the local universe, which includes considering both the peculiar velocities of our Galaxy and NGC1042.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1042 as an outlier with grand-design spiral structure and short bars, yielding low ellipticity bar strength but large disk torque.

quotes.quote: In the same plot, the two galaxies with the largest Q_g for their H band F_ bar values are NGC 1042 and NGC 3319. Both these systems have grand design spiral patterns and short bars. The shortness of the bars appears to drive the low F_ bar values. But the combination of the bars and the strong spiral patterns results in a large disk torque.

sources.summary: The paper reports prominent Balmer emission in NGC 1042, treats it as a young-nucleus case, then adds it to the older-population category because of extinction and population trade-offs.

quotes.quote: Three of the spectra (NGC 1042, NGC 7418, NGC 7424) have prominent Balmer emission lines, which are less broad than the absorption lines.
Thus, both suggest a distinction into two groups of nuclei: those that are 0.1 Gyr (NGC 1042, NGC 1493, NGC 2139, NGC 7418, NGC 7424, NGC 7793) and those that are 1 Gyr (NGC 300, NGC 428, NGC 3423).

The division into 3 old and 6 young nuclei is however still valid in the sense that NGC 300, NGC 428 and NGC 3423 all have less than 3 of their mass in any population younger than 1 Gyr. Additionally, NGC 1042 needs to be added to this category. This is a particularly striking example of the trade-off between extinction and old population.

sources.summary: The paper reports that NGC 1042 shows faint spiral arms but no significant Dragonfly-detected stellar halo, with a non-detection profile reaching the surface-density limit near 5 half-light radii.

quotes.quote: The galaxies show a remarkable variation in their low surface brightness outskirts NGC 1084 has a nearly spherical halo featuring a giant tidal tail, whereas both NGC 1042 and M101 show spiral arms down to very faint surface brightness levels.
NGC 1042 is a non-detection, and its surface density profile reaches 10^ 4 M_ kpc ^ 2 at approximately 5R_ h , causing the minimum extrapolated halo to be underestimated.
At the other extreme, three galaxies (NGC 1042, NGC 3351, and M101) are consistent with having no significant stellar halo, even at the detection limits of Dragonfly.

sources.summary: The paper says NGC 1042 follows average TNG100 matched profiles despite its larger size, but remains at the low end of stellar-halo mass measures.

quotes.quote: Strikingly, the DNGS galaxies appear to be most closely matched by simulated galaxies with lower than average for their stellar mass, with the exception of NGC 1042 and NGC 4258, which follow the average for their mass-matched samples.

We note, however, that these are not the only galaxies to have lower surface densities in the outskirts -- NGC 1084, NGC 2903 and M101 fall into this category as well despite having size-matched galaxies, and NGC 1042 reverses the trend by exhibiting a surface density profile that lies near the median of its mass-matched TNG100 profiles and a size that is towards the upper end of the distribution.

Additionally, we once again find that in spite of having a larger size than most of its mass-matched sample, NGC 1042 is at the low end of the stellar halo mass distribution irrespective of the choice of measurement.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1042 through SDSS surface photometry, noting its visible disk structure and using its radial profile features to classify the galaxy structure.

quotes.quote: NGC 1042 : J024023.9-082558 A large scale gradient (from top to bottom) in the background of the ^ and g^ band image is removed with a linear fit. Only partly fitted since 1/3 of galaxy is beyond SDSS field. Galaxy shows an asymmetric (frayed) extension towards the south-east and a sharper edge towards the north-west, which makes it not really lopsided but looking rather like being affected by moving in a dense IGM.

gmw_00000785.name: NGC1052-DF4
gmw_00000785.overview: NGC1052-DF4 is an ultra-diffuse dwarf galaxy in the NGC1052 group that is notably deficient in dark matter [arXiv:2109.09778, arXiv:2309.08592]. It hosts an unusually luminous population of globular clusters [arXiv:2109.09778, arXiv:1902.02807] and exhibits tidal distortions, suggesting it may have formed from a dwarf galaxy collision [arXiv:2109.09778, arXiv:2205.08552].

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC1052-DF4 as a dark-matter-deficient ultra-diffuse galaxy with luminous globular clusters, stronger outer ellipticity, shared tidal morphology, and evidence against a normal halo.

quotes.quote: The galaxies NGC1052-DF2 and NGC1052-DF4 both have little to no dark matter based on the velocity dispersion of kinetic tracers 2018Natur.555..629V,2018ApJ...863L..15W,2019ApJ...874L...5V,2019ApJ...874L..12D,2019A&A...625A..76E and host a unique population of globular clusters nearly two magnitudes brighter than expected 2018ApJ...856L..30V,2020MNRAS.496.3741M,2021ApJ...909..179S . They are likely associated with the massive elliptical NGC1052 2021ApJ...914L..12S and fall into the category of ultra diffuse galaxies; large, faint objects with the sizes of L_* galaxies but the stellar masses of dwarf galaxies 2015ApJ...798L..45V .

The morphological profiles appear to be similar, though NGC1052-DF4 reaches a higher ellipticity at comparable radii and becomes slightly more twisted compared to its orientation at inner radii. These similar, significant stretches and twists for both galaxies are unlikely to come from identical projection effects, and imply that the galaxies are experiencing, or have experienced, a similar underlying tidal field. There also is a point, again similar for both NGC1052-DF2 and NGC1052-DF4, where the galaxies' surface brightness profiles deviate from S ersic fits. We note that the profiles of the two galaxies do differ, with a S ersic index n_ DF2 = 0.64 for NGC1052-DF2 compared to n_ DF4 = 0.85 for NGC1052-DF4 and an effective (half light) radius R_ e,DF2 = 24 8 compared to R_ e,DF4 = 19 8 .

We find that, if NGC1052-DF4 had a `normal' dark matter content, it would need to be closer to NGC1052 than it appears even in projection on the sky to have the observed r_ break (or any r_ break 7.5 kpc for that matter). This strongly supports the conclusion that NGC1052-DF4 is dark matter deficient.

sources.summary: The paper compares DF4 with DF2 and DF9 as a low-dark-matter NGC 1052-group dwarf, emphasizing bullet-trail membership, stellar-population measurements, and similar ages and metallicities.

quotes.quote: We compare these properties to those of two other galaxies in the group, NGC 1052-DF2 and NGC 1052-DF4, which have low dark matter content. The three galaxies are proposed constituents of a trail of galaxies recently hypothesised to have formed as part of a ``bullet dwarf'' collision. We show that the ages and total metallicities of the three galaxies are within uncertainties of one another which may be expected if they share a related formation pathway.

The trail galaxies DF2 and DF4 have already been tested for each of these three requirements. Both have been found to be dark matter deficient vanDokkum2018, vanDokkum2019, Danieli2019, Emsellem2019, Montes2020, Keim2022 . Additionally, DF2 and DF4 have had their stellar population parameters (i.e., age and metallicity) measured from either spectroscopy (DF2; Fensch2018, RuizLara2019 ) or spectral energy distribution (SED) fitting (DF2/DF4; Buzzo2022 ). The ages and metallicities for both galaxies are within uncertainties of one another Buzzo2022 . Finally, both DF2 and DF4 have measured recessional velocities Danieli2019, vanDokkum2019 that place initial constraints on the geometry of the collision (vD+22).

sources.summary: The paper verifies NGC1052-DF4’s very low velocity dispersion using deep KCWI stellar spectroscopy, ruling out a normal NFW halo and confirming anomalously low dark matter content.

quotes.quote: NGC1052-DF4 was found to be the second ``galaxy lacking dark matter'' in the NGC1052 group, based on its velocity dispersion of _ gc =4.2^ +4.4 _ 2.2 \ as measured from the radial velocities of seven of its globular clusters. Here we verify this result by measuring the stellar velocity dispersion of the galaxy. We observed the diffuse stellar light in NGC1052-DF4 with the Keck Cosmic Web Imager (KCWI) in its highest resolution mode, with _ instr 7 .

We find a stellar velocity dispersion of _ stars = 8.0^ +2.3 _ 1.9 , consistent with the previous measurement from the globular clusters. Combining both measurements gives a fiducial dispersion of _ f = 6.3_ 1.6 ^ +2.5 . The implied dynamical mass within the half-light radius is 8_ 4 ^ +6 10^7 M_ . The expected velocity dispersion of NGC1052-DF4 from the stellar mass alone is 7 1 , and for an NFW halo that follows the stellar mass -- halo mass relation and the halo mass -- concentration relation, the expectation is 30 . The low velocity dispersion rules out a normal NFW dark matter halo, and we confirm that NGC1052-DF4 is one of at least two galaxies in the NGC1052 group that have an anomalously low dark matter content.

sources.summary: The paper discusses NGC1052-DF4 as a dark-matter-deficient galaxy likely at the same distance as NGC1052-DF2, making a foreground NGC 1042 satellite interpretation necessary for the short-distance scenario.

quotes.quote: The galaxies NGC1052-DF2 vd18a and NGC1052-DF4 vd19 have received considerable attention, as their kinematics indicate that they have little or no dark matter. Both galaxies are also unusually large for their mass, and both host a population of unusually luminous globular clusters. Most of the properties of the galaxies are distance-dependent. The distances were measured to be 19-20 Mpc cohen:18 , consistent with that of the nearby elliptical galaxy NGC 1052 and with their radial velocities ( cz=1804 \ for NGC1052-DF2 and cz=1445 \ for NGC1052-DF4).

NGC1052-DF2 and NGC1052-DF4 are likely at the same distance, but their velocity difference of 360 \ rules out the possibility that they are an isolated bound pair. Therefore, the short distance solution only ``works" if they are satellites of a massive galaxy in the foreground of the NGC 1052 group. The only plausible candidate is the spiral galaxy NGC 1042; in projection it is near NGC1052-DF2 and NGC1052-DF4, and Trujillo et al. \ suggest its distance is ``within a range of 8--13 Mpc".

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1052-DF4 as a second low-surface-brightness, dark-matter-free candidate with unusual globular clusters and plausible recent interaction-driven stripping.

quotes.quote: In a subsequent paper, a second DM-free galaxy was reported. NGC 1052-DF4 is a low surface brightness galaxy in the same group, identified by vandokkum19 . The authors infer a total enclosed mass within 7 kpc of 0.4 ^ +1.2 _ 0.3 10 ^8 M _ , and a total stellar mass of (1.5 0.4) 10 ^8 M _ within the same enclosed radius. They conclude that this galaxy is consistent with having no DM. As with NGC 1052-DF2, this galaxy hosts an unusually bright population of GCs, but more extended than NGC 1052-DF2.

Hence it is entirely feasible, but by no means guaranteed, that NGC 1052-DF4 very recently had a close interaction with NGC 1035. We conclude that the proposed scenario for stripping both NGC 1052-DF2 and NGC 1052-DF4 of their DM, namely a recent close interaction with a nearby more massive galaxy, is plausible.

sources.summary: DF4 is discussed as an unusually low-dispersion UDG with little dark matter, challenging cuspy tidal-remnant models unless non-tidal origins, numerical limits, or cored halos are invoked.

quotes.quote: Published studies report a wide range of velocity dispersions for UDGs, from _ los 4 km/s in objects like DF2 and DF4 so low that it may imply little to no dark matter, vanDokkum2018a,vanDokkum2019,Danieli2019 to as high as 50 km/s for the globular cluster population ( 33 km/s for the stellar dispersion at R_ eff ) of DF44, which suggests a fairly massive dark matter halo vanDokkum2016, vanDokkum2019_DF44 .

Much lower velocity dispersions, such as those reported for DF4, seem, at first glance, difficult to reproduce in our simulations, where galaxies form in ``cuspy'', NFW-like halos Bose2019 . If confirmed, accommodating the lower limits of the measured kinematics for DF2 and DF4 would be rather challenging in our modeling. Indeed, few, if any tidal tracks originating in the undisturbed galaxy population would leave a remnant with such low velocity dispersion. Several interpretations are possible for this result. One is that this may indicate a non-tidal origin for these extreme objects; i.e., DF2 and DF4 may simply form in ways and/or halos with properties not reproduced in our simulations. A more conservative interpretation, however, is that the failure to reproduce systems like DF2 and DF4 is a result of numerical limitations in the simulations, which prevent us from including the most extremely tidally stripped systems in our sample see for a similar conclusion Carleton2019 .

Finally, the extreme velocity dispersions of DF2 and DF4 may indicate the presence of a ``core'' in the initial inner density profile of their surrounding halos. Tidal tracks in the presence of a core differ in shape, as indicated by the dashed green curve in Fig. fig:udgs_sigma , which uses the results of the ``cored'' halo progenitor in the simulations of Errani2015 . A core leads to much lower velocity dispersions for extreme tidal remnants, allowing better agreement with DF2 and DF4.

sources.summary: The paper argues that DF4 jointly formed with DF2 in a bullet-dwarf collision, remained bound to NGC1052, shares tidal distortions, and lies on the same dark-matter-deficient galaxy trail.

quotes.quote: The situation at that point, a 300 \ encounter between two galaxies in the gas-rich environment of a young group, is a close match to the initial conditions of mini-bullet-cluster scenarios that have previously been proposed for the formation of DF2 and DF4. In a near head-on collision between two gas-rich galaxies, the collisional gas can be shocked and separated from the collisionless dark matter and pre-existing stars. The accompanying star formation favours massive clumps in highly compressed gas, producing the unusual globular clusters as well as the lack of dark matter. Galaxies that form this way are initially compact, in apparent conflict with the large half-light radii of DF2 and DF4. However, intense feedback accompanying the formation of the globular clusters is expected to increase the sizes of the newly formed galaxies, an effect that is particularly efficient when the stars are not bound by a dark matter halo.

The collision produced DF2 and DF4, with DF2's velocity and orbit similar to progenitor 1 and DF4's similar to progenitor 2. DF2 is currently unbound and about 3 Mpc behind the group, whereas DF4 remained bound and has begun falling back.

It is also consistent with the near-identical tidal distortions of the two galaxies: in the geometry of Fig. \ geometry.fig b, DF2 and DF4 were at the same distance from NGC1052 when they were formed, and as neither galaxy experienced a stronger tidal field afterwards, their morphologies have remained the same.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC1052-DF4 as an ultra-diffuse galaxy reportedly lacking dark matter, using its baryon-dominated dynamics, MOND interpretations, and distance uncertainty as caveats for universal RAR claims.

quotes.quote: Nevertheless, recent discovery of ultra-diffused galaxies lacking dark matter (NGC1052-DF2 and NGC1052-DF4) Dokkum,Dokkum2 and dark matter-dominated galaxy Dragonfly 44 Dokkum3 may potentially challenge the alternative theories of gravity. Most of the modified theories predict a larger dynamical mass while observations indicate that baryonic mass can nearly fully account for the dynamical mass Dokkum,Dokkum2 . This result reveals that the canonical stellar mass - halo mass relation Moster or the RAR may not be universal.

However, some recent studies point out that the discovery of the dark matter-lacking galaxies and the Dragonfly 44 could be consistent with the theory of MOND Kroupa,Famaey2,Haghi,Haghi2 . Besides, the uncertainties of the distance of these dark-matter-lacking galaxies also make this issue more controversial Trujillo,Monelli,Haslbauer . If these galaxies lie at about 10 Mpc, then their stellar masses go down significantly and the ratio between dynamical and stellar mass increases such that these galaxies would become normal dwarf galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper discusses NGC 1052-DF4 as a DF2-like dark-matter-deficient galaxy whose distance, globular-cluster normality, and possible tidal-remnant interpretation are debated.

quotes.quote: To add to the puzzle, in 2019 a second galaxy in the same field, NGC 1052-DF4 (hereafter DF4), was found to have properties that are remarkably similar to DF2, including an inferred DM deficiency vanDokkum2019 . The claim for DM deficiency depends critically on the distances to DF2 and DF4. In two studies, and claim that the distances to DF2 and DF4 are 13-14 , significantly less than the distance of 20 advocated by and . If true, this would make DF2 and DF4 far more ordinary, similar to nearby dwarf galaxies, with no notable DM deficiency, and with normal GC luminosity functions. However, recent analyses of the tip of the red giant branch, based on deep Hubble Space Telescope photometry by and , seem to confirm the larger distances, and thus the deficiency of DM in both DF2 and DF4.
Another alternative formation scenario for DMDGs was proposed by and , who argue that DF2 and DF4 might be extreme examples of tidal remnants.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC1052-DF4 as a second dark-matter-deficient candidate in the NGC1052 group, possibly sharing a DM-free trail with NGC1052-DF2, and uses such observations to motivate TNG satellite analysis.

quotes.quote: By measuring the radial velocities of 10 luminous globular-cluster-like objects in the ultra-diffuse galaxy NGC1052-DF2, claimed the first detection of a galaxy only composed of baryonic matter. Soon after, a second candidate was discovered within the same galaxy group (NGC1052-DF4, vD2019_noDM2 ).
