PKS 1510-089 underwent a very active period in 2011 reaching its historical maximum flux density in October 2011. The gamma-ray flare in July 2011 occurred after a rotation of 380 degree of the optical EVPA suggesting a common region for the optical and gamma-ray emission. The new jet component is likely evidence of a shock propagating downstream along the jet.

sources.summary: H.E.S.S. detected PKS 1510--089 as a steep-spectrum flat-spectrum radio quasar; the detection challenges simple expectations of intrinsic VHE absorption near luminous quasar engines.

quotes.quote: Finally, the VHE detection of the flat-spectrum radio quasar PKS 1510--089 ( z=0.361 ) has been presented in . The source was detected at the significance level of 9.2 after 16 h exposure in 2009, with very steep spectrum _ VHE = 5.4 0.7_ stat 0.3_ sys , and F( _ >0.15 TeV ) = 1.0 10^ 11 cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 . The relevance of the discovery follows from the fact that in the case of quasars, which unlike BL Lac objects accrete at high rates and are therefore characterized by a very intense circumnuclear infrared--to--X-ray photon field, VHE ray photons produced close to the central engine, are in principle expected to be intrinsically absorbed via the photon-photon annihilation (see the discussion in ). Therefore, the H.E.S.S. detection of PKS 1510--089, along with the previous MAGIC discoveries of the rapid VHE flares in the two other flat-spectrum radio quasar 3C 279 and PKS 1222+21 , indicate that our current view on the location and the structure of the ray emission zone in luminous blazars may not be accurate. We note that even though the H.E.S.S. data provided insufficient evidence for the VHE variability in PKS 1510--089, exceptionally rapid ray flux changes in the source have been recently reported in the HE range (doubling timescale <1 h; ).

sources.summary: The paper notes PKS 1510-089 has nearly uniform light-curve binning, reports a usable long-timescale posterior, cites prior sub-hour variability, and finds shallower slopes than NM13.

quotes.quote: It can be seen that the time bin distribution in some light curves (PKS 1510-089, B2 1520+31 and Mrk 421) reseambles closely a uniform distribution. It can be inferred that while formally we can derive the median long and short characteristic time scales and their 90 confidence intervals in all sources, they may depend on our choice of _ min and _ max .The least deviated shapes are those representing the long characteristic time scale in PKS 1510-089; and the short characteristic time scales in 3C 454.3, and (to a lesser degree) in 3C 66A, PKS 0716+714, and BL Lac. In five cases with the posterior probability distributions resembling most closely a Gaussian distribution ( _ S in 3C 454.3, 3C 66A, PKS 0716+714, and BL Lac; _ L in PKS 1510-089) we provided additionally the median values and their uncertainties. Sub-hour variability time scales have been reported previously in another FSRQ, PKS 1510-089, in the context of the ray flux doubling/halving time scales, rather than the characteristic time scales, in the study of the ray flares in this source (Saito et al. In 3C 454.3, PKS 1510-089, 3C 273, BL Lac and Mkn 421 our slopes were shallower than those derived by NM13.

sources.summary: The paper uses RXTE and VLBA observations of PKS1510-089, selecting the second radio epoch and estimating an inverse Compton Doppler factor of 14.7 versus the previous 26.26.

quotes.quote: For PKS1510-089 the X-ray observations were conducted by the Rossi X-Ray Timing Explorer (RXTE) between mid December 2007 and early August 2009. Radio observations were conducted by the VLBA in two epochs. The first epoch was between 06/07/08 and 21/12/08, while the second was between 01/04/09 and 16/08/09 Marscher2010 . We used data from the second epoch.

In the case of PKS1510-089 Marscher2010 , our estimate for the Doppler factor is 14.7 whereas the previous value was 26.26, and for the fourth source, PKS1633+382 Jorstad2011 , the Doppler factor is equal to 24.7 whereas the previous value was 3.52.

sources.summary: The paper reports two previous VHE flares from PKS 1510-089 and notes VERITAS observations near both flares, including nightly coverage of the 2012 event.

quotes.quote: PKS 1510-089 (FSRQ, z=0.361 ) Two VHE flares from this quasar have been reported so far: the first during March-April 2009, seen by \ around 0.6 of the Crab Nebula flux above 260 GeV, see 1510hess , the second during February 2012, seen by \ around 1 of the Crab Nebula flux above 260 GeV, see 1510Magic . The \ observations presented in this paper are quasi-simultaneous with both flares (see Table knownVHElist for details). For the 2009 flare, \ observations were taken a few days before the VHE flare seen by . For the 2012 flare, \ observations were taken every night from February 19 to February 27, covering the \ flare. The \ upper limit is 2.9 of the Crab Nebula flux above 260 GeV.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 1510 089 as a highly polarized FSRQ with emission from radio to gamma rays, extensive optical variability, polarization changes, and a detected extended bent jet.

quotes.quote: PKS 1510 089: at a redshift z=0.36 0.002 Thompson90 , is classified as a highly polarized FSRQ VeronCetty01,DAmmando09 . Its emission spans from radio wavelengths to gamma-rays, including optical and X-ray bands Kataoka08,Cusumano10 . This quasar has an extensive history of long and short-term variability at optical bands see, for example, Liller75, Pica88, Villata97, Romero02, Cellone07 , with continuous changes in brightness over long periods of time. Moreover, optical polarization has been detected with variability both in P and in position angle Andruchow05 , as well as a rapid variation in rays DAmmando09 . Simultaneously with this ray flare, these authors also found intense variability at optical and radio wavelengths.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 1510-089 as a ray-bright FSRQ/LSP with optical and high-energy flares, VHE detections, and EVPA rotations accompanying major activity episodes.

quotes.quote: PKS 1510-089 PKS 1510-089 is a ray bright acciari2009 FSRQ/LSP at z=0.36 burbidge1966 . The source has shown bright flares in optical, radio, x-ray and HE rays at the beginning of 2009 (MJD 54910-54920), along with the first detection of VHE rays 2013AandA...554A.107H . During the ray flaring from MJD 54950 - MJD 55000 (April 2009 onwards) the optical electric vector position angle (EVPA) was reported to rotate by 720 ^ and during the major optical flare the optical polarisation degree increased to 30 marscher2010 . In early 2012 (MJD 55960 and onwards) it again showed high activity in HE rays and was also detected in VHE rays. Again there was a 180 ^ rotation of the EVPA following this flare but the polarisation degree stayed low 2 aleksic2014 .

sources.summary: The paper uses PKS 1510 089 as the successful Ring of Fire case, where an orphan gamma-ray flare and stacked radio map support a jet sheath around the blazar spine.

quotes.quote: This model is able to successfully reproduce a prominent orphan ray flare observed within the blazar PKS 1510 089 (see marscher10 ). 0.87 In order to verify the existence of a sheath of plasma surrounding the spine of the jet in PKS 1510 089, implemented a method of radio map stacking (in Stokes I, Q, and U) in order to detect a tenuous polarimetric signal produced by the jet sheath, which is inherently less luminous than the highly Doppler beamed spine. As discussed in , if the jet sheath represents a ``shear layer'' between the relativistic jet and the environment into which the jet expands, one would expect the shear between the jet and the surrounding environment to ``stretch out'' the jet's initially helical (or tangled) magnetic field and align it parallel to the jet axis. The observational characteristic of a shear layer would then be an increase in the observed fractional linear polarization towards the edges of the jet, with the orientation of that polarization being indicative of magnetic field that is aligned parallel to the jet axis. The orientation of polarized radio emission is delineated by the electric vector position angle (EVPA). The EVPA angle ( ) within a given pixel is computed based on the values of Stokes Q and U within that pixel: = 1 2 arctan ( U/Q ) . Neglecting the influence of relativistic aberration and Faraday rotation ( lyutikov05 ), the EVPA should be aligned predominantly perpendicular to the projection of the magnetic field onto the plane of the sky. Therefore, increased polarization towards the edges of the jet, with EVPA aligned roughly perpendicular to the jet axis, can be interpreted as a polarimetric footprint of a jet sheath. This signal was detected in the stacked radio map of PKS 1510 089.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes eight years of Fermi-LAT data for the highly variable flat-spectrum radio quasar PKS 1510-089, identifying major flares, hour-scale variability, and curved gamma-ray spectra.

quotes.quote: We have analyzed data from the Flat Spectrum Radio Quasar PKS 1510-089 collected over a period of 8 years from August 2008 to December 2016 with the Fermi-LAT. We have identified several flares of this highly variable source, studied their temporal and spectral properties in detail and compared with previous works on flares of PKS 1510-089. Five major flares and few sub-flares/sub-structures have been identified in our study. The fastest variability time is found to be 1.30 0.18 hr between MJD 55852.063 and 55852.188 where we estimate the minimum size of the emission region to be 4.85 10^ 15 cm. In most of the flares the spectral energy distributions are better fitted with Logparabolic distribution compared to simple Power law or Power law with exponential cut-offs.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 1510-089 as an FSRQ with a 2016 giant flare, VHE detection, tens-of-minutes variability, and constraints on its emission site.

quotes.quote: Lastly, observations by both and MAGIC of giant flare from another FSRQ, namely PKS 1510-089, which underwent an massive outburst in May 2016, collectively provided exceptional temporal coverage of the flaring event at VHE. For all three of these FSRQ flares, the detection of VHE emission from them during their outbursting episodes presents new surprising and unexpected challenges for their modeling. Specifically, the presence of the broad line region (BLR), an intense radiation field in the vicinity of the AGN, presents a barrier for the escape of VHE emission from within it. In turn, the detection of VHE emission from these sources can be used to place strong constraints on the position of the emission site with respect to the BLR location . With gamma-ray emission beyond 200 GeV detected from each of these systems during their outbursts, the emission site is found to be constrained to sit at a distance beyond r_ BLR 10^ 17 cm L_ disk /10^ 45 erg s ^ 1 ^ 1/2 , where L_ disk is the thermal luminosity of the accretion disk. Information about the spatial size of the emission site, from where the outburst originates, is also provided by the minimum temporal variability time-scale in the observed lightcurve. During their recent outbursts, unexpectedly short time-scale variability have been revealed for both objects. Specifically, for 3C 279, minute time-scale structure in the > 100 MeV HE lightcurves was discovered . Likewise, at VHE, for PKS 1510-089 tens of minute time-scale structure. Together, both the lack of internal absorption features in the flaring FSRQ spectra, and the short variability time-scales observed during the flare, make for rather challenging constraints.

sources.summary: The paper highlights PKS 1510-08 as a VHE-detected FSRQ whose LAT spectra show no BLR cut-off, placing the dominant gamma-ray region beyond the BLR.

quotes.quote: A particularly interesting case is represented by the FSRQ 4C +21.35 and PKS 1510-08. Their detection at VHE by MAGIC magic4c and H.E.S.S. hess1510 provided evidence for an emitting region beyond the BLR, also in objects with strong BLR and IR emission. This is confirmed by the LAT data, with spectra in the high state extending to 80 GeV rest-frame with no signs of cut-off (see Fig. tevs ). Remarkably, we find that also during the low states the LAT spectrum extends to very high energies -- almost 100 GeV -- with no sign of BLR absorption. This means that for both 4C +21.35 and PKS 1510-08, the dominant gamma-ray emitting region is always located beyond the BLR, and not only during the high, VHE-bright and fast-flaring episodes.

sources.summary: The paper follows blazar PKS 1510-089 during renewed activity and finds its optical polarization dropped strongly over several days while the polarization angle changed only modestly.

quotes.quote: PKS 1510-089 PKS 1510-089 is a well known Blazar that periodically enters outburst producing very high energy emission (for examples see Wu2005,DAmmando2011 ). Previous optical polarimetry campaigns have shown that the polarisation of the source varies significantly over time-scales of few days Marscher2010 . We observed the source three times during our observing runs in response to reports of increased activity both in the NIR and at high energies DeNaurois2016,Mirzoyan2016,Carrasco2016i . We observed the source on 2016 June 19, 20 and 22 and measured decreasing polarisation levels of P = 8.76( 0.16) , P = 3.14( 0.16) and P = 1.94( 0.35) and polarisation angles of =155.77( 0.54) deg, =138.55( 1.49) deg and =142.03( 5.08) deg in V band. We calculate E(B-V) = 0.09 at the source position corresponding to P_ Gal, dust 0.81 . Our results confirm that the level of polarisation of PKS 1510-089 varies significantly on short time-scales, with only a small change of polarisation angle measured.

sources.summary: The paper describes H.E.S.S. and MAGIC monitoring of PKS 1510-089, establishing low-state VHE detection, variability, and persistent emission that likely occurs outside the BLR.

quotes.quote: This has led to the establishment of monitoring programs by the IACT experiments H.E.S.S. and MAGIC on the FSRQ PKS 1510-089, which is one of the closest of this type of blazars (redshift =0.361 ). These programs are supplemented with multiwavelength data in the HE ray, X-ray and optical regime. The first important result obtained by MAGIC is that PKS 1510-089 can be detected at VHE rays during low states in the HE band . Similarly, variability has also been established through MAGIC observations . The H.E.S.S. monitoring described in detail here adds important features to these earlier results.

sources.summary: The paper reports that PKS 1510-089 matches IC/CMB gamma-ray predictions but notes the case is weaker because a variable core low state could mimic the minimum flux.

quotes.quote: Here we report the detection of a low-state Fermi/LAT gamma-ray spectrum associated with two jetted AGN which is consistent with the predictions of the IC/CMB model for their X-ray emission. We have used archival multiwavelength observations to make precise predictions for the expected minimum flux in the GeV band, assuming that the X-ray emission from the kpc-scale jet is entirely due to the IC/CMB process. In both sources -- OJ 287 and PKS 1510-089 -- the minimum-detected gamma-ray flux level agrees with predictions. Both sources exhibit extreme superluminal proper motions relative to their jet power, which argues for the well-aligned jets required by the IC/CMB model. In the case of PKS 1510-089, it cannot be ruled out that the minimum gamma-ray flux level is due to a low state of the variable core which only matches the IC/CMB prediction by chance.

sources.summary: The paper models PKS 1510-089 as a flaring FSRQ at z=0.361, using MAGIC data to 300 GeV, disk luminosity, torus geometry, and an adopted SED shape.

quotes.quote: PKS 1510-089 - It is a FSRQ observed at redshift z=0.361 . We use the observational data points from MAGIC PKS1510 which show energies up to 300 GeV . The disk luminosity is estimated to be L_ disk 6.7 10^ 45 erg s^ 1 PKS1510LUM , from which, having fixed f_ cov =0.6 , we can derive the geometry of the torus as a function of the temperature T only. In particular, r_ torus,in directly reads from Eq. ( t1 ), as mentioned above r_ torus,out =2 r_ torus,in , h is obtained from Eq. ( t2 ) and r_ BLR from Eq. ( t4 ). In order to obtain the SED we take E_0 = 100 GeV and k = 3.0 in Eq. ( int ).

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 1510 089 as a bright, highly variable FSRQ detected at HE and VHE energies, with campaigns finding correlated optical/gamma variability and rapid HE variability.

quotes.quote: PKS 1510 089 PKS 1510 089 ( =15^ h 12^ m 50 53, =- 09 05 59 83, J2000 epoch) is a bright and highly variable FSRQ located at z=0.361 Burbidge66,Thompson90 , with a central BH mass of 1.6 10^ 8 M_ Lian03 . The blazar was detected in HE rays by EGRET Hartman99 , and then in VHE regime by the High Energy Stereoscopic System HESS13 . Several multiwavelength observational campaigns were conducted to study its variability and to search for correlations between different energy bands. Abdo10b reported a complex variability in rays and optical/UV bands, showing a strong correlation of rays and optical fluxes. Foschini13 notified the shortest flux variability ever detected in HE, with the time scale of 20 minutes.

sources.summary: The paper finds hour-scale PKS 1510-089 variability compatible with BLR-sized regions, while spectral and cooling-lag evidence points to both BLR and MT emission.

quotes.quote: PKS 1510-089 The November 2011 flare for PKS 1510-089 was found to have a fastest variability timescale of 1.79 0.27 hours while the February 2012 flare has a shortest timescale of 1.39 0.41 hours. Assuming the entire width of the jet to be responsible for the emission, this would indicate emission from within the BLR. This possibility is supported by the spectrum of the February 2012 flare favouring a log parabolic model over a power law, although the November 2011 flare favours neither model significantly. An investigation into the energy dependence of the cooling timescales shows evidence that both flares exhibit a positive time-lag between the high- and low-energy emission. Under the assumption that the flux increase in both energy bands occurs simultaneously, this in turn indicates emission from the MT.

sources.summary: The paper reports that 1510 089 has MOJAVE monitoring, a one-sided parsec-scale jet recovered by TANAMI, high apparent speeds, and flare-linked jet components.

quotes.quote: 1510 089: 1510 089 has been part of the MOJAVE program since 1995 , but observations were dropped in 2013 , and have been re-taken recently in 2021 . On parsec scales the jet shows a one-sided structure, which we also recover in our 2.3 GHz images. The MOJAVE kinematic analysis lister19 shows maximum apparent speeds up to 28.0 0.6 , which suggests a highly beamed emission liodakis17 . Multi-frequency radio observations during high states in 2015-2017 reveal the emergence of two new jet components nearly simultaneously to the high-energy flares, and that during one of the flares the jet showed a limb-brightened linear polarization structure in the core region 2019ApJ...877..106P . The authors conclude that the passing of the new components through stationary shocks gives rise to the ray emission, and that the jet shows a complex transverse velocity structure that changes with time 2015ApJ...804..111M, 2017Galax...5...67C .

sources.summary: The paper reports low-amplitude short-timescale X-ray variability in 1510 089 and includes it among quasars requiring a soft-excess spectral component.

quotes.quote: The blazar 1510 089 showed a factor of 3.5 variation in flux on a timescale 2 years, between survey and pointed observations with ROSAT (Siebert et al. 1996). The blazar 1641+399 showed a factor of 2 variation also on a timescale 2 years (Sambruna 1997). Here we examine whether short-term variability is also present within our Chandra exposures. Table 3 reports the results of the ^2 test applied to the 500s-bins Chandra light curves of the core, and the probability that the light curve is constant ( ^2 probability). No high-amplitude variability is present based on this test. Only 1510 089 has a large enough reduced ^2 value, _r^ 2 =2.63, to indicate possible variability.

We also investigated whether the flux variability in 1510 089 is energy-dependent. Light curves binned at 750 s were accumulated in a soft (0.3--1 keV) and a hard (2--8 keV) energy band, and the ^2 and KS tests were applied. At soft X-rays we find a 92 probability of variation in the light curve, and at hard X-rays we find a 96 probability of variation. We conclude that variability is present at both soft and hard X-rays in 1510 089. In conclusion, none of the objects decisively demonstrated short-term, high-amplitude variability in our Chandra exposures. The only exception is 1510 089 which showed a low-amplitude flux change within a timescale of 25 minutes.

sources.summary: The paper uses PKS 1510-089 as an observed example where gamma-ray flares coincide with radio total-flux, polarization, and EVPA behavior suggestive of a shock event.

quotes.quote: Figure fig1 shows the UMRAO monitoring data for the QSO PKS 1510-089. A series of ray flares occurred during the time period shown which have been analyzed in detail in abd10a and in mar10 . A particularly large and isolated event was identified with start date 10 March, 2009 and stop date 9 April, 2009 abd10a . The UMRAO light curves reveal that a radio band total flux density flare commenced in early 2009 at which time both a resolved linear polarization flare and an ordered swing in EVPA are apparent at 14.5 and 8.0 GHz.

sources.summary: The paper says PKS 1510-089's optical data are host-contaminated, its optical-UV emission is thermal, and its blob environment makes EC cooling dominant.

quotes.quote: We believe that the bad reproduction at optical band of PKS 1510-089 is caused by the fact that the observed optical data are contaminated by the emission from its host galaxy.

It should be noted that the thermal emission of PKS 1510-089, 3C 273 and B2 1520+31 are so significant that the optical-UV emission are purely from the thermal emission. Meanwhile, they have the smallest value of ^ _ b and ^ _ max . This indicates that the environment around the blob have a significant effect on the distribution of electrons in blob and the cooling of the electrons are dominated by EC process for these sources.

sources.summary: PKS 1510-089 is used as an observed BL Lac jet where a bright feature produced multi-band flares and was interpreted as plasma excited by a conical standing shock.

quotes.quote: Marscher et al. (2008, 2010) showed high-resolution radio images and optical polarization measurements of the BL Lac type object PKS 1510-089, revealed a bright feature in its jet that caused a double flare of radiation from optical frequencies to TeV gamma-ray energies, as well as a delayed outburst at radio wavelengths. It was concluded that the observational event started in a region with a helical magnetic field which was identified with the acceleration and collimation zone predicted by theoretical works, and the variability in the observed brightness was due to emission by the plasma excited by a conical standing shock wave. Furthermore, observations of the jet structure of M87 (Walker et al., 2008) and PKS 1510-089 (Marscher et al., 2008) near the central black hole indicate that, while there can be moving shocks between 10 and 1000 Schwarzschild-radii ( r_s ), the first strong stationary shock occurs at 3000 r_s , as already proposed by Markoff et al. (2005) and confirmed by Britzen et al. (2008) in the case of the BL Lac type object S5 1803+784.
Specifically we assume that particle acceleration can take place in such internal shocks as theorized or observed in e.g. PKS 1510-089, NGC6251, etc.

sources.summary: The paper identifies PKS 1510 089 as a rare VHE flat-spectrum radio quasar detected at 9.2 sigma, with day-scale high-energy and optical variability.

quotes.quote: Due to the synchrotron peak of their emission lying at low frequency and their soft ray spectrum, flat spectrum radio quasars (FSRQ) are rare at VHE, with only three known sources: 4C + 21.35 , 3C 279 and PKS 1510 089 . The later was detected at a 9.2 CL in about 16 h of good quality data. Even though no significant variability was detected in the VHE range, contemporaneous observations with in the high energies and ATOM in the optical band revealed strong variability on a day scale (see Fig. fig:pks1510 ). The VHE spectrum was used to derive upper limits on the photon density of the extragalactic background light (EBL, see Section sec:ebl ).

sources.summary: The paper cites PKS 1510 089 as showing the largest known EVPA swing and several additional rotations, interpreted as evidence for irregular circular jet motion.

quotes.quote: The largest EVPA swing to date is observed in PKS 1510 089 2010ApJ...710L.126M and amounts 720 ^ . Assuming circular motion of the blazar jet, this overall rotation might be represented by a sequence of 180 ^ -360 ^ EVPA turns. Other five detected rotational events in this blazar 2011PASJ...63..489S,2014AA...569A..46A,2015MNRAS.453.1669B,2016MNRAS.457.2252B have amplitudes from 200^ to 500 ^ and occur in different directions (Fig. fig_pks15_blin shows an example of blazar activity in 2014). These continuous EVPA variations imply that the PKS 1510 089 jet executes irregular, circular motion.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 1510--089 as a well-studied flat-spectrum radio quasar repeatedly observed by INTEGRAL, with long-baseline data used for SED modeling and periodicity searches.

quotes.quote: 2015), the flat-spectrum radio quasars 3C 279, 3C 454.3, PKS 1502+106, and PKS 1510--089 were INTEGRAL targets in various occasions.

The flat-spectrum radio quasar PKS 1510--089 ( z = 0.36 ) is well studied at all frequencies, including TeV (HESS Collaboration 2013), and was repeatedly observed by INTEGRAL . We used this circumstance to assemble data for this source over a rather long baseline (20 years) and to reconstruct its multi-wavelength history. The spectral energy distribution was consistently compared with a model to trace the time-dependent behavior of the physical parameters and we searched the long data series for possible (quasi-)periodicities which were found however to be not significant (Castignani et al.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes PKS 1510-089 as a bright Fermi-LAT blazar, finding broad gamma-ray variability and blazar-like flux statistics.

quotes.quote: Using a continuous gamma-ray light curves over 3-days cadence from August 2008 – October 2015, we present the first systematic study of these features in four sources: the FR I radio galaxy NGC 1275 and three blazars- Mrk 421, B2 1520+31 and PKS 1510-089. For all, except Mrk 421, the flux spans 2 orders of magnitude. For blazars, a log-normal profile describes the flux histograms better compared to a Gaussian, while none is favored for NGC 1275, the only non blazar source, suggesting a complex distribution. Regardless of flux histogram profile, the rms-flux relation is linear for all with PSDs being consistent with a power-law shot noise spectrum despite hints of breaks.

sources.summary: The paper finds PKS 1510--089 has many flares in both bands but no significant long-term gamma-ray/radio correlation, with only a tendency toward gamma-ray harder-when-brighter behavior.

quotes.quote: PKS 1510--089. There are many big flares in the both energy bands, however, the four main flares at 43 GHz correspond to different ray emission states, as illustrated in Figure LC_1510 (a). The DCF result indicates that no significant correlation of flux variations over 90 confidence level is observed between ray and 43 GHz in the long-term light curves. The PSD slope of the radio band ( 1.18 0.18 ) corresponds to the red-noise process while that in the ray band ( 0.94 0.23 ) is roughly consistent with the red-noise process. And that only a correlation tendency is observed between _ and L_ with =0.47 and p=2.5 10^ 7 .

sources.summary: The paper treats PKS 1510 089 as a selected core-dominated quasar, assuming IC/CS X-ray emission from its kpc-jet knots and estimating its jet geometry.

quotes.quote: These criteria are met by the following objects: 3C 273, PKS 1127 145, PKS 1045 188, PKS 1510 089.
The greatest values (of several dozen degrees) correspond to PA ( PA +180^ for PKS 1510 089).

Therefore, we assume that the X-ray radiation of all knots of the jets of PKS 0637 752, PKS 1045 188, and PKS 1510 089 is formed due to IC/CS. In the farthest knots of these jets detected in the radio range, X-rays are not detected, and it is possible that IC/CMB occurs in them.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1510 089 as a highly polarized, X-ray/gamma-ray bright quasar with a bent superluminal radio jet; the campaign saw simultaneous radio fading and noticeable near-infrared variations.

quotes.quote: 1510 089 This is another extreme quasar, highly polarized, and bright at X-ray and ray energies. It exhibits a highly bent radio jet structure with fast superluminal speeds of up to 20 ( 1997ApJ...491..515S,1999ApJS..123...79H,2001ApJ...549..840H ). In the radio band, we saw the source fading simultaneously at all frequencies (Fig. fig:lc ). Noticeable variations were seen also in the near-IR (Fig. fig:olc ).

sources.summary: The paper says J1510 0906 showed confirmed INOV during recent monitoring, large long-term brightening and fading, but earlier campaigns found it non-variable.

quotes.quote: J1510 0906 (PKS 1510-08; z = 0.360 ) This FSRQ showed confirmed INOV on 2 of the 3 nights it was monitored by us over the span of 5 years (Goyal et al., in prep.). In the long term, it brightened by 1.5 mag over the course of 4 years and then faded by 0.25 mag over the next 19 days when we last monitored it on 20 May 2009. In earlier INOV campaigns by Romero et al. \ (2002) and Stalin et al. \ (2005), this blazar was found to be non-variable.

sources.summary: The paper models bright Fermi-LAT flares of PKS 1510-089, locating the gamma-ray flaring zone at about 0.3-3 pc and emphasizing multi-band constraints on jet geometry.

quotes.quote: In this paper we presented a new approach for constraining luminous blazars, incorporating fully time-dependent and self-consistent modeling of particularly bright and well-resolved ray flares of PKS 1510-089 detected with Fermi LAT.

And indeed, the best-fit location of the ray flaring region estimated here for PKS 1510-089 turns out as R 0.3 pc for a free-expanding jet with the opening angle _ jet 1/ 2.6 deg, up to R 3 pc for a collimated outflow with _ jet 0.1/ 0.26 deg as advocated by Clausen13,Jorstad05,Zdziarski15 .

Only strictly simultaneous observations of flaring PKS 1510--089 at infrared, X-ray, and GeV photon energies, augmented by fully self-consistent and time-dependent simulations as presented in this paper, may help to remove such an order-of-magnitude uncertainty, by imposing precise constraints on the magnetization and opening angle of the emitting region.

sources.summary: For 1510 089, the paper finds weaker gamma-ray variations than Paper I, a zero-lag optical/gamma-ray flare around 2013.7, and synchronous millimetre-band variability.

quotes.quote: 1510 089 : The ray variations in this source are by a factor >2 larger in Paper I compared to those analysed here. The ray and the optical flare around 2013.7 correlated with a zero-time lag. In the millimetre waveband the 37 GHz lagged those at 95 GHz by 5 d, implying the variability at both these bands to be synchronous.

sources.summary: The paper uses PKS1510 as a high-power FSRQ example, contrasting its Compton dominance and lower peak frequencies with Mkn501, fitting its quiescent emission, and predicting a longer radio lag.

quotes.quote: The main spectral differences between PKS1510 and Mkn501 are that PKS1510 has a higher Compton-dominance and lower peak emission frequencies.

The quiescent emission of PKS1510 has been fitted using model parameters in Table Table2 , where crucially the majority of the ray emission originates from inverse-Compton scattering of CMB and starlight seed photons.
For low power BL Lac blazars, such as Markarian 501, the radio lag at 5GHz is roughly 6 months, whilst in the high power FSRQ PKS1510 this lag increases to 4 years.

sources.summary: The paper reports weak suborbital gamma-ray variability evidence for PKS 1510-089, notes its very-high-energy detection, and discusses variable BLR lines.

quotes.quote: Consecutively zooming in on the brightest flares, which we identify in an objective way through Bayesian blocks and a hill-climbing algorithm, we find variability on subhour time scales and as short as minutes for two sources in our sample (3C 279, CTA 102) and weak evidence for variability at time scales less than the Fermi satellite's orbit of 95 minutes for PKS 1510-089 and 3C 454.3. This suggests extremely compact emission regions in the jet. We do not find any signs for ray absorption in the broad-line region (BLR), which indicates that rays are produced at distances greater than hundreds of gravitational radii from the central black hole. This is further supported by a cross-correlation analysis between ray and radio/millimeter light curves, which is consistent with ray production at the same location as the millimeter core for 3C 273, CTA 102, and 3C 454.3. The inferred locations of the ray production zones are still consistent with the observed decay times of the brightest flares if the decay is caused by external Compton scattering with BLR photons. However, the minute-scale variability is challenging to explain in such scenarios.

As noted in the Introduction, two of the sources (3C 279 and CTA 102) have already been shown to be variable on extremely short timescales TheFermi-LAT:2016dss,2018ApJ...854L..26S . Here we will introduce a novel objective method to identify a large set of flares in order to conduct a comprehensive search for the short variability in our source sample. Furthermore, PKS B1222+216 2011ApJ...730L...8A , 3C 279 2008Sci...320.1752M , and PKS 1510-089 2011ApJ...730L...8A,2013A&A...554A.107H,2014A&A...569A..46A are among the seven FSRQs also detected above 100 GeV with imaging air Cerenkov Telescopes. For 3C 454.3, the MAGIC and VERITAS telescopes only obtained upper limits on the flux during flaring states in 2007 2009A&A...498...83A and 2010 2016AJ....151..142A .

Furthermore, evidence exists that the the luminosity of BLR emission lines is variable in 3C 454.3, PKS 1510-089, and PKS B1222+216 2013ApJ...763L..36L,2015ApJ...804....7I and correlates with the emission 2013ApJ...763L..36L,2015IAUS..313...43L , which could indicate that are produced through IC scattering with BLR photons (see also Section sec:tcool ).

sources.summary: The paper selects PKS 1510-089 as an extreme FSRQ, models its SED with SSC contribution, and places its emission region outside but close to the BLR edge.

quotes.quote: We selected the six FSRQs (CTA 102, 3C 273, 3C 279, 3C 454.3, PKS 1510-089 PKS B1222+216) from the Fermi LAT monitored source list, also studied in 2019ApJ...877...39M , which are known for exhibiting brightest ray flares with average daily fluxes over 10^ 5 cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 within 1 statistical uncertainties above 100 MeV 2019ApJ...877...39M .
The contributions from EC emissions of Doppler de-boosted disk radiation ( EC_ disk ) were required in 3C 273 and 3C 454.3 SED, and SSC contribution was required in CTA 102 and PKS 1510-089 SED.
In case of CTA 102 and PKS 1510-089 the emission regions were close to the outer edge of the BLR and far away from the dusty torus.

sources.summary: The paper measures positive 15 GHz core circular polarization for 1510 089 and compares this result with earlier 15 and 5 GHz nondetections or weak peak values.

quotes.quote: 1510 089: We measure m_c = +0.20 0.09 in the core at 15 GHz in November of 2002. HW99 did not detect circular polarization in the core at 15 GHz during 1996: three epochs had limits of 0.1 to 0.2 , and for two epochs they reported peak CP values of +0.2 0.2 . HAW01 find a limit of |m_c| < 0.24 in the core at 5 GHz in December of 1996.

gmw_00000798.name: Q2138-431
gmw_00000798.overview: Q2138-431 is a wide-separation double quasar system that acts as a gravitational lens [arXiv:2104.02454]. Deep infrared observations have detected a faint lensing galaxy with an estimated mass of 1.31 × 10^12 solar masses positioned between the two quasar images [arXiv:2104.02454].

sources.summary: The paper reviews Q2138-431 as a wide-separation double quasar lens candidate, reporting a faint lensing galaxy detection, mass modeling, time-delay evidence, and suitability for Hubble-constant work.

quotes.quote: This paper reviews the question of whether the wide separation double quasar Q2138-431 is a gravitational lens. From early work, the two quasar images are known to have almost identical spectra and redshifts, but no lensing galaxy has so far been detected. In this paper we used recent deep surveys in infrared and optical bands to search for the presence of a galaxy with the expected properties of a gravitational lens. The search revealed a 5 detection of a faint galaxy between the two quasar images on a deep J band frame from the VISTA Science Archive, with apparent magnitude J = 20.68 . Non-detection in the I band implied a redshift z > 0.6 , and mass modelling of the quasar system gave a mass of 1.31 10^ 12 M_ for the lensing galaxy, with mass-to-light ratio M_ /L_ = 9.0 .

Although the similarity of the spectra and the detection of the lensing galaxy are the most compelling arguments for the classification of Q2138-431 as a gravitational lens, the time delay and mass-to-light ratio provide a consistent picture to support this conclusion. The wide separation of the quasar images and the simplicity of the mass model make Q2138-431 an excellent system for the measurement of the Hubble constant.

gmw_00000799.name: WPVS 007
gmw_00000799.overview: WPVS 007 is a low-redshift, low-luminosity narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:2405.06027, arXiv:1508.01257] that exhibits a highly ionized, high-velocity outflow [arXiv:2405.06027]. The galaxy displays strong variability across X-ray, ultraviolet, optical, and mid-infrared wavelengths [arXiv:2405.06027, arXiv:1905.06538], which is accompanied by changing broad absorption lines [arXiv:1508.01257, arXiv:1905.06538].

sources.summary: The paper investigates WPVS 007 as a low-redshift NLS1 with BAL-like outflows, finding highly ionized gas, strong mass loss, and variability driven by changing covering fraction.

quotes.quote: WPVS 007, a low-redshift, low-luminosity Narrow-line Seyfert 1 (NLS1) shows strong variability over shorter timescales, providing a unique opportunity to study the driving mechanism behind this variability that may mimic longer scale variability in much more massive quasars. We present the first variability study using the spectral synthesis code , which provides velocity-resolved changes in physical conditions of the gas using constraints from multiple absorption lines. Overall, we find WPVS 007 to have a highly ionized outflow with a large mass-loss rate and kinetic luminosity. We determine the primary cause of the absorption-line variability in WPVS 007 to be a change in covering fraction of the continuum by the outflow.

An excellent choice for a case study as described above is WPVS 007, a Narrow-line Seyfert 1 (NLS1) galaxy at redshift 0.02861 (NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database redshift value) with a small black hole mass ( 4.1 10^6 M_ ; 2009Leighly ) and low luminosity ( 5.20 10^ 43 erg s^ 1 ; Leighly2015 ) that is behaving like a BALQ with a high-velocity outflow. Low-luminosity AGN with smaller black hole masses and size scales will show variability on smaller timescales than the much larger BALQs making a rare low-redshift source like WPVS 007 an excellent laboratory to study variability in broad absorption line quasars. WPVS 007 has a high velocity for its luminosity, making it an outlier for the velocity-luminosity relationship found by 2002Laor for low-redshift, soft X-ray weak quasars see Figure 13 and related discussion in 2009Leighly . When WPVS 007 was first detected in the ROSAT All Sky Survey 1999Voges , it showed the softest x-ray spectrum ever observed in an AGN 1995Grupe .

sources.summary: The paper reports a UV occultation event in WPVS 007 and connects its reddening, broad absorption-line variability, and low-luminosity narrow-line Seyfert nature to torus-origin absorbing gas.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of an occultation event in the low-luminosity narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy WPVS 007 in 2015 February and March. In concert with longer timescale variability, these observations place strong constraints on the nature and location of the absorbing material. monitoring has revealed a secular decrease since 2010 accompanied by flattening of the optical and UV photometry that suggests variable reddening.

WPVS 007 is a low luminosity ( M_V 19.7 ), nearby ( z=0.02882 ) narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy grupe95 with peculiar UV properties. In a 1996 Hubble Space Telescope FOS spectrum, it showed moderately narrow absorption lines that classified it as a mini-BAL quasar. A FUSE spectrum from 2003 showed evidence for a dramatic change in the absorption: in addition to the mini-BALs, a broad absorption line with V_ max 6,000 km s^ 1 had appeared leighly09 .

sources.summary: The paper describes HE0036-5133 as an elongated elliptical QSO host whose photometry suggests high AGN fraction and possible hidden substructure, including indications of a nuclear bar and spiral arms.

quotes.quote: 05 HE0036-5133 The galaxy is an elongated elliptical ( =0.4 ). The position in the color-color-diagram is consistent with a high AGN fraction. Only fits with AGN and one Sersic-component are successful. However, the low resulting Sersic-index of about 2, the bump in the ellipticity and the residuum can be seen as indicators for a hidden substructure. Fischer (2008, PhD-thesis) reported indications for a nuclear bar and spiral arms.

sources.summary: The paper identifies WPVS 7 as the softest AGN and brightest corrected Seyfert in the sample, noting its poorly determined hard ratio and strong transient X-ray fading.

quotes.quote: WPVS 7: With HR1 = -0.97 , this NLS1 is the softest AGN in the sample. There are almost no counts in bands H1 and H2 , so the hardness ratio HR2 is not well-determined. The foreground HI column is comparatively high at 2.5 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 and, after correcting for the Galactic transmission, this is the brightest Seyfert galaxy in the sample (in terms of its incident flux). WPVS 7 appears to be a transient source, as subsequent pointed \ observations show that its X-ray flux dropped by a factor of 400 with respect to the survey measurement, the largest such variation seen for any Seyfert 1 galaxy (Grupe 1995).

sources.summary: The paper finds large mid-infrared, UV, and optical variability in narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy WPVS 007 and links its BAL variability to intrinsic ionizing-continuum changes.

quotes.quote: firstpage - lastpage We report the discovery of large-amplitude mid-infrared variabilities (MIR; 0.3 mag) in the Wide-field Infrared Survey Explorer W1 and W2 bands of the low-luminosity narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy WPVS 007, which exhibits prominent and varying broad-absorption lines (BALs) with blueshifted velocity up to 14000 km \ s^ 1 .

Therefore, we propose that the BAL variability observed in WPVS 007 is likely induced by the intrinsic ionizing continuum variation, alternative to the rotating-torus model proposed in a previous work.

The low-luminosity ( M_V 19.7 ) narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy (NLS1) WPVS 007, located at z = 0.02882 Grupe1995 , is one of the most extensively studied source owing to its peculiar variability behavior e.g., Leighly2009, Grupe2013, Leighly2015 .

sources.summary: The paper describes WPVS 007 as a Narrow-Line Seyfert 1 whose RASS soft X-ray emission later faded to inactive-galaxy levels, possibly from accretion-disk Comptonization changes.

quotes.quote: WPVS 007 The Narrow-Line Seyfert 1 galaxy (NLS1) WPVS 007 was the softest AGN observed during the RASS and had a mean count rate of about 1 cts s ^ 1 . In later pointings the source shows only the flux expected from a normal inactive galaxy. A possible scenario to explain this dramatic turn-off is a temperature change in the Comptonization layer of the accretion disk that shifts the soft X-ray photons out of the ROSAT energy window.

gmw_00000800.name: IC 3599
gmw_00000800.overview: IC 3599 is a weakly active spiral galaxy [arXiv:1502.07835] classified as a changing-look active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2401.10600] or Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:1502.06946, arXiv:1505.01093]. It has exhibited repeated, high-amplitude X-ray and optical outbursts [arXiv:1502.06946, arXiv:2401.10600] that may be caused by periodic partial tidal disruption events [arXiv:1502.07835] or accretion disk instabilities [arXiv:2401.10600].

sources.summary: The paper detects compact nuclear radio emission in IC 3599, places it within the ROSAT error circle and optical nucleus, and argues the emission is tied to the nucleus that underwent Seyfert-like evolution during the TDE epoch.

quotes.quote: IC 3599 Figure fig:ic3599 shows the image of the radio counterpart for a TDE detected with ROSAT in 1990. The image has been made with only baselines longer than 20 k in order to filter out faint, diffuse emission associated with the galaxy. The remaining object is well-fit as a compact point source with flux density of 185 28 Jy. The centroid of the source is well within the 40 \ error circle associated with ROSAT and is within 0.2 \ of the optical nucleus of the galaxy.

We argue that the radio emission from IC 3599 is associated with the nucleus responsible for the X-ray TDE candidate. IC 3599 exhibited variable optical emission lines that are consistent with a Seyfert nucleus prior to and after the detection of the X-ray flare 1995MNRAS.273L..47B,1995A&A...299L...5G,1999A&A...343..775K . In particular, optical spectroscopy demonstrated an evolution of the Seyfert-like spectrum on a timescale of 1 years from a Sy 1.5 to Sy 1.9 classificaiton. The disk of IC 3599 is 20 , comparable in size to the error circle from the ROSAT observations.

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 3599 as a weakly active spiral/AGN whose repeated X-ray flares are best explained as periodic partial tidal disruptions rather than ordinary AGN variability.

quotes.quote: A candidate periodic sequence of partial disruption events has been observed in the spiral galaxy IC 3599 (see for more details). This is a weakly active galaxy in the Coma Cluster ( 92 Mpc). IC 3599 was discovered as a soft X-ray source during the ROSAT All Sky Survey in December 1990. Following ROSAT and Chandra observations from 1992 to 2002 showed an X-ray flux decrease of a factor of about 100 and an X-ray spectral hardening.

Several authors , , , interpreted this emission as coming from the total tidal disruption of a star orbiting around the central massive black hole ( 3 10^5 M_ ) of the galaxy, even if IC 3599 is a low-luminosity active galactic nucleus (AGN). Indeed in AGNs it is difficult to distinguish between variability coming from the galaxy itself and variability induced by the disruption of a star, even if in AGNs the presence of a pre-existent accretion disk enhances the probability to have tidal disruption events , . From 2010 to 2014 IC 3599 was observed again by the Swift/XRT telescope. Surprisingly this source showed a new increase and successive decrease of a factor of about 30 in its bolometric luminosity an also a new softening and then hardening in its X-ray spectrum very similarly to the already observed event.

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 3599 as an extreme changing-look AGN whose X-ray/optical outburst produced transient broad and high-ionization lines that later faded.

quotes.quote: The AGN IC 3599 (Zwicky 159.034) harbored an extreme CL event, dramatically changing its optical emission-line spectrum following a high-amplitude soft X-ray (and optical) outburst with a peak luminosity of L_ x = 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 Brandt1995, Grupe1995 . Optical spectra taken during the first outburst revealed bright optical emission lines, including broad Balmer lines with FWHM of 1200 and high-ionization iron lines of similar width Brandt1995 . These then faded away strongly in subsequent years. In low-state, IC 3599 is classified as Sy 1.9 galaxy with faint broad-line emission only detectable in H and with a long-lived narrow-line region (NLR) including faint high-ionization iron lines KomossaBade1999 .

However, in any case, IC 3599 can certainly be regarded as an extreme case of a CL AGN. The high-amplitude outbursts of IC 3599 were suggested by Grupe2015 to be due to possible thermal instabilities in the accretion disk, propagating through the inner region at the sound speed and causing an increase of the local accretion rate. Episodes of repeat flaring are then produced on the timescale of decades when the inner disk empties and refills. The two bright outbursts of IC 3599 were separated by 20 yrs.

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 3599 as a Seyfert galaxy with repeated X-ray/optical outbursts, weighing TDE-related explanations against AGN accretion-disk or binary-SMBH scenarios.

quotes.quote: The Seyfert galaxy IC 3599 (Zwicky 159.034) underwent a dramatic X-ray outburst during the ROSAT all-sky survey, accompanied by a strong brightening of the optical emission lines which then faded again (Brandt et al. 1995, Grupe et al. 1995a, Komossa Bade 1999). Several outburst scenarios were discussed, including high-amplitude NLS1 variability, a disk instability, or a stellar tidal disruption event (TDE). IC 3599 was already known as a Seyfert galaxy before the outburst (based on its narrow emission lines like [ OIII ] ), so it was difficult to make a strong case for a TDE, because of the long-term presence of an accretion disk in that galaxy, and therefore the possibility to link the high-amplitude variability to accretion processes (similar to, but more extreme than, other AGN). IC 3599 had remained very faint ever since, but was recently discovered in another outburst with Swift (Fig. 4).

After we noticed the new high state, we triggered new observations with Swift , which show that the 2010 outburst is over, and X-ray emission levels are down again by a factor 100. If IC 3599 hosts a binary black hole or recoiling black hole, tidal disruption rates are temporarily strongly boosted (e.g., Chen et al. 2009, Komossa Merritt 2008, Stone Loeb 2011), and a new disruption event can occur within a decade. Given that IC 3599 hosts a long-lived AGN, extreme processes in its accretion disk, possibly due to instabilities, are a possible cause of its recurrent outbursts.

sources.summary: The review discusses IC 3599 as an active Seyfert galaxy with ROSAT and Swift X-ray outbursts whose repeated flaring makes a TDE explanation debated.

quotes.quote: The Seyfert galaxy IC 3599 showed an X-ray outburst during the ROSAT all-sky survey, accompanied by a temporary remarkable brightening and fading of the optical emission lines (Brandt et al. 1995, Grupe et al. 1995a, Komossa Bade 1999). The cause of the outburst remained unknown, and high-amplitude narrow-line Seyfert 1 (NLS1) variability we note in passing, that IC 3599 is not a classical NLS1 galaxy, though, since it lacks the strong, characteristic FeII emission, typical for these galaxies, and since the only available optical high-state spectrum (Brandt et al. 1995) was just a snapshot of the fading broad emission lines responding to the flare, and the full line width can therefore be much higher than the value of 2000 km/s typically used to define NLS1 galaxies as a class , a disk instability, or a TDE have all been considered. IC 3599 was already known as an active galaxy before the outburst (based on its narrow emission lines like [ OIII ] ). Recently, a second outburst of IC 3599 was discovered by Swift in 2010 (Komossa et al. 2015, Campana et al. 2015, Grupe et al. 2015a).

Recurrent flaring within decades is uncommon in TDE scenarios, even though not impossible, since, for instance, rates are strongly boosted under some conditions in galaxy mergers (e.g., Chen et al. 2009), and repeat tidal stripping can occur for orbiting stars surviving the first encounter. This latter possibility was explored by Campana et al. (2015), while processes related to the AGN accretion disk (and SMBBHs) where discussed by Komossa et al. (2015) and Grupe et al. (2015a).

sources.summary: The paper identifies IC 3599 as a transient active galactic nucleus: a candidate Seyfert 2 formerly classed as an NLS1, now showing weak narrow permitted lines after fading.

quotes.quote: Mrk 663 is probably therefore the only candidate Seyfert 2 in the present sample besides the transient IC 3599. Both IC 3599 and NGC 5905 have been identified as X-ray transient AGN (e.g., GRU95A ; BRA95A ; BAD96 ; KOM99B ). Both objects seem to have been detected by \ during some kind of nuclear outburst and have in recent years `switched off,' showing a substantial drop in their X-ray and optical continua as well as broad and high-excitation line emission. IC 3599 was originally identified as a NLS1 (see BRA95A ) but now shows only very weak and narrow permitted lines, while NGC 5905 now resembles an H ii region more than a Seyfert galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper identifies IC 3599 as a Seyfert 2 galaxy with a RASS X-ray outburst, optical-line response, and possible accretion-event or tidal-disruption origin.

quotes.quote: IC 3599 The Seyfert 2 galaxy IC 3599 has shown an X-ray outburst during the RASS followed by a response in its optical emission lines ( , ). A possible explanation of this X-ray outburst is an accretion event either caused by an instability of the accretion disk or by a tidal disruption of a star orbiting around the central black hole.
While in WPVS007 the question is why its X-ray source is off right now, the question for IC 3599 and RX J1624.9+7554 is what was the reason for the outburst.

The outburst in IC3599 and RX J1624.9+7554 can be explained by accretion events, e.g. the tidal disruption of a star that comes to close to the central black hole, a scenario that has been suggested by e.g. .

sources.summary: The paper treats IC 3599 as a highly transient NLS1-like source whose later observations dropped sharply and whose spectral fitting suggests extra absorption or complexity.

quotes.quote: IC 3599: This is another highly transient source, with later pointed \ observations giving a flux 100 times lower than at the time of the all-sky survey. A recent optical spectrum most closely resembles that of an extragalactic HII region, but at the time of the survey this source clearly had the appearance of a NLS1 (Brandt, Pounds Fink 1995) and that classification is adopted herein.

The other sources are IC 3599 and RE J1034+39, both of which are NLS1s, and LB 1727, a BLS1 galaxy. More detailed spectral fitting of IC 3599 (Brandt 1995), RE J1034+39 (Pounds 1995) and LB 1727 (Turner 1999) is consistent with additional N_H or a relatively complex spectral form in these objects.

sources.summary: The paper compares IC 3599 to AT2019pev as an AGN flare case with repeated flares, harder-when-fainter behavior, and a much longer flare recurrence interval.

quotes.quote: This model has been used to interpret the flares in AGNs such as NGC 3599 Saxton2015 and IC 3599 Brandt1995,Grupe1995,Grupe2015 . IC 3599 shows repeated flares and a ``harder-when-fainter'' behavior, similar to that seen in AT2019pev. However, the second flare of IC 3599 occurs about 20 years after the first flare, while the second peak of AT2019pev is only about 200 days after the first peak.

gmw_00000801.name: RX J2217.9--5941
gmw_00000801.overview: RX J2217.9--5941 is a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy and a candidate X-ray transient [arXiv:astro-ph/0108398]. The source is highly variable on timescales of days to years and has become fainter over time [arXiv:astro-ph/0108398].

sources.summary: The paper treats RX J2217.9--5941 as a possible Narrow-Line Seyfert 1 X-ray transient, highly variable over days to years and fading in pointed ROSAT and ASCA observations.

quotes.quote: RX J2217.9--5941 The NLS1 RX J2217.9--5941 is a possible X-ray transient candidate. It is highly variable on time scales of days as well as years . During its two-day RASS observation the count rate decrease by a factor of 15. Observed several times in pointed observations by ROSAT and ASCA the source has become fainter over the years. It is not clear yet if this source will become a transient.

gmw_00000802.name: Mrk 0609
gmw_00000802.overview: Mrk 0609 is a composite starburst and Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0701053, arXiv:astro-ph/0204514] featuring a pseudo-bulge, an inner disk, and an outer disk [arXiv:1710.02194]. Its nucleus exhibits LINER-like characteristics [arXiv:astro-ph/0701053], while its active galactic nucleus shows long-term X-ray variability [arXiv:astro-ph/0204514] and lacks X-ray evidence of obscuration [arXiv:1404.0012].

sources.summary: The paper studies Mrk 609 as a composite starburst/Seyfert galaxy, resolving its central 2 kpc, finding complex emission-line morphology, a supermassive black hole indicator, a LINER-like nucleus, and molecular gas fuel.

quotes.quote: We present first results of near-infrared (NIR) J and H+K ESO-SINFONI integral field spectroscopy of the composite starburst/Seyfert 1.8 galaxy Mrk 609. The data were taken during the science verification period of SINFONI. We aim to investigate the morphology and excitation conditions within the central 2 kpc. Additional Nobeyama 45 m CO(1-0) data are presented, which we used to estimate the molecular gas mass.

Our NIR data reveal a complex emission-line morphology that is possibly associated with a nuclear bar seen in the reconstructed continuum images. The detections of [ Si VI ] and a broad Pa component are clear indicators of the presence of an accreting super-massive black hole at the center of Mrk 609. In agreement with previous observations, we find that the circumnuclear emission is not significantly extincted. Analysis of the high angular-resolution, molecular hydrogen emission and [ Fe II ] emission reveals the LINER character of the nucleus.

sources.summary: The paper finds Mrk 0609 lacks X-ray evidence for obscuration despite optical Sy2 spectra, favoring either a changing-look AGN or a true/naked Sy2 interpretation.

quotes.quote: Mrk 0609 - A ``Changing-Look'' AGN or True Sy2? Though the SDSS spectrum from 2001 and the CTIO spectrum from 2007 trippe shows an absence of broad optical emission lines, there is no X-ray evidence to support that the view to the central engine is obscured. Not only do we find no absorption above that from our Galaxy, the Fe K feature is not significant above the 2 level me , suggesting a lack of global obscuration. BeppoSAX observations from 2000 also detected no signatures of absorption. Additionally, the observed 2-10 keV X-ray luminosity of 4 10^ 42 erg s ^ 1 is consistent with that of an unabsorbed nucleus, given the observed [ OIII ] 5007 \ luminosity of 2 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 and [ OIV ] 26 m luminosity of 2.14 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 and the relationships found between 2-10 keV X-ray and [ OIII ] and [ OIV ] luminosities for Sy1s (log(L _ 2-10keV, unabsorbed /L _ OIII ) = 1.59 0.48 dex, Heckman et al. 2005; log(L _ 2-10keV, unabsorbed /L _ [ OIV ] ) = 1.92 0.60 dex, Diamond-Stanic et al. 2009).

Mrk 0609 can possibly be a ``changing-look'' AGN e.g., ren,elvis04,puccetti,risaliti09,bianchi09 where the optical observations in 2001 and 2007 caught the source in an obscured state while the 2000 and 2007 X-ray observations detected the object while unobscured. Alternatively, Mrk 0609 may belong to the class of ``true'' Sy2s that lack circumnuclear obscuration: broad emission lines are not observed because they do not exist.

sources.summary: The paper models the AGN host as pseudo-bulge plus inner and outer disks and explains its B/T discrepancy by the inner-disk treatment.

quotes.quote: Mrk 609 (Fig. 11.18) --- \ \ The WFPC2 mosaic image is saturated in the center. The AGN host appears to have a pseudo-bulge ( n=0.37 ), an inner disk, and another outer disk. We adopt the hybrid method to model the inner disk. Our fitting result ( B/T 0.11 ) is notably different from that in Orban de Xivry et al. (2011; B/T 0.66 ). This is because we use an inner disk to model the host. If instead we assume the inner disk is part of the bulge, B/T becomes dramatically larger ( 0.77 ) comparable to that from Orban de Xivry et al.

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk609 as a starburst/Seyfert composite whose X-ray spectrum, luminosity, and long-term variability indicate AGN-dominated emission despite its optical starburst features.

quotes.quote: We present observations of the starburst/Seyfert composite galaxy Mrk609. This enigmatic object has an optical spectrum dominated by the features of starburst galaxies, yet its X-ray luminosity (6.3 10^ 42 ) is typical of an AGN. The X-ray spectrum of Mrk609 can be parameterised by a single power-law model with a photon index 1.6 0.1 and no evidence for significant absorption above the Galactic value. Long term variability in both the 0.1-2 keV and 2-10 keV energy bands is detected, again suggesting that the X-ray emission is dominated by an AGN.

gmw_00000803.name: SDSS J093952.74+355358.0
gmw_00000803.overview: SDSS J093952.74+355358.0 is a radio-loud, Type 2 quasar [arXiv:2301.07193] and Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy [arXiv:1404.7499, arXiv:1003.0623] situated within a group of 12 small galaxies [arXiv:1003.0623]. The active nucleus is heavily obscured by patchy gas [arXiv:2301.07193] and exhibits a reflection-dominated, Compton-thick X-ray spectrum [arXiv:1407.6944].

sources.summary: The paper reports low-frequency LOFAR observations and energetics analysis of 3C223, using revised lobe fluxes to infer higher energy content from a steeper low-frequency spectrum.

quotes.quote: As part of the LOFAR nearby AGN key science project, observations were therefore made of the nearby FR-II source 3C223 between 30 and 240 MHz. Although at only the early stages of analysis, preliminary results are promising. One of the primary limiting factors with such spectral analysis is determining an accurate flux scale to constrain any spectral curvature present within the lobes of FR-II galaxies.

A preliminary analysis has also been undertaken into the energetics of 3C223. X-ray inverse-Compton measurements have previously been used to constrain the total energy content of the lobes of radio galaxies; however, it has been necessary to make assumptions about the spectral profile at radio wavelengths. With these new observations, we are now able to revisit these results and better constrain the models at low-frequencies. Using the methods of croston04 , the integrated flux values for the northern and southern lobes of 3C223 were added to extend the frequency coverage of the original values. Fitting these revised measurements to the inverse-Compton models, we find a significant increase in the total energy content, which in the case of the northern lobe may be up to twice the originally derived value.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C223 as a group-hosted FRII giant radio source whose low-frequency morphology includes diffuse relic-like emission and a younger synchrotron age.

quotes.quote: The host galaxy of 3C223 is in a group of 12 small galaxies . It is a typical double radio source with a regular FRII structure . It has been previously studied in with the WRST at 608 MHz, 1.4 and 5 GHz ( Hogbom1979 ; vanBreugel1982 ; Jaegers1987 ) and at high resolution with the VLA at 1.4 GHz . As can be observed in the higher brightness contours of the image, the morphology of the source preserves the FRII structure. An extended low brightness structure is easily visible to the West of the southern lobe and some faint extended emission seems also to be present to the East of the northern lobe.
For 3C35 the estimated age is about 143 20 Myr while for 3C223 is about 72 4 Myr.

sources.summary: The paper finds 3C 223's lobe X-ray flux matches IC/CMB predictions with near-equipartition fields, a 1.5 keV group-scale environment, and radio lobes near pressure balance.

quotes.quote: For the lobes of both sources (each modelled separately), the measured X-ray fluxes are in good agreement with the predictions for IC scattering of the CMB, with magnetic field strengths within a factor of 2 of equipartition. Both sources have group-scale environments (L _ X 10^ 43 ergs s ^ 1 ) with temperatures of 1.5 keV (3C 223) and 1 keV (3C 284). These results are in agreement with the predictions of L _ X /T _ X relations.

From our model for the lobe-related X-ray emission of 3C 223 and 3C 284 we find the internal energy of the radio lobes to be close to the minimum energy values. We can compare these values with the external pressure from the X-ray-emitting gas to study the lobe dynamics. We find that the lobes of both sources appear to be in pressure balance with their surroundings.

sources.summary: The paper measures 3C 223 as a double-lobed radio source, estimating its black-hole mass and finding asymmetric lobe heads with different jet powers and ages.

quotes.quote: 3C 223 has radio lobes that extend up to r_ h = 340 kpc in both sides. Its SMBH mass is estimated as 1.4 10^ 8 M_ by WU02 , based on the observed stellar velocity dispersions. As can be seen in Fig. radio , 3C 223 has asymmetry in A_ h . While a well developed cocoon head is seen at the northern hot spot ( A_ h = 4300 kpc ^2 ), the cocoon head at the southern hot spot is quite compact ( A_ h = 1800 kpc ^2 ). Reflecting this asymmetry, the obtained power and age show quite large difference: L_ j = (0.15 - 2.9) 10^ 46 ergs s ^ 1 and t_ age = 140 - 610 Myr for the northern jet, and L_ j = (0.71 - 2.0) 10^ 45 ergs s ^ 1 and t_ age = 330 - 560 Myr for the southern jet.

sources.summary: The paper highlights SDSS J093952.74+355358.0 as a Type 2 QSO with a prominent Fe K feature, Compton-thick line-of-sight absorption, and likely toroidal-ring obscuration.

quotes.quote: SDSS J093952.74+355358.0 - Compton-thick Ring of Obscuration The X-ray spectrum of SDSS J093952.74+355358.0 reveals interesting characteristics in its circumnuclear absorber that suggests different global and line-of-sight column densities. The Fe K feature is quite prominent EW 0.5 keV, jj , precluding the global and line-of-sight absorbers from being equal and Compton-thin since the line of sight continuum would dilute the line compared with a pure reflection spectrum. If both column densities are Compton-thick, then the continuum between 2 - 6 keV would be curved downwards rather than flat. This continuum curvature can be avoided if the global column density is Compton-thin. To obtain the large Fe K EW, however, the direct continuum has to be hidden behind a Compton-thick absorber.

sources.summary: The paper models 3C 223 as one of four FR II radio sources with powerful jets, large cocoon energies, and substantial invisible energy components beyond minimum-energy estimates.

quotes.quote: We investigate the total kinetic powers ( L_ j ) and ages ( t_ age ) of powerful jets of four FR II radio sources (Cygnus A, 3C 223, 3C 284, and 3C 219) by the detail comparison of the dynamical model of expanding cocoons with observed ones. It is found that these sources have quite large kinetic powers with the ratio of L_ j to the Eddington luminosity ( L_ Edd ) resides in 0.02 <L_ j /L_ Edd <10 . Reflecting the large kinetic powers, we also find that the total energy stored in the cocoon ( E_ ) exceed the energy derived from the minimum energy condition ( E_ min ): 2< E_ /E_ min <160 . This implies that a large amount of kinetic power is carried by invisible components such as thermal leptons (electron and positron) and/or protons.

sources.summary: The paper classifies 3XMMJ093952.7+355358 as a reflection-dominated Compton-thick AGN, noting model differences but emphasizing its extremely flat hard spectrum.

quotes.quote: In four cases (3XMMJ091804.2+514113, 3XMMJ093952.7+355358, 3XMMJ140700.3+282714, and 3XMMJ215649.5-074531), we estimate that the column density is likely higher than 10 ^ 24 cm ^ 2 because the X-ray spectrum is reflection dominated, meaning that there is no sign of direct emission.

3XMMJ093952.7+355358: If we fit the same best-fit model as in hard06 , that is, a double power-law plus an emission line, both best-fit N _ H values are consistent within errors, but theirs is lower, probably because the second power-law photon index was left free to vary, which resulted in a very flat slope ( 0.5). Using this model, the source would be also classified as a CT candidate in the current work based solely on the Fe K line EW, but we were unable to find the relevant value in hard06 . Given the extremely flat hard spectrum, we classify this source as a reflection-dominated CT AGN in this work.

sources.summary: The paper applies a total-cocoon-pressure method to 3C 223 as an FRII radio galaxy, finding positron-free proton-electron plasma implausible and requiring electron-positron mixtures independent of jet power and cocoon size.

quotes.quote: We apply this method to four FRII radio galaxies (Cygnus A, 3C 219, 3C 223 and 3C 284), for which the total cocoon pressures have been already evaluated. We find that the positron-free plasma comprising of protons and electrons is ruled out, when we consider plausible particle distribution functions. In other words, the mixture of positrons is required for all four FRII radio galaxies; the number density ratio of electrons/positrons to protons is larger than two. Thus, we find that the plasma composition is independent of the jet power and the size of cocoons.

sources.summary: The paper models 3C 223 as a radio-loud Type 2 quasar with patchy, heavy but not globally Compton-thick X-ray obscuration and possible jet-related spectral effects.

quotes.quote: 3C 223 is a radio loud, Type 2 quasar at z=0.1365 with an intriguing XMM-Newton spectrum that implicated it as a rare, Compton-thick ( N_ H 1.25 10^ 24 cm ^ 2 ) active galactic nucleus (AGN). We obtained contemporaneous XMM-Newton and NuSTAR spectra to fit the broad-band X-ray spectrum with the physically-motivated MYTorus and borus02 models. We confirm earlier results that the obscuring gas is patchy with both high (though not Compton-thick) levels of obscuration ( N_ H > 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 ) and gas clouds with column densities up to an order of magnitude lower. The spectral fitting results indicate additional physical processes beyond those modeled in the spectral grids of MYTorus and borus02 impact the emergent spectrum: the Compton-scattering region may be extended beyond the putative torus; a ring of heavy Compton-thick material blocks most X-ray emission along the line of sight; or the radio jet is beamed, boosting the production of Fe K line photons in the global medium compared with what is observed along the line of sight.

sources.summary: The paper compares its 3C 223 XMM-Newton modelling with Croston et al., attributing parameter and flux-density differences to model choices and calibration changes.

quotes.quote: 3C 223 The XMM-Newton data we use here were analysed by Croston et al. (2004). They fitted a model consisting of an unabsorbed power law, plus an absorbed power law and iron line; however, they fixed the photon index of the second power-law at = 1.5 , which leads to differences in the best-fitting spectral parameters. An apparent discrepancy in the flux densities obtained by the two analyses can be attributed to changes to the XMM calibration files since the work of Croston et al.

gmw_00000804.name: MORA-5
gmw_00000804.overview: MORA-5 is a high-redshift, dusty star-forming galaxy [arXiv:2111.02428] that is classified as near-infrared-dark [arXiv:2110.06930]. It is an active starburst galaxy with a star-formation rate of approximately 830 solar masses per year [arXiv:2111.02428].

sources.summary: MORA-5 is a near-infrared-dark submillimeter source with no spectroscopic redshift, radio/FIR and millimeter photometric constraints, and an adopted hybrid redshift estimate.

quotes.quote: MORA-5 a.k.a. 850.13 or AzTEC C114 The sixth source, MORA-5, has been identified at both 850 \ and 1.1 mm named 850.13 and AzTEC C114, respectively casey13a,aretxaga11a . There is no spectroscopic redshift for MORA-5, and there is no OIR-based photometric redshift from either the COSMOS2015 or COSMOS2020 catalogs due to a lack of counterpart in the near-infrared. brisbin17a offer a FIR photometric redshift of z_ FIR =5.3 3.2 , while noting that a radio-FIR-based photometric redshift is consistently lower, based on the source's detection at both 1.4 GHz and 3 GHz (respective radio-FIR photometric redshifts of z_ 1.4GHz =2.9^ +4.2 _ 0.4 and z_ 3.0GHz =1.9^ +1.1 _ 0.3 ). Our millimeter-derived photometric redshift is z_ mm =3.4^ +1.1 _ 0.9 . This source is undetected at all wavelengths shortward of 3.6 , including deep CANDELS H band and Ultra-VISTA K band imaging.

sources.summary: The paper presents MORA-5 as an OIR-dark high-redshift DSFG: a z~4.3 extreme starburst with strong ALMA continuum, high infrared luminosity, and large stellar/dust mass.

quotes.quote: Our results suggest the two galaxies, called MORA-5 and MORA-9, represent two extremes of the ``OIR-dark" class of DSFGs. MORA-5 ( z_ phot =4.3^ +1.5 _ 1.3 ) is a significantly more active starburst with a star-formation rate of 830 ^ +340 _ 190 M _ yr ^ 1 compared to MORA-9 ( z_ phot =4.3^ +1.3 _ 1.0 ) whose star-formation rate is a modest 200 ^ +250 _ 60 M _ yr ^ 1 . Based on the stellar masses (M _ 10^ 10-11 M _ ), space density ( n (5 2) 10^ 6 Mpc ^ 3 , which incorporates two other spectroscopically confirmed OIR-dark DSFGs in the MORA sample at z=4.6 and z=5.9 ), and gas depletion timescales ( <1 Gyr) of these sources, we find evidence supporting the theory that OIR-dark DSFGs are the progenitors of recently discovered 3<z<4 massive quiescent galaxies.
The emission in both bands is unresolved and the reported band 6 and band 7 flux densities for MORA-5 are 2.26 0.17 mJy and 6.49 0.22 mJy.

gmw_00000805.name: MORA-9
gmw_00000805.overview: MORA-9 is a high-redshift, optically and infrared-dark dusty star-forming galaxy [arXiv:2111.02428] with a photometric redshift estimated around z ~ 4.3 to 5.5 [arXiv:2111.02428, arXiv:2110.06930]. It exhibits a modest star-formation rate [arXiv:2111.02428] and is detected in the millimeter, submillimeter, and infrared bands [arXiv:2111.02428, arXiv:2110.06930].

sources.summary: The paper presents MORA-9 as an OIR-dark z~4.3 DSFG with modest star formation, ACA/mm detections, a Ks-band counterpart, and possible progenitor status for massive quiescent galaxies.

quotes.quote: Our results suggest the two galaxies, called MORA-5 and MORA-9, represent two extremes of the ``OIR-dark" class of DSFGs. MORA-5 ( z_ phot =4.3^ +1.5 _ 1.3 ) is a significantly more active starburst with a star-formation rate of 830 ^ +340 _ 190 M _ yr ^ 1 compared to MORA-9 ( z_ phot =4.3^ +1.3 _ 1.0 ) whose star-formation rate is a modest 200 ^ +250 _ 60 M _ yr ^ 1 . Based on the stellar masses (M _ 10^ 10-11 M _ ), space density ( n (5 2) 10^ 6 Mpc ^ 3 , which incorporates two other spectroscopically confirmed OIR-dark DSFGs in the MORA sample at z=4.6 and z=5.9 ), and gas depletion timescales ( <1 Gyr) of these sources, we find evidence supporting the theory that OIR-dark DSFGs are the progenitors of recently discovered 3<z<4 massive quiescent galaxies.

Comparatively, MORA-9 lacks coverage at other frequencies, so we secured follow-up Atacama Compact Array (ACA) observations under program 2019.2.00143 (PI Casey) to obtain band 6, band 7, and band 8 observations for improved dust continuum constraints. The band 6 observations resulted in a detection just under 5 with flux density of 1.24 0.25 mJy. The aggregate band 7 continuum has a flux density of 2.54 0.37 mJy. This is consistent with SCUBA-2 data (see Section subsec:ancillary ), but more precise both astrometrically and in the measurement of its flux density. The band 8 continuum flux density for MORA-9 is 3.62 0.66 mJy.

sources.summary: MORA-9 is detected at 2 mm and 850 micron, has faint Spitzer and K-band counterparts, and its 850-to-2 mm ratio supports a high-redshift photometric solution.

quotes.quote: MORA-9, has been detected at 850 \ in simpson19a at 4.4 significance; it had not been detected in the earlier compilation of geach17b . Aside from its detection at 2 mm and 850 , the source is also detected at 3.6 \ and 4.5 \ from Spitzer and at low significance in Ultra-VISTA K band imaging. This source is undetected in all other datasets, but its K band counterpart renders it present in the COSMOS2020 photometric catalogs with an OIR photometric constraint of z_ OIR =4.57^ +1.33 _ 1.22 . It is one of only two > 5 sources not already surveyed by ALMA at other wavelengths (the other being MORA-12). The ratio of 850 to-2 mm flux density is highly suggestive of a high- z solution; we derive a millimeter photometric redshift of z_ mm =5.5^ +0.6 _ 0.8 for this source.

gmw_00000806.name: dw1046+1244
gmw_00000806.overview: The galaxy dw1046+1244 is a newly discovered ultra-faint dwarf stellar overdensity [arXiv:2303.03025]. It is located at a distance of 10.2 Mpc in the Leo-I group, where it likely acts as a satellite of the ultra-diffuse galaxy M96-DF6 [arXiv:2303.03025].

sources.summary: The paper reports dw1046+1244 as a newly detected ultra-faint dwarf stellar overdensity near M96-DF6, with Local Group-like scaling properties and probable satellite-of-satellite status.

quotes.quote: Going through Hubble Space Telescope data of 117 dwarf galaxies, we report the discovery of a previously unknown dwarf galaxy around the ultra-diffuse M96 companion M96-DF6 at 10.2 Mpc in the Leo-I group. We confirm its dwarf galaxy nature as a stellar overdensity. Modelling its structural parameters with a growth curve analysis, we find that it is an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy with a luminosity of 1.5 10 ^5 L _ , which is 135 times fainter than its host. Based on its close projection to M96-DF6 it is unlikely that their association occurs just by chance.

As expected, both the dwarf galaxy M96-DF6 and its newly discovered satellite follow this relation. The dwarf galaxy dw1046+1244 is similar to AndXXX 2016ApJ...833..167M , also known as CassII, with M_V=-8.0 mag and r_h=260 pc. This dwarf has a velocity dispersion of 11.8 ^ +7.7 _ 4.7 and is dark matter dominated 2013ApJ...768..172C .

gmw_00000807.name: 12A
gmw_00000807.overview: 12A is a Seyfert 2 galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.99 [arXiv:astro-ph/0001360]. Its spectral energy distribution resembles NGC 6240, though it exhibits a stronger starburst component indicated by higher radio luminosity and lower intrinsic X-ray absorption [arXiv:astro-ph/0001360].

sources.summary: The paper describes 12A as a z=0.99 Seyfert 2 ROSAT Deep Survey source whose SED resembles NGC 6240, with relatively strong radio emission and lower intrinsic X-ray absorption.
